26-2.html.nextLabel: The Act of Suspension for a Bhikkhu who does not renounce a sinful view.

26-3.html.title: The Act of Suspension for Not Relinquishing an Evil View
26-3.html.body:
While the Blessed One was staying at Jeta’s Grove in Sāvatthī, a bhikkhu named Ariṭṭha conceived the following evil view: "If the touch of soft cushions, mattresses, and rugs is allowable, then the touch of a woman should also be allowable. It is not an obstructive thing." Hearing of this, the bhikkhus exhorted the bhikkhu Ariṭṭha in various ways to make him relinquish that view. However, Ariṭṭha did not comply. The bhikkhus reported this matter to the Tathāgata. The Blessed One also admonished him, and when he still did not comply, the Tathāgata commanded that the Act of Suspension be performed against that bhikkhu, excluding him from communion. When performing the Act of Suspension for not relinquishing an evil view, you must summon the bhikkhu into the midst of the Community, charge him, remind him of the offense, and make him acknowledge the fault before carrying out the formal act.

The Formal Act (Kamma-vācā)

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho. (ariṭṭhassa bhikkhuno gaddhābādhipubbassa) evarūpaṃ pāpakaṃ diṭṭhigataṃ uppannaṃ. “Tathā’haṃ bhagavatā dhammaṃ desitaṃ ājānāmi. Yathā ye me anantarāyikā dhammā vuttā bhagavatā te paṭisevato nālaṃ antarāyāyāti.” so taṃ diṭṭhiṃ nappaṭinissajjati. Yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ. Saṅgho (ariṭṭhassa bhikkhuno gaddhābādhipubbassa) pāpikāya diṭṭhiyā appaṭinissagge ukkhepanīya kammaṃ karey‍ya asambhogaṃ saṅghena. Esā ñatti.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, (ariṭṭhassa bhikkhuno gaddhābādhipubbassa) evarūpaṃ pāpakaṃ diṭṭhigataṃ uppannaṃ “Tathā’haṃ bhagavatā dhammaṃ desitaṃ ājānāmi yathā ye me antarāyikā dhammā vuttā bhagavatā te paṭisevato nālaṃ antarāyāyāti.” so taṃ diṭṭhiṃ nappaṭinissajjati. Saṅgho (ariṭṭhassa bhikkhuno gaddhābādhipubbassa) pāpikāya diṭṭhiyā appaṭinissagge ukkhepanīyakammaṃ karoti asambhogaṃ saṅghena. Yassāyasmato khamati (ariṭṭhassa bhikkhuno gaddhābādhipubbassa) pāpikāya diṭṭhiyā appaṭinissagge ukkhepanīyassa kammassa karaṇaṃ asambhogaṃ saṅghena, so tuṇhassa. Yassa nakkhamati. So bhāsey‍ya.

Dutiyampi etamat‍thaṃ vadāmi. Suṇātu me -pe-

Tatiyampi etamat‍thaṃ vadāmi. Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho (ariṭṭhassa bhikkhuno gaddhābādhipubbassa) evarūpaṃ pāpakaṃ diṭṭhigataṃ uppannaṃ “Tathā’haṃ bhagavatā dhammaṃ desitaṃ ājānāmi yathā ye me antarāyikā dhammā vuttā bhagavatā te paṭisevato nālaṃ antarāyāyāti. So taṃ diṭṭhiṃ nappaṭinissajjati. Saṅgho (ariṭṭhassa bhikkhuno gaddhābādhipubbassa) pāpikāya diṭṭhiyā appaṭinissagge ukkhepanīyakammaṃ karoti asambhogaṃ saṅghena. Yassāyasmato khamati (ariṭṭhassa bhikkhuno gaddhābādhipubbassa) pāpikāya diṭṭhiyā appaṭinissagge ukkhepanīyassa kammassa karaṇaṃ asambhogaṃ saṅghena, so tuṇhassa. Yassa nakkhamati so bhāsey‍ya.

Kataṃ saṅghena (ariṭṭhassa bhikkhuno gaddhābādhipubbassa) pāpikāya diṭṭhiyā appaṭinissage ukkhepanīyakammaṃ asambhogaṃ saṅghena. Khamati saṅghassa, tasmā tuṇhī. Evametaṃ dhārayāmi.

There are eighteen duties or rules that must be observed by a bhikkhu against whom the Act of Suspension has been performed for not relinquishing an evil view. They are as follows:

  1. He should not ordain others.
  2. He should not give dependence (nissaya).
  3. He should not take a novice.
  4. He should not accept the agreement to exhort bhikkhunīs.
  5. Even if he has already received the agreement, he should not exhort bhikkhunīs.
  6. He should not commit the same offense for which the Act of Suspension was performed.
  7. He should not commit any other similar offense.
  8. He should not commit any worse offense.
  9. He should not disparage the Act of Suspension.
  10. He should not disparage those who performed the act.
  11. He should not suspend the Uposatha of a regular bhikkhu.
  12. He should not suspend the Pavāraṇā of a regular bhikkhu.
  13. He should not issue commands.
  14. He should not take a position of leadership in the monastery.
  15. He should not ask for leave to charge another.
  16. He should not charge another.
  17. He should not remind another of an offense.
  18. He should not incite bhikkhus against one another.

The Request for Revocation of the Act of Suspension

Ahaṃ bhante! saṅghena pāpikāya diṭṭhiyā appaṭinissagge ukkhepanīyakammakato sammā vattāmi. Lomaṃ pātemi. Netthāraṃ vattāmi. Pāpikāya diṭṭhiyā appaṭinissagge ukkhepanīyassa kammassa paṭippassaddhiṃ yācāmi. Dutiyampi -pe- tatiyampi -pe-

The Formal Act for Revocation (Kamma-vācā)

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho. Ayaṃ (tisso) bhikkhu saṅghena pāpikāya diṭṭhiyā appaṭinissagge ukkhepanīyakammakato sammā vattati. Lomaṃ pāteti. Net‍thāraṃ vattati. Pāpikāya diṭṭhiyā appaṭinissagge ukkhepanīyassa kammassa paṭippassaddhiṃ yācati. Yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ. Saṅgho (tissassa) bhikkhuno pāpikāya diṭṭhiyā appaṭinissagge ukkhepanīyakammaṃ paṭippassambhey‍ya. Esā ñatti.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, ayaṃ (tisso) bhikkhu saṅghena pāpikāya diṭṭhiyā appaṭinissagge ukkhepanīyakammakato sammā vattati. Lomaṃ pāteti. Net‍thāraṃ vattati. Pāpikāya diṭṭhiyā appaṭinissagge ukkhepanīyassa kammassa paṭippassaddhiṃ yācati. Saṅgho (tissassa) bhikkhuno pāpikāya diṭṭhiyā appaṭinissagge ukkhepanīya kammaṃ paṭippassambheti. Yassāyasmato khamati (tissassa) bhikkhuno pāpikāya diṭṭhiyā appaṭinissagge ukkhepanīyassa kammassa paṭippassaddhi. So tuṇhassa. Yassa nakkhamati. So bhāsey‍ya.

Dutiyampi etamat‍thaṃ vadāmi. Suṇātu me -pe-

Tatiyampi etamat‍thaṃ vadāmi. Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, ayaṃ (tisso) bhikkhu saṅghena pāpikāya diṭṭhiyā appaṭinissagge ukkhepanīyakammakato sammā vattati. Lomaṃ pāteti. Net‍thāraṃ vattati. Pāpikāya diṭṭhiyā appaṭinissagge ukkhepanīyassa kammassa paṭippassaddhiṃ yācati. Saṅgho (tissassa) bhikkhuno pāpikāya diṭṭhiyā ukkhepanīyakammaṃ paṭippassambheti. Yassāyasmato khamati (tissassa) bhikkhuno pāpikāya diṭṭhiyā appaṭinissagge ukkhepanīyassa kammassa paṭippassaddhi, so tuṇhassa. Yassa nakkhamati. So bhāsey‍ya.

Paṭippassaddhaṃ saṅghena (tissassa) bhikkhuno pāpikāya diṭṭhiyā appaṭinissagge ukkhepanīyakammaṃ. Khamati saṅghassa. Tasmā tuṇhī. Evametaṃ dhārayāmi.
26-3.html.prevLabel: The Act of Suspension for a Bhikkhu who is Unwilling to Remedy an Offense

26.html.title: Acts of Suspension
26.html.body:
Sub-topics

  • The Act of Suspension for not acknowledging an offense.
  • The Act of Suspension for not making amends for an offense.
  • The Act of Suspension for not renouncing a pernicious view.
26.html.prevLabel: The Act of Overturning the Alms-bowl.
26.html.nextLabel: The Act of Suspension for not acknowledging an offense.

27-10.html.title: Authorization to be Separate from the Triple Robe
27-10.html.body:
After the expiration of the privileges of the Kathina, if one stays even for one night separate from the triple robe, it is an offense of Expiation involving Forfeiture (nissaggiya pācittiya). Therefore, if a bhikkhu has a triple robe that has been determined (adhiṭṭhāna), he must take them wherever he goes. A bhikkhu who is unable to take the triple robe with him due to some infirmity should obtain the ticīvarena avippavāsa sammuti (Authorization to be separate from the triple robe). For a bhikkhu who has received this, there is no offense in staying separate from the triple robe.

The sick bhikkhu should approach the Community, arrange his upper robe over one shoulder, pay homage at the feet of the senior bhikkhus, sit in the squatting position, and having saluted the Community, speak this sentence:

Ahaṃ bhante! gilāno, na sakkomi ticīvaraṃ ādāya pakkamituṃ. So’haṃ bhante saṅghaṃ ticīvarena avippavāsasammutiṃ yācāmi. Dutiyampi -pe- tatiyampi -pe-

The Procedural Motion

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, ayaṃ (tisso) bhikkhu gilāno, na sakkoti ticīvaraṃ ādāya pakkamituṃ. So saṅghaṃ ticīvarena avippavāsasammutiṃ yācati. Yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ, saṅgho (tissassa) bhikkhuno ticīvarena avippavāsasammutiṃ dadey‍ya. Esā ñatti.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, ayaṃ (tisso) bhikkhu gilāno, na sakkoti ticīvaraṃ ādāya pakkamituṃ. So saṅghaṃ ticīvarena avippavāsasammutiṃ yācati. Saṅgho (tissassa) bhikkhuno ticīvarena avippavāsasammutiṃ deti. Yassāyasmato khamati (tissassa) bhikkhuno ticīvarena avippavāsasammutiyā dānaṃ, so tuṇhassa. Yassa nakkhamati, so bhāsey‍ya.

Dinnā saṅghena (tissassa) bhikkhuno ticīvarena avippavāsasammuti. Khamati saṅghassa, tasmā tuṇhī, evametaṃ dhārayāmi.
27-10.html.prevLabel: The Procedure for Giving the Robe of a Deceased Samanera

27-11.html.title: Authorization for New Construction.
27-11.html.body:
When a faithful person builds or causes a dwelling or monastery to be built, the bhikkhus should give advice to the donor for their convenience. Since not everyone can provide such advice, the Community should select a suitable bhikkhu for that task and grant him the 'Authorization for New Construction'. The bhikkhu who has received the authorization should advise the donor and the workers so that the monastery construction is completed quickly and the work is carried out appropriately.

When the Great Lay-Devotee Visakha was building the Pubbarama Great Monastery, the Venerable Moggallana the Great organized the work. The construction of the Pubbarama Monastery, which otherwise could not have been completed for many years, was finished in nine months. (Nowadays, certain over-learned individuals who do not know the Vinaya mislead the public by preaching falsely that building monasteries and temples is not work belonging to bhikkhus.)

The Procedural Text for Granting the Authorization for New Construction.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho. Yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ, saṅgho (itthannāmassa) gahapatino vihāraṃ (itthannāmassa) bhikkhuno navakammaṃ dadey‍ya. Esā ñatti.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, (itthannāmassa) gahapatino vihāraṃ (itthannāmassa) bhikkhuno navakammaṃ deti. Yassāyasmato khamati (itthannāmassa) gahapatino vihāraṃ (itthannāmassa) bhikkhuno navakammassa dānaṃ. So tuṇhassa. Yassa nakkhamati, so bhāsey‍ya.

Dinno saṅghena (itthannāmassa) gahapatino vihāro (itthannāmassa) bhikkhuno navakammaṃ. Khamati saṅghassa. Tasmā tuṇhī. Evametaṃ dhārayāmi.
27-11.html.prevLabel: Authorization for Separation from the Three Robes.
27-11.html.nextLabel: Authorization for the Assigner of Lodgings.

27-12.html.title: Authorization of an Assigner of Lodgings.
27-12.html.body:
In monasteries belonging to the Community where many bhikkhus arrive and reside, the Community should appoint a bhikkhu to assign lodgings—that is, to designate specific lodgings for arriving bhikkhus. That bhikkhu should count the bhikkhus and the lodgings, and then appropriately assign the bhikkhus to those respective lodgings.

The Formal Act for the Authorization of an Assigner of Lodgings.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ, saṅgho (tissaṃ) bhikkhuṃ senāsanagāhāpakaṃ sammanney‍ya, esā ñatti.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, saṅgho (tissaṃ) bhikkhuṃ senāsanagāhāpakaṃ sammannati. Yassāyasmato. Khamati (tissassa) bhikkhuno senāsanagāhāpakassa sammuti. So tuṇhassa. Yassa nakkhamati, so bhāsey‍ya. Sammato saṅghena (tiso) bhikkhu senāsanagāhāpako. Khamati saṅghassa, tasmā tuṇhī. Evametaṃ dhārayāmi.
27-12.html.prevLabel: Authorization of a Supervisor of New Construction.
27-12.html.nextLabel: Authorization of an Appointer of Lodgings.

27-13.html.title: Authorization of an Assigner of Lodgings.
27-13.html.body:
In monasteries where many bhikkhus frequently arrive, the Community should appoint a bhikkhu to assign lodgings. The bhikkhu who has received this authorization must appropriately assign lodgings to the bhikkhus arriving at the monastery.

The formal proclamation for the authorization of an Assigner of Lodgings.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ, saṅgho (tissaṃ) bhikkhuṃ senāsanapaññāpakaṃ sammanney‍ya. Esā ñatti.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, saṅgho (tissaṃ) bhikkhuṃ senāsanapaññāpakaṃ sammannati. Yassāyasmato khamati (tissassa) bhikkhuno senāsanapaññāpakassa sammuti, so tuṇhassa. Yassa nakkhamati, so bhāsey‍ya.

Sammato saṅghena (tisso) bhikkhu senāsanapaññāpako, khamati saṅghassa, tasmā tuṇhī. Evametaṃ dhārayāmi.

The Arahant Dabba Mallaputta, son of the Malla kings, lived having received both the authorization of an Assigner of Lodgings and the authorization of a Distributor of Meals.
27-13.html.prevLabel: Authorization of a Receiver of Lodgings.
27-13.html.nextLabel: Authorization of a Distributor of Meals.

27-14.html.title: Authorization of a Meal-Assigner.
27-14.html.body:
Those wishing to offer alms come to monasteries where many bhikkhus reside and invite bhikkhus to their homes in groups of five, ten, and so forth. A bhikkhu should be authorized by the Community to accept invitations made to the Community and to designate and dispatch bhikkhus for them. The authorized bhikkhu, having accepted the invitations, should record the places to be visited for alms on slips of wood, bamboo, or leaves, mix them all together, and distribute the voting-slips (salāka) in order, starting from the standing roster (ṭhitikā) or, if there is no standing roster, starting from the senior Elder. The bhikkhus who have received the voting-slips should go to the places mentioned on their respective slips. This is the method for assigning meals.

The Procedural Text for the Authorization.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ, saṅgho (tissaṃ) bhikkhuṃ bhattuddesakaṃ sammanney‍ya. Esā ñatti.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, saṅgho (tissaṃ) bhikkhuṃ bhattuddesakaṃ sammannati. Yassāyasmato khamati (tissassa) bhikkhuno bhattuddesakassa sammuti so tuṇhassa. Yassa nakkhamati, so bhāsey‍ya.

Sammato saṅghena (tisso) bhikkhu bhattuddesako, khamati saṅghassa, tasmā tuṇhī. Evametaṃ dhārayāmi.
27-14.html.nextLabel: The Procedural Text for the Authorization of a Conjey-Distributor.

27-15.html.title: Formal Act for the Authorization of a Distributor of Rice-gruel.
27-15.html.body:
Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ, saṅgho (tissaṃ) bhikkhuṃ yāgubhājakaṃ sammanney‍ya. Esā ñatti.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, saṅgho (tissaṃ) bhikkhuṃ yāgubhājakaṃ sammannati. Yassāyasmato khamati (tissassa) bhikkhuno yāgubhājakassa sammuti. So tuṇhassa. Yassa nakkhamati, so bhāsey‍ya.

Sammato saṅghena (tisso) bhikkhu yāgubhājako, khamati saṅghassa, tasmā tuṇhī. Evametaṃ dhārayāmi.
27-15.html.prevLabel: Authorization of a Distributor of Meals.
27-15.html.nextLabel: Formal Act for the Authorization of a Distributor of Fruits.

27-16.html.title: Formal Act for the Authorization of a Fruit-Distributor
27-16.html.body:
Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ, saṅgho (tissaṃ) bhikkhuṃ phalabhājakaṃ sammanney‍ya. Esā ñatti.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, saṅgho (tissaṃ) bhikkhuṃ phalabhājakaṃ sammannati. Yassāyasmato khamati (tissassa) bhikkhuno phalabhājakassa sammuti, so tuṇhassa. Yassa nakkhamati, so bhāsey‍ya.

Sammato saṅghena (tisso) bhikkhu phalabhājako, khamati saṅghassa, tasmā tuṇhī. Evametaṃ dhārayāmi.
27-16.html.prevLabel: Formal Act for the Authorization of a Rice-gruel Distributor
27-16.html.nextLabel: Formal Act for the Authorization of a Confection-Distributor

27-17.html.title: Formal Act for the Appointment of a Distributor of Confectionery
27-17.html.body:
Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ, saṅgho (tissaṃ) bhikkhuṃ khajjabhājakaṃ sammanney‍ya. Esā ñatti.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, saṅgho (tissaṃ) bhikkhuṃ khajjabhājakaṃ sammannati. Yassāyasmato khamati (tissassa) bhikkhuno khajjabhājakassa sammuti, so tuṇhassa. Yassa nakkhamati, so bhāsey‍ya.

Sammato saṅghena (tisso) bhikkhu khajjabhājako, khamati saṅghassa, tasmā tuṇhī. Evametaṃ dhārayāmi.
27-17.html.prevLabel: Formal Act for the Appointment of a Distributor of Fruits
27-17.html.nextLabel: Appointment of a Disposer of Trifles

27-18.html.title: Authorization of a Distributor of Trifles.
27-18.html.body:
During times when the Community's stores do not receive enough requisites to be distributed among all the bhikkhus, the Tathagata authorized the 'Authorization of a Distributor of Trifles' to appoint a bhikkhu to distribute small items required by individual bhikkhus. A bhikkhu who has received this authorization has the authority to distribute small items from the stores without consulting the Community. Such a bhikkhu should give a needle to a bhikkhu requesting one for sewing robes. He should give a small knife to one who asks for a knife. He should give a pair of sandals to a bhikkhu departing on a long journey. He should give a waist-band to a bhikkhu requesting one. He should give a shoulder-strap to a bhikkhu who needs one. He should give a water-filter to a bhikkhu who needs one. He should give a bowl-bag to a bhikkhu who needs one. He should give cloth to bhikkhus requesting it for long panels, cross panels, circles, half-circles, or borders. He should give ghee, honey, and such items in an amount sufficient for a single serving.

The Formal Act.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ, saṅgho (tissaṃ) bhikkhuṃ appamattakavissajjakaṃ sammanney‍ya. Esā ñatti.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, saṅgho (tissaṃ) bhikkhuṃ appamattakavissajjakaṃ sammannati. Yassāyasmato khamati (tissassa) bhikkhuno appamattakavissajjakassa sammuti. So tuṇhassa. Yassa nakkhamati, so bhāsey‍ya.

Sammato saṅghena (tisso) bhikkhu appamattakavissajjako, khamati saṅghassa, tasmā tuṇhī. Evametaṃ dhārayāmi.
27-18.html.prevLabel: The Formal Act for the Authorization of a Distributor of Confections.
27-18.html.nextLabel: Authorization of a Receiver of Bowls.

27-19.html.title: Authorization of an Alms-bowl Distributor.
27-19.html.body:
There are two types of authorizations for an Alms-bowl Distributor. One is the authorization to distribute bowls that have been received by the Community; this is set forth in the Senāsanakkhandhaka of the Cūḷavaggapāḷi. The other is the authorization to cause the bhikkhus to accept a bowl forfeited to the Community by a bhikkhu who has incurred an offense by obtaining a new bowl when his current bowl has fewer than five mends, according to the training rule: “Yo pana bhikkhu ūnapañcabandhanena pattena aññaṃ navaṃ pattaṃ cetāpeyya nissaggiyaṃ pācittiyaṃ”. This is declared in the Pārājikāpāḷi. There is no difference in the formal act sentences (kammavācā) between the two.

The formal act is as follows:

Suṇātu me bhante! Saṅgho, yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ, saṅgho (Tisso) bhikkhuṃ pattagāhāpakaṃ sammanneyya. Esā ñatti.

Suṇātu me bhante! Saṅgho, saṅgho (Tissaṃ) bhikkhuṃ pattagāhāpakaṃ sammannati. Yassāyasmato khamati (Tissassa) bhikkhuno pattagāhāpakassa sammuti. So tuṇhassa. Yassa nakkhamati, so bhāseyya.

Sammato saṅghena (Tisso) bhikkhu pattagāhāpako, khamati saṅghassa. Tasmā tuṇhī. Evametaṃ dhārayāmi.

The bhikkhu who obtained a new bowl while possessing one with fewer than five mends should approach the Community, arrange his upper robe over one shoulder, pay homage at the feet of the senior bhikkhus, sit in the squatting position, and with joined palms [say]:

“Ayaṃ me bhante! patto ūna pañcabandhanena pattena cetāpito nissaggiyo. Ima’haṃ saṅghassa nissajjāmi”

Having thus forfeited the bowl to the Community, he should confess the offense. The Community must assemble for this procedure with the bowl present. To cause the bhikkhus to accept the forfeited bowl, a bhikkhu must be authorized using the aforementioned formal act. The authorized bhikkhu should take that bowl, describe its qualities, and offer it to the Elder of the Community. If that bowl is better than his own, the Elder of the Community should accept it and give his own bowl to the authorized bhikkhu. If he does not accept it out of compassion for the original owner, the Elder incurs an offense of wrong-doing (dukkaṭa). There is no offense if he does not accept it because the bowl is of poor quality. If the Elder of the Community accepts that bowl, his bowl should then be offered to the second senior monk. In this manner, having offered the bowls to all the bhikkhus, the final remaining bowl, which will be the most inferior, should be given to the bhikkhu who [wrongfully] requested the bowl.
27-19.html.prevLabel: Authorization of a Distributor of Trifles.

27-2.html.title: Authorization of a Learner (Sekha).
27-2.html.body:
In Savatthi, a husband and wife in a certain house were very devoted to the bhikkhus. Gradually, they fell into poverty, yet they did not reduce their giving. On certain days, they would give everything prepared in their house for the forenoon meal to the bhikkhus and remain hungry themselves. People who knew this criticized the bhikkhus, saying, "These bhikkhus accept things from others without consideration." The bhikkhus heard this and informed the Blessed One. Then the Blessed One authorized the granting of the 'Authorization of a Learner' (Sekha-sammuti) to families who are increasing in faith but decreasing in wealth.

The Procedural Text for the Authorization of a Learner.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho. (it‍thannāmaṃ) kulaṃ saddhāya vaḍḍhati. Bhogena hāyati. Yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ, saṅgho (itthannāmassa) kulassa sekhasammutiṃ dadey‍ya. Esā ñatti.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, (it‍thannāmaṃ) kulaṃ saddhāya vaḍḍhati. Bhogena hāyati. Saṅgho (itthannāmassa) kulassa sekhasammutiṃ deti. Yassāyasmato khamati (itthannāmassa) kulassa sekhasammutiyā dānaṃ, so tuṇhassa. Yassa nakkhamati. So bhāsey‍ya.

Dinnā saṅghena (itthannāmassa) kulassa sekhasammuti. Khamati saṅghassa, tasmā tuṇhī. Evametaṃ dhārayāmi.

If a bhikkhu who is not ill, without an invitation, goes to a family that has received the Authorization of a Learner and, having received food with his own hand, eats it, it is an offense to be confessed (Pāṭidesanīya). There is no offense in receiving and eating something that they bring outside the house.
27-2.html.nextLabel: Authorization of a Receiver of Robes.

27-20.html.title: Authorization of a Disposer of Money
27-20.html.body:
Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ, saṅgho (tissaṃ) bhikkhuṃ rūpiyachaḍḍhakaṃ sammanney‍ya. Esā ñatti.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, saṅgho (tissaṃ) bhikkhuṃ rūpiyachaḍḍhakaṃ sammannati. Yassāyasmato khamati (tissassa) bhikkhuno rūpiyachaḍḍhakassa sammuti, so tuṇhassa. Yassa nakkhamati, so bhāsey‍ya.

Sammato saṅghena (tisso) bhikkhu rūpīyachaḍḍhako. Khamati saṅghassa. Tasmā tuṇahī. Evametaṃ dhārayāmi.
27-20.html.prevLabel: Authorization of a Receiver of Bowls
27-20.html.nextLabel: Authorization of a Receiver of Cloths

27-21.html.title: Authorization of a Receiver of Bathing Cloths
27-21.html.body:
Since the bathing cloths received by the Community must be distributed appropriately, the Blessed One authorized the Authorization of a Receiver of Bathing Cloths.

The procedural text is as follows:

Suṇātu me bhante! Saṅgho, yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ, saṅgho (Tissaṃ) bhikkhuṃ sāṭiyagāhāpakaṃ sammanneyya. Esā ñatti.

Suṇātu me bhante! Saṅgho, saṅgho (Tissaṃ) bhikkhuṃ sāṭiyagāhāpakaṃ sammannati. Yassāyasmato khamati (Tissassa) bhikkhuno sāṭiyagāhāpakassa sammuti, so tuṇhassa. Yassa nakkhamati, so bhāseyya.

Sammato saṅghena (Tisso) bhikkhu sāṭiyagāhāpako. Khamati saṅghassa, tasmā tuṇhī. Evametaṃ dhārayāmi.
27-21.html.prevLabel: Authorization of a Discarder of Money
27-21.html.nextLabel: Authorization of a Director of Monastery Attendants

27-22.html.title: Authorization of an Overseer of Monastery Attendants.
27-22.html.body:
Monastery attendants are laypeople who reside in the monastery for the purpose of assisting the bhikkhus. When there is no one to assign them to various tasks, they do not perform the work. Even if they do, they do not do it properly. Therefore, the Tathāgata authorized the appointment of an Overseer of Monastery Attendants. An "Overseer of Monastery Attendants" is one who assigns the monastery attendants to their respective duties. The bhikkhu who has received the Authorization of an Overseer of Monastery Attendants must direct the monastery attendants in their various tasks.

Procedural Text.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ, saṅgho (tissaṃ) bhikkhuṃ ārāmikapesakaṃ sammanney‍ya. Esā ñatti.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, saṅgho (tissaṃ) bhikkhuṃ ārāmikapesakaṃ sammannati. Yassāyasmato khamati (tissassa) bhikkhuno ārāmikapesakassa sammuti. So tuṇhassa. Yassa nakkhamati so bhāsey‍ya.

Sammato saṅghena (tisso) bhikkhu ārāmikapesako. Khamati saṅghassa, tasmā tuṇhī. Evametaṃ dhārayāmi.
27-22.html.prevLabel: Authorization of a Receiver of Bathing Cloths.
27-22.html.nextLabel: Authorization of an Overseer of Novices.

27-23.html.title: Authorization of a Novice-Assigner
27-23.html.body:
Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ, saṅgho (tissaṃ) bhikkhuṃ sāmaṇerapesakaṃ sammanney‍ya, esā ñatti.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, saṅgho (tissaṃ) bhikkhuṃ sāmaṇerapesakaṃ sammannati. Yassāyasmato khamati (tissassa) bhikkhuno sāmaṇerapesakassa sammuti, so tuṇhassa. Yassa nakkhamati, so bhāsey‍ya.

Sammato saṅghena (tisso) bhikkhu sāmaṇerapesako. Khamati saṅghassa, tasmā tuṇhī. Evametaṃ dhārayāmi.

27-24.html.title: Authorization for a Staff
27-24.html.body:
A bhikkhu who is unable to walk without a staff must obtain the Authorization for a Staff in order to use one. The bhikkhu desiring it should approach the Community, arrange his upper robe over one shoulder, pay homage to the feet of the senior bhikkhus, sit in the squatting position, and with hands joined in reverence, say:

Ahaṃ bhante! gilāno, na sakkomi vinā daṇḍena āhiṇḍituṃ. So’haṃ bhante saṅghaṃ daṇḍasammutiṃ yācāmi. Dutiyampi -pe- tatiyampi -pe-

Having spoken these words, he should request the Authorization for a Staff from the Community.

The Procedural Text for Granting the Authorization for a Staff.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, ayaṃ (tisso) bhikkhu gilāno na sakkoti vinā daṇḍena āhiṇḍituṃ, so saṅghaṃ daṇḍasammutiṃ yācati. Yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ, saṅgho (tissassa) bhikkhuno daṇḍasammutiṃ dadey‍ya. Esā ñatti.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, ayaṃ (tisso) bhikkhu gilāno, na sakkoti vinā daṇḍena āhiṇḍituṃ, so saṅghaṃ daṇḍasammutiṃ yācati. Saṅgho (tissassa) bhikkhuno daṇḍasammutiṃ deti. Yassāyasmato khamati (tissassa) bhikkhuno daṇḍasammutiyā dānaṃ. So tuṇhassa. Yassa nakkhamati, so bhāsey‍ya.

Dinnā saṅghena (tissassa) bhikkhuno daṇḍa sammuti. Khamati saṅghassa, tasmā tuṇhī. Evametaṃ dhārayāmi.

27-25.html.title: Authorization for a Sling.
27-25.html.body:
The Authorization for a Sling is granted to weak, sick bhikkhus who find it difficult to carry their alms-bowl without hanging it in a sling. A bhikkhu who desires to receive this should approach the Community of monks, arrange his outer robe over one shoulder, bow at the feet of the senior bhikkhus, sit in a squatting position, and request the Authorization for a Sling from the Community with the following statement:

Ahaṃ bhante gilāno. Na sakkomi vinā sikkāya pattaṃ pariharituṃ, so’haṃ bhante saṅghaṃ sikkā sammutiṃ yācāmi. Dutiyampi -pe- tatiyampi -pe-

Monastic Procedure Text.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, ayaṃ (tisso) bhikkhu gilāno, na sakkoti vinā sikkāya pattaṃ pariharituṃ, so saṅghaṃ sikkāsammutiṃ yācati. Yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ, saṅgho (tissassa) bhikkhuno sikkāsammutiṃ dadey‍ya. Esā ñatti.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, ayaṃ (tisso) bhikkhu gilāno, na sakkoti vinā sikkāya pattaṃ pariharituṃ, so saṅghaṃ sikkāsammutiṃ yācati. Saṅgho (tissassa) bhikkhuno sikkāsammutiṃ deti. Yassāyasmato khamati (tissassa) bhikkhuno sikkāsammutiyā dānaṃ. So tuṇhassa. Yassa nakkhamati, so bhāsey‍ya.

Dinnā saṅghena (tissassa) bhikkhuno sikkāsammuti. Khamati saṅghassa. Tasmā tuṇhī. Evametaṃ dhārayāmi.
27-25.html.nextLabel: Authorization for a Staff and Sling

27-26.html.title: Authorization for a Staff and String
27-26.html.body:
The Formula for Requesting.

Ahaṃ bhante gilāno. Na sakkomi vinā daṇḍena āhiṇḍituṃ. Na sakkomi vinā sikkāya pattaṃ pariharituṃ. So’haṃ bhante saṅghaṃ daṇḍasikkāsammutiṃ yācāmi. Dutiyampi -pe- tatiyampi -pe-

The Procedural Text.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, ayaṃ (tisso) bhikkhu gilāno, na sakkoti vinā daṇḍena āhiṇḍituṃ, na sakkoti vinā sikkāya pattaṃ pariharituṃ, so saṅghaṃ daṇḍasikkā sammutiṃ yācati. Yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ, saṅgho (tissassa) bhikkhuno daṇḍasikkāsammutiṃ dadey‍ya. Esā ñatti.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, ayaṃ (tisso) bhikkhu gilāno, na sakkoti vinā daṇḍena āhiṇḍituṃ, na sakkoti vinā sikkāya pattaṃ pariharituṃ. So saṅghaṃ daṇḍasikkāsammutiṃ yācati. Saṅgho (tissassa) bhikkhuno daṇḍasikkāsammutiṃ deti. Yassāyasmato khamati (tissassa) bhikkhuno daṇḍasikkāsammutiyā dānaṃ, so tuṇhassa. Yassa nakkhamati, so bhāsey‍ya.

Dinnā saṅghena (tissassa) bhikkhuno daṇḍasikkāsammuti. Khamati saṅghassa, tasmā tuṇhī. Evametaṃ dhārayāmi.

27-27.html.title: Authorization for the Insane.
27-27.html.body:
For a Monastic Procedure to be performed, all bhikkhus residing within the boundary must be present. The consent of bhikkhus who cannot attend must be taken. In the time of the Buddha, a bhikkhu named Gagga, who was insane, would attend the Monastic Procedure if he wished, and would not attend if he did not wish. This became a trouble for the Community. In order to eliminate the trouble caused to the Community by insane bhikkhus, the Tathagata authorized the “Authorization for the Insane” for insane bhikkhus.

The Formal Statement.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, (gaggo) bhikkhu ummattako, sarati’pi uposathaṃ na’pi sarati, sarati’pi saṅghakammaṃ na’pi sarati āgacchati’pi uposathaṃ na’pi āgacchati, āgacchati’pi saṅghakammaṃ na’pi āgacchati. Yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ, saṅgho (gaggassa) bhikkhuno ummattakassa ummattakasammutiṃ dadey‍ya. Sarey‍ya vā (gaggo) bhikkhu upeāsathaṃ na vā sarey‍ya. Sarey‍ya vā saṅghakammaṃ na vā sarey‍ya, āgacchey‍ya vā uposathaṃ na vā āgacchey‍ya, āgacchey‍ya vā saṅghakammaṃ na vā āgacchey‍ya, saṅgho saha vā (gaggena) vinā vā (gaggena) uposathaṃ karey‍ya. Saṅghakammaṃ karey‍ya. Esā ñatti.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, (gaggo) bhikkhu ummattako sarati’pi uposathaṃ na’pisarati sarati’pi saṅghakammaṃ na’pi sarati, āgacchati’pi uposathaṃ na’pi. Āgacchati. Āgacchati’pi saṅghakammaṃ na’pi āgacchati, saṅgho (gaggassa) bhikkhuno ummattakassa ummattaka sammutiṃ deti. Sarey‍ya vā (gaggo) bhikkhu uposathaṃ na vā sarey‍ya, sarey‍ya vā saṅghakammaṃ na vā āgacchey‍ya, saṅgho saha vā (gaggena) vinā vā (gaggena) uposathaṃ karissati, saṅghakammaṃ karissati, yassāyasmato khamati (gaggassa) bhikkhuno ummattakassa ummattakasammutiyā dānaṃ. Sarey‍ya vā (gaggo) bhikkhu uposathaṃ na vā sarey‍ya, sarey‍ya vā saṅghakammaṃ na vā sarey‍ya, āgacchey‍ya vā uposathaṃ na vā āgacchey‍ya, āgacchey‍ya vā saṅghakammaṃ na vā āgacchey‍ya, saṅgho saha vā (gaggena) vinā vā (gaggena) uposathaṃ karissati, saṅghakammaṃ karissati, so tuṇhassa. Yassa nakkhamati, so bhāsey‍ya.

Dinnā saṅghena (gaggassa) bhikkhuno ummattakassa ummattaka sammuti, sarey‍ya vā (gaggo) bhikkhu uposathaṃ na vā sarey‍ya, sarey‍ya vā saṅghakammaṃ na vā sarey‍ya, āgacchey‍ya vā uposathaṃ na vā āgacchey‍ya, āgacchey‍ya vā saṅghakammaṃ na vā āgacchey‍ya, saṅgho saha vā (gaggena) vinā vā (gaggena) uposathaṃ karissati. Saṅghakammaṃ karissati, khamati saṅghassa, tasmā tuṇhī. Evametaṃ dhārayāmi.

After the Authorization for the Insane has been granted to an insane bhikkhu in this manner, whether that bhikkhu is present in the midst of the Community or outside the boundary of the Community, the Monastic Procedure performed thereby is not harmed.

27-3.html.title: Authorization for a Robe-Receiver.
27-3.html.body:
In the time of the Buddha, people would bring robes to the monastery to offer to the Community. Unable to find anyone to receive them, the people would take the robes they had brought back with them. As a result, the Community received fewer robes. The bhikkhus reported this to the Tathāgata. Thereupon, the Tathāgata permitted the authorization of a bhikkhu as a robe-receiver, one who would not go astray, who could give a good word of appreciation, and who was capable of inspiring people.

Formal Statement for the Authorization of a Robe-Receiver.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ, saṅgho (tissaṃ) bhikkhuṃ cīvarapaṭiggāhakaṃ sammanney‍ya. Esā ñatti.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, saṅgho (tissaṃ) bhikkhuṃ cīvarapaṭiggāhakaṃ sammannati. Yassāyasmato khamati (tissassa) bhikkhuno cīvarapaṭiggāhakassa sammuti. So tuṇhassa. Yassa nakkhamati, so bhāsey‍ya.

Sammato saṅghena (tisso) bhikkhu cīvarapaṭiggāhako. Khamati saṅghassa. Tasmā tuṇhī. Evametaṃ dhārayāmi.

27-4.html.title: Authorization of a Robe-Depositor.
27-4.html.body:
At that time, the bhikkhus who had received the authorization to receive robes, having received the robes, would leave them lying about here and there. Consequently, the robes belonging to the Community were being lost. When the bhikkhus informed the Blessed One of this matter, he allowed the Community to authorize a bhikkhu endowed with five qualities as a robe-depositor.

The Formal Motion.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅ‍gho, yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ, saṅgho (tissaṃ) bhikkhuṃ cīvaranidahakaṃ sammanney‍ya. Esā ñatti.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, saṅgho (tissaṃ) bhikkhuṃ cīvaranidahakaṃ sammannati. Yassāyasmato khamati (tissassa) bhikkhuno cīvaranidahakassa sammuti. So tuṇhassa. Yassa nakkhamati, so bhāsey‍ya,

Sammato saṅghena (tisso) bhikkhu cīvaranidahako, khamati saṅghassa, tasmā tuṇhī. Evametaṃ dhārayāmi.

27-5.html.title: Authorization of a Storehouse.
27-5.html.body:
At that time, the bhikkhus who were looking after the robes had no designated place to keep them, and so they kept the robes in various places. Mice ate those robes. Termites also ate them. When this matter was reported to the Blessed One, he allowed the authorization of a storehouse.

The Procedural Text.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ, saṅgho (it‍thannāmaṃ) vihāraṃ bhaṇḍāgāraṃ sammanney‍ya. Esā ñatti.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, saṅgho (it‍thannāmaṃ) vihāraṃ bhaṇḍāgāraṃ sammannati. Yassāyasmato khamati. (itthannāmassa) vihārassa bhaṇḍāgārassa sammuti, so tuṇhassa. Yassa nakkhamati, so bhāsey‍ya.

Sammato saṅghena (it‍thannāmo) vihāro bhaṇḍāgāraṃ, khamati saṅghassa, tasmā tuṇhī. Evametaṃ dhārayāmi.

27-6.html.title: Authorization of a Storekeeper.
27-6.html.body:
At that time, as there was no one to take care of them, the robes in the Community's storehouse were not being looked after. When this matter was reported to the Blessed One, he allowed the Community to authorize a bhikkhu endowed with five qualities as a storekeeper.

The Procedural Text.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ, saṅgho (tissaṃ) bhikkhuṃ bhāṇḍāgārikaṃ sammanney‍ya. Esā ñatti.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, saṅgho (tissaṃ) bhikkhuṃ bhaṇḍāgārikaṃ sammannati. Yassāyasmato khamati (tissassa) bhikkhuno bhaṇḍāgārikassa sammuti, so tuṇhassa. Yassa nakkhamati, so bhāsey‍ya.

Sammato saṅghena (tisso) bhikkhu bhaṇḍāgāriko; khamati saṅghassa. Tasmā tuṇhī. Evametaṃ dhārayāmi.

27-7.html.title: Authorization of a Robe-Distributor.
27-7.html.body:
Robes received by the Community are stored in the storehouse to be distributed when a sufficient quantity has accumulated. When the robes in the storehouse became numerous, the bhikkhus informed the Tathagata. At that time, the Tathagata allowed distribution by the Community assembled within the precinct, saying: “Anujānāmi bhikkhave! sammukhībhūtena saṅghena bhājetuṃ”. During the distribution, there was much noise, with some saying, "Give to our preceptors," or "Give to our teachers," and so forth. When this was reported to the Blessed One, He allowed the authorization of a bhikkhu endowed with five qualities to distribute the robes. The five qualities of a Robe-Distributor are: that he does not follow a wrong course through favoritism, through hatred, through delusion, or through fear; and that he knows what has been distributed and what has not.

The Procedural Text for Authorization.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ, saṅgho (tissaṃ) bhikkhuṃ cīvarabhājakaṃ sammanney‍ya. Esā ñatti.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, saṅgho (tissaṃ) bhikkhuṃ cīvarabhājakaṃ sammannati. Yassāyasmato khamati (tissassa) bhikkhuno cīvarabhājakassa sammuti. So tuṇhassa. Yassa nikkhamati, so bhāsey‍ya.

Sammato saṅghena (tisso) bhikkhu cīvarabhājako. Khamati saṅghassa, tasmā tuṇhī. Evametaṃ dhārayāmi.

The Robe-Distributor bhikkhus wondered, "How should the robes be distributed?" When this was reported to the Tathagata, He said: “Anujānāmi bhikkhave! paṭhamaṃ uccinit‍vā tulayit‍vā vaṇṇāvaṇṇaṃ kat‍vā bhikkhu gaṇetvā vaggaṃ bandhitvā cīvarapaṭiviṃsaṃ ṭhapetuṃ” — "Bhikkhus, I allow you first to sort the robes, weigh their value, equalize the value by adding items to those of lesser value, count the bhikkhus, form them into groups, and set aside the robe-shares." Among the robes offered individually by many people, there are those of good and bad quality, and higher and lower value. Therefore, one should examine the robes and equalize the shares by adding other robes to those of lesser value. Bhikkhus should be grouped for distribution if their number is so large that individual distribution becomes difficult. If there are many bhikkhus, they should be grouped into tens, or fours or fives; robe-shares should be set aside according to the number of groups, and distributed by drawing lots. The bhikkhus within each group should then further divide the shares among themselves by drawing lots. This is the method for distributing robes.
27-7.html.nextLabel: The Procedure for Offering Robes for the Deceased.

27-8.html.title: The Monastic Procedure for Bestowing Robes to the Deceased.
27-8.html.body:
If a bhikkhu passes away, all that belongs to that bhikkhu becomes the property of the Community. The notion that “the belongings of deceased bhikkhus belong to their disciples” is a Vinaya-contrary rule established in modern times according to the system of property ownership among laypeople.

“Bhikkhussa bhikkhave! kālakate saṅgho sāmi pattacīvare. Api ca gilānupaṭṭhākā bahukārā. Anujānāmi bhikkhave! saṅghena ticīvaraṃ ca pattaṃ ca gilānupaṭṭhākānaṃ dātuṃ. Yaṃ tattha lahubhaṇḍaṃ lahuparikkhāraṃ taṃ sammukhībhūtena saṅghena bhājetuṃ, yaṃ tattha garubhaṇḍaṃ garuparikkhāraṃ taṃ āgatānāgatassa cātuddisassa saṅghassa avissajjikaṃ avebhaṅgikaṃ” This is the Vinaya rule concerning the requisites of deceased bhikkhus, as stated in the Cīvarakkhandhaka of the Mahāvagga Pāḷi.

The attendant bhikkhu should take the bowl and robes of the bhikkhu who has passed away to the Community, and inform the Community: “Itthannāmo Bhante! Bhikkhu kālakato, idaṃ tassa ticīvaraṃ ca patto ca”. Although these belong to the Community, they should be given by the Community to the bhikkhu who attended the sick.

The Monastic Procedure Text.

Suṇātu me Bhante! Saṅgho. (Itthannāmo) bhikkhu kālakato. Idaṃ tassa ticīvaraṃ ca patto ca. Yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ, saṅgho imaṃ ticīvaraṃ ca pattaṃ ca gilānupaṭṭhākānaṃ dadeyya. Esā ñatti.

Suṇātu me Bhante! Saṅgho. (Itthannāmo) bhikkhu kālakato, idaṃ tassa ticīvaraṃ ca patto ca, saṅgho imaṃ ticīvaraṃ ca pattaṃ ca gilānupaṭṭhākānaṃ deti. Yassāyasmato khamati imassa ticīvarassa ca pattassa ca gilānupaṭṭhākānaṃ dānaṃ, so tuṇhassu. Yassa nakkhamati, so bhāseyya.

Dinnaṃ saṅghena ticīvaraṃ ca patto ca gilānupaṭṭhākānaṃ. Khamati saṅghassa, tasmā tuṇhī. Evametaṃ dhārayāmi.
27-8.html.nextLabel: The Monastic Procedure for Bestowing Robes to a Deceased Sāmaṇera

27-9.html.title: Monastic Procedure for Giving Robes to a Deceased Novice
27-9.html.body:
The Formal Announcement.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho. (it‍thannāmo) sāmaṇero kālakato, idaṃ tassa cīvarañca patto ca. Yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ, saṅgho imaṃ cīvarañca pattañca gilānupaṭṭhākānaṃ dadey‍ya. Esā ñatti.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho. (it‍thannāmo) sāmaṇero kālakato. Idaṃ tassa cīvarañca patto ca, saṅgho imaṃ cīvarañca pattañca gilānupaṭṭhākānaṃ deti. Yassāyasmato khamati imassa cīvarassa ca pattassa ca gilānupaṭṭhākānaṃ dānaṃ, so tuṇhassa. Yassa nakkhamati, so bhāsey‍ya.

Dinnaṃ idaṃ saṅghena cīvarañca patto ca gilānupaṭṭhākānaṃ. Khamati saṅghassa, tasmā tuṇhī. Evametaṃ dhārayāmi.
27-9.html.prevLabel: Monastic Procedure for Giving Robes to the Deceased.
27-9.html.nextLabel: Authorization for Dwelling Apart from the Three Robes.

27.html.title: Authorizations
27.html.prevLabel: The Act of Suspension for Not Relinquishing an Evil View.

28-1.html.title: Establishing a New Boundary
28-1.html.body:
Establishing a boundary is a procedure that can be performed in a few minutes. The difficulty lies in the purification of the boundary site. When the Community has authorized a boundary endowed with the threefold perfection, even if all its boundary markers are lost and there are no bhikkhus who know the boundary, it remains as a valid boundary for performing all Monastic Procedures until the disappearance of the Dispensation, provided it has not been revoked by the Community through a formal act. Even if the soil is removed and the boundary site is turned into a reservoir, that ground remains the boundary. For a boundary once authorized to become a non-boundary again, it must be revoked by the Community through a formal act. After revocation, even if markers such as stone pillars remain, that ground is a non-boundary.

Over two thousand years have passed since the Buddha's Dispensation was established on this island. During that long period, many monasteries have arisen in various places and fallen into ruin without leaving even a trace. There is no doubt that boundaries were established there for performing Monastic Procedures. Their markers are not visible today. Even without markers, those grounds remain valid boundaries for performing Monastic Procedures today. It cannot be said that such unknown ancient boundaries do not exist in any given place in this country. When a piece of land is selected for establishing a boundary, it may sometimes be within a larger ancient boundary. Sometimes a small ancient boundary may be entirely encompassed within it. Sometimes a portion of an ancient boundary may be encompassed. Sometimes the land may overlap with an ancient boundary.

If the ground chosen for the authorization of a boundary is entirely within an ancient boundary, it is not a new boundary but the ancient boundary itself, as one boundary cannot be authorized within another. If another bhikkhu is present within the area belonging to the ancient boundary at the time a procedure is performed within that authorized boundary, the procedure does not achieve validity due to being an incomplete group act. If no other bhikkhu is present within the ancient boundary site at the time a Monastic Procedure is performed, that procedure achieves validity.

As it is declared in the Vinaya: “Na bhikkhave! sīmāva sīmā ajjhottharitabbā. Yo ajjhottharey‍ya āpatti dukkaṭassa” (Bhikkhus, one boundary should not overlap another boundary. Whoever should overlap one, there is an offense of wrong-doing), a new boundary should not be authorized by encompassing an entire ancient boundary or a part of an ancient boundary. If done so, according to the Buddha's command: “Yesaṃ bhikkhave! sīmā pacchā sammatā, tesaṃ taṃ kammaṃ adhammikaṃ kuppaṃ aṭṭhānārahaṃ” (Bhikkhus, for those whose boundary is authorized later, that act of theirs is un-Dhamma, unstable, and fit to be set aside), that boundary does not become a valid boundary.

As it is declared: “Na bhikkhave! sīmāya sīmā sambhinditabbo, yo sambhindey‍ya āpatti dukkaṭassa” (Bhikkhus, a boundary should not be made to merge with another boundary. Whoever should merge them, there is an offense of wrong-doing), a new boundary should not be authorized by mixing it with or making it touch an ancient boundary site. If so authorized, it is also a non-boundary.

Since newly established boundaries may become invalid due to overlapping with ancient boundary sites, Vinaya experts first purify the ground when establishing a new boundary. The purification of the ground must be done by revoking the boundary through a formal act. Revoking a boundary that has existing markers and known limits is not a difficult task. When a group of no fewer than four bhikkhus enters that boundary site and recites the formal act for revocation, the revocation of the boundary occurs. In establishing a new boundary these days, what must be done is the revocation of unknown boundaries that have no markers. This is not an easy task. Therefore, it takes much time to certainly purify a piece of ground for establishing a new boundary.

There are boundaries as large as three leagues in length and breadth. There are also small boundaries. Revoking a great boundary that encompasses many villages and has unknown limits is extremely difficult. This is because while bhikkhus are gathered in one place to revoke the boundary, other bhikkhus may be present elsewhere within that great boundary. If a bhikkhu is present anywhere within that boundary without having come within arm's reach during the revocation, that act of revocation becomes an incomplete group act and the revocation of the boundary does not occur. Therefore, if ancient great boundaries are encompassed by the places where boundaries are currently being established, all those boundaries become non-boundaries. The only consoling factor in this is that great boundaries are not common. Most boundaries found in the country are less than a hundred cubits in length and breadth.

Apart from the Great Boundary of Anuradhapura, it has not been heard that a great boundary was established anywhere else in this country. Bhikkhus in the past mostly established small boundaries, just as they do now. Therefore, there should be small boundary sites in various places in the country. When bhikkhus within a range of twenty or thirty fathoms gather and perform the revocation, the revocation of those boundaries can occur. In this land, there are more places that are not encompassed by boundaries than those that are. Therefore, it is only rarely that a ground for establishing a new boundary overlaps with an ancient boundary site. Thus, if you perform the act of revocation without leaving a large area vacant of bhikkhus and purify the ground, there is no reason to doubt the boundaries being established now.
28-1.html.prevLabel: The Monastic Procedure for Establishing a Boundary

28-2.html.title: Purification of the Boundary Area.
28-2.html.body:
When a new boundary is to be delimited, purify the ground as follows: Level and clean the area of ground to be taken for the boundary delimitation and a space of about two fathoms outside it on all four sides, and divide it into bed-sized plots. While doing this, perform it cleanly by driving stakes and pulling ropes to draw lines, or by drawing lines with lime or similar substances, or by driving stakes at all four corners of every plot. Thereafter, a group of four, five, or six bhikkhus should perform the act of boundary removal in order, standing in all the plots. While doing this, it is better if all bhikkhus who are not participating in the act are removed from the village, thus performing the purification of the village as well. If the village cannot be purified, remove other bhikkhus to a distance of about twenty or twenty-five fathoms nearby and perform the act of removal.

Since it is difficult to understand the suitability or unsuitability of the bhikkhus participating in the act, it is good for the sake of purification and to remove doubts regarding the boundary to have different groups of bhikkhus perform the act of removal two or three times. When performing this act, some bhikkhus who do not know the Monastic Discipline may wish to come near to watch the act being performed. If bhikkhus come near, they must be prevented. The 'bed-size' is a measure of five cubits in length and two and a half cubits in width. When the Community stands in all such divided chambers in any ground and performs the act of removal, if any part of an ancient boundary was caught in that ground, that boundary is removed and the ground becomes purified.

This removal is performed to purify a new boundary ground where it is not known where an existing boundary might be. A boundary removal occurs only if the group of bhikkhus performing the act stands within the boundary being removed while performing the act. If even one of the four bhikkhus performing the act of removal stands outside the boundary ground, the removal does not take place. Divide the area into chambers and perform the boundary removal while standing everywhere so that wherever a boundary might have been, it will be removed.

Sometimes a small portion of an ancient boundary, not large enough for four bhikkhus to stand in, might be caught in the ground where the new boundary is being delimited. By performing the act of removal only in the ground where the new boundary is being delimited, that boundary containing the small portion is not removed. This is because the Community did not stand within it to perform the removal. Therefore, perform the act of removal in the ground outside the new boundary for at least a distance of two fathoms. Do not allow other bhikkhus to come near the act of removal because if other bhikkhus are inside the ancient boundary being removed while the act is being performed, the removal will not take place.

“Sīmā bhikkhave! samuhanantena paṭhamaṃ ticīvarena avippavāso samuhantabbo. Pacchā samāna saṃvāsā simā samuhan tabbā” According to this rule of Monastic Discipline, when removing a boundary, first perform the removal of the boundary of non-separation from the three robes with the removal formula. Thereafter, perform the removal of the boundary of common communion with the removal formula. Since the boundary of common communion and the boundary of non-separation from the three robes exist on the same ground, if they are not removed in both ways, the purification of the ground does not occur.

After the act of removal is performed systematically as stated, if there were ancient boundaries caught in that ground, all of them disappear and that ground becomes a purified village boundary. All Monastic Procedures can be performed in a village boundary as well. Boundary delimitation is also done within a village boundary. While the village itself is a suitable boundary for all Monastic Procedures, delimiting another boundary within it is for the convenience of performing Monastic Procedures. If a Monastic Procedure is to be performed in a village boundary, all bhikkhus residing in the village must be gathered there. The consent of bhikkhus who cannot attend must be obtained. While performing a Monastic Procedure in this way, if a bhikkhu happens to arrive anywhere in that village, it results in the failure of the act. Because of these facts, it is not easy to frequently perform Monastic Procedures in village boundaries. When a small boundary is delimited, only the bhikkhus residing within that boundary need to be gathered for a Monastic Procedure. Even if many bhikkhus are standing outside the boundary in plain sight, it does not harm the acts performed within the boundary. Small boundaries are delimited for that purpose.

After systematically removing the boundary and purifying the ground, if the boundary is to be delimited using stone marks, place adequate stone pillars in the four directions or the eight directions. If the boundary is to be delimited using water marks, dig holes in the four or eight directions and fill them with water. There is no fault in setting up the stone pillars before purifying the ground. At the time of delimiting the boundary within the prepared marks, bring all fellow bhikkhus living in that village to that boundary ground. If there are other bhikkhus living in that village who do not come there, their consent must be obtained. Or, make the bhikkhus who do not come there at the time of the act stay outside that village.

Place guards on the roads to stop guest bhikkhus who might enter the village unexpectedly. If a bhikkhu travels even in a vehicle on a road through the village while the act of boundary delimitation is being performed, the boundary delimitation fails. After guarding the village and the Community has gathered at the place of boundary delimitation, first proclaim the marks of the boundary being delimited so the Community can hear. This should be done by the bhikkhu who recites the formal act. The bhikkhu reciting the formal act should stand up, go to the eastern side of the boundary ground, and ask: “Puratthimāya disāya kiṃ nimittaṃ?” ('What is the mark in the eastern direction?'). If the mark is a stone pillar, another should answer: “Pāsāṇo bhante” ('A stone, venerable sir'). There is no fault even if the answer is given by an unordained person. Thereafter, the expert in Monastic Discipline should proclaim the mark so the Community can hear: “Phaso pāsāṇo nimittaṃ” ('This stone is the mark').

Thereafter, go to the south-east and ask: “Puratthimāya anudisāya kiṃ nimittaṃ?” If the mark there is a stone pillar, the answer “Pāsāṇo bhante” should be given. Thereafter, the expert in Monastic Discipline should proclaim the mark: “Phaso pāsāṇo nimittaṃ”. In this way, after asking and proclaiming the marks in all eight directions in order, to connect mark to mark, go back to the east and ask and proclaim the mark as before. Proclaiming the marks once in this way is sufficient. However, it is better if it is done three times.

When proclaiming the marks, if there are many marks in one direction, ask the first mark: “Puratthimāya disāya kiṃ nimittaṃ?” and then proclaim the remaining marks by asking: “Purat‍thimāya disāya aparaṃ kiṃ nimittaṃ” as long as there are marks. The same should be followed for the remaining directions. After proclaiming the marks, as it was said: “Sīmaṃ bhikkhave sammannantena paṭhamaṃ samānasaṃvāsā sīmā sammannitabbā pacchā ticīvarena avippavāso sammannitabbo”, when granting the boundary authorization, first perform the authorization of the boundary of common communion. Afterwards, perform the authorization of non-separation from the three robes on that same ground as appropriate. Immediately upon finishing the recitation of the boundary delimitation formula, such as “Suṇātu me bhante saṅgho”, according to the Buddha's command without error, the ground within the proclaimed marks becomes the boundary ground suitable for the Monastic Procedures of the Community, extending down to the water that supports the earth.

A ground where the boundary has been delimited once in this way will remain as a boundary as long as the Dispensation of the All-Knowing One lasts, unless the Community removes it again. Only the Community can make that ground a non-boundary again. Even if the boundary ground is raised later by filling it with soil, that ground remains the boundary. Even if a river flows over the boundary ground, pillars can be set up there, a platform built, and Monastic Procedures performed.

If two boundaries are delimited as a Major Boundary and a Section Boundary, an empty space must be left between those two boundaries. Stone pillars should also be placed outside the marks of the Section Boundary so that the boundary interval is clearly visible. Since Major Boundary delimitation is not performed nowadays, the method of delimiting boundaries as Section Boundaries and Major Boundaries has not been included in this book.

28.html.title: The Monastic Procedure for the Binding of a Boundary
28.html.body:
If the binding of a boundary is not performed in accordance with the Disciplinary Code, not a single monastic procedure performed therein will be valid. Therefore, the binding of a boundary is a monastic procedure that must be performed with extreme caution, great care, and utmost purity. To perform it purely, one must know the eleven defects of a boundary and the threefold perfection of a boundary.

“Ati khuddakā, ati mahati, khaṇḍanimittā, chāyānimittā, animittā, bahi sime ṭhitasammatā, nadiyā sammatā, samudde sammatā, jātassare sammatā, simāya simaṃ sambhindantena sammatā, simāya simaṃ ajjhottharantena sammatāti imehī ekādasahī ākāretī simato kammāni vipajjanti”

As it has been declared, the eleven defective boundaries are: a boundary that is too small, a boundary that is too large, a boundary with broken marks, a boundary authorized by shadow marks, a boundary authorized without proclaiming marks, a boundary authorized while standing outside the boundary, a boundary authorized in a river, a boundary authorized in the sea, a boundary authorized in a natural lake, a boundary authorized by mixing it with an ancient boundary, and a boundary authorized by overlapping an ancient boundary. Monastic procedures performed within such boundaries do not achieve validity.

(1) A boundary that is too small is one that does not have enough space for twenty-one bhikkhus to sit and perform a monastic procedure.

(2) A boundary that is too large is one that exceeds three leagues (yojanas) in length or breadth even by a small amount.

(3) A boundary with broken marks (khaṇḍanimitta) is one authorized without connecting mark to mark during the proclamation. Marks are the boundary indicators. When authorizing a boundary, the Vinaya expert must start from one point and ask, “Puratthimāya disāya kiṃ nimittaṃ?” and so on. Upon receiving the answer, he must proclaim the marks in order, such as “Eso pāsāṇo nimittaṃ”. In doing so, after proclaiming the final mark, the first proclaimed mark must be proclaimed again so that it connects with the last. If one stops at the proclamation of the final mark without doing this, it becomes a boundary with broken marks. It is a non-boundary. Furthermore, a boundary authorized by taking something unsuitable as a mark is also called a khaṇḍanimitta boundary.

(4) A shadow-mark boundary is one authorized by taking the shadow of a tree, mountain, or any other object as a mark.

(5) A markless boundary is one authorized without proclaiming the marks, even if marks have been placed.

(6) A boundary authorized while standing outside is one authorized by proclaiming the marks while standing outside of those marks.

(7,8,9) As it has been declared, “Sabbā bhikkhave nadi asīmā, sabbo samuddo asimo, sabbo jātassaro asimo”, boundaries authorized in a river, the sea, or a natural lake are not valid boundaries.

(10) A boundary authorized by including a small portion of a previously authorized boundary is also a non-boundary.

(11) A boundary authorized by including an entire previously authorized boundary, or a portion of it large enough for four bhikkhus to sit, is a boundary authorized by overlapping an ancient boundary. This too is a non-boundary.

The threefold perfection consists of: perfection of marks, perfection of the assembly, and perfection of the proclamation.

Marks are the boundary indicators of the ground being authorized. As it is stated: “Paṭhamaṃ nimittā kittetabbā, pabbatanimittaṃ, pāsāṇanimittaṃ, vananimittaṃ rukkhanimittaṃ magganimittaṃ vammikanimittaṃ nadinimittaṃ udakanimittaṃ”, there are eight objects that may be taken as marks for a boundary. They are: a mountain, a stone, a forest, a tree, a road, an anthill, a river, and water.

Mountain Mark (Pabbatanimitta)

There are three types of mountains: earth mountains, stone mountains, and mixed earth-and-stone mountains. If an elevation is smaller than the size of an elephant, it is not reckoned as a mountain. Sand dunes are also not reckoned as mountains. Therefore, hills not as high as an elephant and sand dunes are not taken as boundary marks. Where there are four mountains on four sides, a boundary authorization can be made by proclaiming them as marks. When there are three mountains, the binding of a boundary can be done with three marks. The binding of a boundary cannot be done with only one or two marks. If a portion of a mountain is to be included within the boundary ground, do not proclaim the mountain itself as the mark; instead, take a tree, stone, or anthill situated upon it as the boundary mark. The mark must always remain outside the boundary ground. Two connected mountains should be taken as a single mark, not as two marks. If a curved mountain lies on two or three sides of the boundary ground, it should be taken as a mark for only one direction. Even if the mountain exists, something else must be taken as a mark for the other sides. If the entire mountain is included within the boundary ground, trees or stones situated outside it must be taken as marks.

Stone Mark (Pāsāṇanimitta)

A stone belonging to any type is suitable as a boundary mark. Iron-slag is also reckoned as stone; therefore, it is suitable as a boundary mark. A stone as large as an elephant or larger should be proclaimed as a mountain mark, not as a stone mark. What should be proclaimed as a stone mark are stones smaller than an elephant. A heap of small pebbles or a brick should not be taken as a stone mark. The Vinaya Commentary states: “Battiṃsa pala guṇapiṇḍa parimāṇo vaṭṭati na tato khuddakataro”, meaning a stone smaller than a lump of sugar weighing thirty-two palas should not be taken as a boundary mark.

According to the medical usage in this country, twenty madati seeds make one kalañda. Twelve kalañdas make one pala. By that measure, thirty-two palas is approximately five and three-quarter pounds. It is said that the measure used in the Magadha country is twice the common worldly measure, and that the thirty-two palas should be taken by the Magadhan measure. Since there are various systems of measurement, the pala mentioned in the Commentary is uncertain. However, since such large lumps of sugar are unlikely, it cannot be said that stone pillars about two or three feet high, taken as boundary marks, are insufficient in size.

In Burma, Vinaya-observant Elders do not bind boundaries by setting up stone pillars as marks. They dig holes, fill them with water, and bind the boundary using water marks. Later, they set up stone pillars in those places to identify the locations. A rock plateau, no matter how large, can be taken as a stone mark. If one wishes to include a portion of a large rock plateau within the boundary ground, do not take the rock plateau as a boundary mark; instead, take something else upon it as the boundary mark. If the same rock plateau lies on two or three sides of the boundary ground, it should be taken as a mark for only one side. The same rock plateau should not be proclaimed as two or three marks. Something else must be taken as marks for the other sides.

Forest Mark (Vananimitta)

A forest made of trees without heartwood, such as palmyra, coconut, or kitul, is unsuitable to be taken as a boundary mark. What should be taken as a boundary mark is a forest made only of trees with heartwood, or one mixed with trees having heartwood. To be considered a forest, there must be at least four or five trees. Two or three trees should not be taken as a forest mark. No matter how large the forest taken as a mark is, there is no defect in its greatness. If a boundary is being bound in the middle of a forest, the forest should not be taken as a mark. Even when including a portion of a forest in the binding of a boundary, the forest should not be considered a mark. In a place where there is no forest within the boundary ground but a forest exists around that ground, the surrounding forest can be proclaimed as a mark for one direction.

Tree Mark (Rukkhanimitta)

A tree taken as a tree mark must also belong to a species that has heartwood. Trees where the heartwood is on the outside, such as bamboo, rattan, coconut, or areca, are not suitable to be taken as boundary marks. If it belongs to a species with heartwood, a living tree at least eight inches high and as thick as a stylus is suitable as a boundary mark. Even if large, a dead tree is unsuitable. A tree rooted in the ground is suitable, but a tree in a pot is unsuitable as a boundary mark. If one wishes to make a tree planted in a pot a boundary mark, it must be planted in the ground. A tree just planted, whose survival is uncertain, should also not be taken as a boundary mark. There are banyan trees that spread over a large area by rooting from their branches. Such a tree should be proclaimed as a mark for only one place, not for two or three places, because even though it is rooted in many places, it is still a single tree.

Road Mark (Magganimitta)

Short paths leading to gardens, fields, tanks, or ponds are unsuitable as boundary marks. What should be taken as boundary marks are long roads that pass through at least two or three villages. If a foot-path starts from a carriage-road and then falls back into that same carriage-road and ends, that path should also not be taken as a boundary mark. Even if it is a long road, if it has been abandoned by travelers, it is unsuitable as a boundary mark. What is suitable for proclamation as a road mark is a road used by travelers that passes through at least two or three villages. If two roads starting separately have joined at some point, only one of them, not both, should be made boundary marks. Boundary marks must be distinct from one another.

Anthill Mark (Vammikanimitta)

An anthill built on that very day, eight inches high and as thick as a cow's horn, is suitable to be taken as a boundary mark. An anthill smaller than that is not suitable to be taken as a boundary mark. Even if it is as large as a hill, an anthill is suitable as a boundary mark. If there is an anthill connected around the boundary ground, it must be proclaimed as a mark for only one direction.

River Mark (Nadinimitta)

During the rainy season, when it rains once every ten or fifteen days, if the flow of water stops as soon as the rain stops, it does not reach the status of a river. If, as stated, during the time of rain in the rainy season, the water flows without stopping, and if there is enough water to wet the inner robe of a bhikkhunī dressed with the three circles covered when she enters the river at a crossing or non-crossing point to cross over, then that river is a suitable river for a boundary mark and other monastic procedures. Even if the flow of water has stopped during the dry season, the river does not become a non-river. At that time too, it is suitable to be taken as a river mark.

If the flow of water in a small river has been stopped by people building dams, it is thereafter not suitable to be taken as a river mark. If the water flows over the obstruction, it is suitable as a river mark. What is suitable for proclamation as a river mark is flowing water. If standing water is proclaimed as a boundary mark, it must be done as a water mark. A river flowing around the boundary ground should be taken as a mark for only one direction, not for two or three directions. If there are rivers around the boundary ground that have no connection to each other, they can be taken as marks for those respective directions. A canal cut to take water from a great river, even if water flows constantly, is not suitable as a boundary mark. If that watercourse dug by people later becomes large due to the flow of much river water, and becomes a river where boats and crocodiles dwell, then it is suitable to be taken as a river mark. No specific length or breadth is given for a river suitable to be taken as a mark.

Water Mark (Udakanimitta)

Standing water collected anywhere on the ground that does not flow is suitable as a water mark. There is no fixed measure for it. Water collected in a hole dug by pigs or dogs, or in a hole dug by children for play, is suitable to be taken as a water mark. Even water filled into a hole just dug is suitable to be taken as a boundary mark if it lasts until the end of the recitation of the formal act. When a boundary is bound using such a small amount of water as a mark, stone pillars should be placed in the locations where the water used as marks was situated, so that the boundary ground can be identified in the future. Or some other permanent sign should be established.

The authorization of a boundary is for the purpose of performing Community acts. Since it is not a matter concerning the ownership of land, there is no fault for the bhikkhus in placing stone pillars in those places. In the authorization of a boundary, there is no question regarding land ownership. A boundary can be bound on land belonging to anyone. The ground does not become the property of the Sangha because of the binding of a boundary.

For the monastic procedure of binding a boundary to be possible, an assembly of no fewer than four bhikkhus must gather. The binding of a boundary is done within a village boundary. Therefore, when binding a boundary, all bhikkhus staying within that village who have not entered a bound boundary must be brought to that place. The consent (chanda) of those bhikkhus who cannot be brought must be obtained. By doing so, the binding of the boundary is endowed with the perfection of the assembly. If there is even a single bhikkhu within the village where the boundary is being bound who has not given consent and has not come to the place, the binding of the boundary will not be valid due to the defect of the assembly.

In some villages, there may be defective boundaries bound improperly by those who are not experts in the Vinaya. Since such boundaries are non-boundaries, if there is a bhikkhu who has not given consent within such a non-boundary in the village where the boundary is being bound, the new boundary also becomes a non-boundary. Since the status of previously existing boundaries may not be known, the most certain method when binding a boundary in any village is to bring all the bhikkhus living in that village to the site, and to remove any doubtful bhikkhus from the village before binding the boundary. By binding the boundary while reciting the formal motion and proclamation prescribed by the Buddha for the binding of a boundary without error, the monastic procedure of binding a boundary is endowed with the perfection of the proclamation.

Sub-topics

  • The Binding of a New Boundary
  • The Purification of the Boundary Ground

29.html.title: Formal Declaration concerning the Annulment of Boundaries and the Authorization of Boundaries.
29.html.body:
Annulment of the Boundary of Non-Separation from the Three Robes.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, yo so saṅghena ticīvarena avippavāso sammato, yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ, saṅgho taṃ ticīvarena avippavāsaṃ samūhaney‍ya. Esā ñatti.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, yo so saṅghena ticīvarena avippavāso sammato. Saṅgho taṃ ticīvarena avippavāsaṃ samūhanti. Yassāyasmato khamati etassa ticīvarena avippavāsassa samugghāto, so tuṇhassa, yassa nakkhamati, so bhāsey‍ya.

Samūhato so saṅghena ticīvarena avippavāso, khamati saṅghassa, tasmā tuṇhī. Evametaṃ dhārayāmi.

Annulment of the Boundary of Common Residence.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, yā sā saṅghena sīmā sammatā samānasaṃvā ekūposathā, yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ, saṅgho taṃ sīmaṃ samuhaney‍ya samānasaṃvāsaṃ ekūposathaṃ. Esā ñatti.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho. Yā sā saṅghena sīmā sammatā samāna saṃvāsā ekūposathā. Saṅgho taṃ sīmaṃ samūhanti samānasaṃvāsaṃ ekūposathaṃ. Yassāyasmato khamati, etissā sīmāya samānasaṃvāsāya ekūposathāya samugghāto. So tuṇhassa. Yassa nakkhamati. So bhāsey‍ya.

Samūhatā sā sīmā saṅghena samāna saṃvāsā ekūposathā, khamati saṅghassa, tasmā tuṇhī. Evametaṃ dhārayāmi.

Recitation of the Marks during the Authorization of a Boundary

What is the mark in the eastern direction?

A rock, Venerable Sir.

This rock is the mark.

What is the mark in the southeastern direction?

A rock, Venerable Sir.

This rock is the mark.

What is the mark in the southern direction?

A rock, Venerable Sir.

This rock is the mark.

What is the mark in the southwestern direction?

A rock, Venerable Sir.

This rock is the mark.

What is the mark in the western direction?

A rock, Venerable Sir.

This rock is the mark.

What is the mark in the northwestern direction?

A rock, Venerable Sir.

This rock is the mark.

What is the mark in the northern direction?

A rock, Venerable Sir.

This rock is the mark.

What is the mark in the northeastern direction?

A rock, Venerable Sir.

This rock is the mark.

What is the mark in the eastern direction?

A rock, Venerable Sir.

This rock is the mark.

Authorization of a Boundary of Common Residence

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho yāvatā samantā nimittā kittitā, yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ, saṅgho etehi nimittehi sīmaṃ sammanney‍ya, samānasaṃvāsaṃ ekūposathaṃ. Esā ñatti.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, yāvatā samantā nimittā kittitā, saṅgho etehi nimittehi sīmaṃ sammannati samānasaṃvāsaṃ ekūposathaṃ. Yassāyasmato khamati etehi nimittehi sīmāya sammuti samānasaṃvāsāya ekūposathāya. So tuṇhassa. Yassa nakkhamati, so bhāsey‍ya.

Sammatā sā sīmā saṅghena etehi nimittehi samānasaṃvāsā ekūposathā. Khamati saṅghassa, tasmā tuṇhī. Evametaṃ dhārayāmi.

Authorization of a Boundary of Non-Separation.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, yā sā saṅghena sīmā sammatā samānasaṃvāsā ekūposathā, yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ, saṅgho taṃ sīmaṃ ticīvarena avippavāsaṃ sammann‍ye ṭhapet‍vā gāmañca gāmupacārañca. Esā ñatti.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, yā saṃ saṅghena sīmā sammatā samānasaṃvāsā ekūposathā, saṅgho taṃ sīmaṃ ticīvarena avippavāsaṃ sammannati ṭhapet‍vā gāmañca gāmūpacārañca. Yassāyasmato khamati etissā sīmāya ticīvarena avippavāsāya sammuti ṭhapet‍vā gāmañca gāmupacārañca. So tuṇhassa. Yassa nakkhamati, so bhāsey‍ya.

Sammatā sā sīmā saṅghena ticīvarena avippavāsā ṭhapet‍vā gāmañca gāmūpacārañca. Khamati saṅghassa, tasmā tuṇhī. Evametaṃ dhārayāmi.
29.html.nextLabel: The Community Performing the Monastic Procedures

3-1.html.title: The Formal Act for Higher Ordination
3-1.html.body:
“Suṇātu me bhante saṅgho. Ayaṃ nāgo āyasmato tissassa upasampadāpekkho parisuddho antarāyikehi dhammehi, paripuṇṇassa pattacīvaraṃ. Nāgo saṅghaṃ upasampadaṃ yācati, āyasmatā tissena upajjhāyena, yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ. Saṅgho nāgaṃ upasampādey‍ya, āyasmatā tissena upajjhāyena. Esā ñatti.

Suṇātu me bhante saṅgho, ayaṃ nāgo āyasmato tissassa upasampadāpekkho, parisuddho antarāyike hi dhammehi, paripuṇṇassa pattacīvaraṃ, nāgo saṅghaṃ upasampadaṃ yācati, āyasmatā tissena upajjhāyena. Saṅgho nāgaṃ upasampādeti. Āyasmatā tissena upajjhāyena. Yassāyasmato khamati nāgassa upasampadā āyasmatā tissena upajjhāyena. So tuṇhassa. Yassa nakkhamati so bhāsey‍ya.

Dutiyampi etamat‍thaṃ vadāmi. Suṇātu me bhante saṅgho. Ayaṃ nāgo āyasmato tissassa upasampadāpekkho, parisuddho antarāyike hi dhammehi. Paripuṇṇassa pattacīvaraṃ. Nāge saṅghaṃ upasampadaṃ yācati. Āyasmatā tissena upajjhāyena. Saṅgho nāgaṃ upasampādeti. Āyasmatā tissena upajjhāyena. Yassāyasmato khamati nāgassa upasampadā, āyasmatā tissena upajjhāyena. So tuṇhassa. Yassa nakkhamati so bhāsey‍ya.

Tatiyampi etamat‍thaṃ vadāmi. Suṇātu me bhante saṅgho. Ayaṃ nāgo āyasmato tissassa upasampadāpekkho. Parisuddho antarāyike hi dhammehi. Paripuṇṇassa patta cīvaraṃ. Nāgo saṅghaṃ upasampadaṃ yācati. Āyasmatā tissena upajjhāyena. Saṅgho nāgaṃ upasampādeti. Āyasmatā tissena upajjhāyena. Yassāyasmato khamati nāgassa upasampadā. Āyasmatā tissena upajjhāyena. So tuṇhassa. Yassa nakkhamati so bhāsey‍ya.

Upasampanno saṅghena nāgo āyasmatā tissena upajjhāyena. Khamati saṅghassa. Tasmā tuṇhī, evametaṃ dhārayāmi.
3-1.html.nextLabel: Exhortation given at the conclusion of Higher Ordination

3-2.html.title: Instruction given at the conclusion of the Higher Ordination
3-2.html.body:
Tāvadeva jāyāmetabbā. Utuppamāṇaṃ ācikkhitabbaṃ divasabhāgo ācikkhitabbo. Saṅgīti ācikkhitabbā. Cattāro nissayā ācikkhitabbā. Cattāri akaraṇiyāni ācikkhitabbāni.

Since our Fortunate, Worthy, Perfectly Self-Enlightened Buddha, who has traversed all knowable realms and is greatly compassionate, has thus declared, and since the Great Community of monks, endowed with virtues such as good conduct, has consecrated the Venerable One with the supremely pure Higher Ordination, it has been permitted to declare these at the time of receiving the Higher Ordination in this sima (ordination hall): the measure of the shadow, the measure of the season, the division of the day, all of these, the four supports, and the four prohibitions. Therefore, please listen attentively to our words.

Venerable One, who has been consecrated with the supremely pure Higher Ordination precept! You, on the ................ day, the ................... tithi of the ................ month, in the ................ year of the Buddhist Era, corresponding to the ................ day of the ................ month, in the ................ year of the Common Era, at ................ in the ................ sima (ordination hall), were raised from the state of a novice and brought to the state of a bhikkhu with Higher Ordination by the Great Community of monks, led by ................... Bearing this in mind for the duration of your life, protect the Higher Ordination precept you have now received as you would your own life, without breaking even the smallest training rule.

3-3.html.title: The Four Resources
3-3.html.body:
Venerable one, who has entered the Dispensation of the Buddha desiring to realize Nibbana having seen the fear in the round of rebirths! Listen to the Four Resources prescribed by the Great Compassionate One for the maintenance of the life of a renunciant.

(1) “Piṇḍiyālopa bhojanaṃ nissāya pabbajjā. Tattha te yāvajīvaṃ ussāho karaṇīyo. Atireka lābho saṅghabhattaṃ. Uddesabhattaṃ, nimantaṇaṃ, salākabhattaṃ, pakkhikaṃ, uposathikaṃ, pāṭipadikaṃ.”

Friend! This going-forth is on account of food received by alms-gathering. Therein effort is to be made by you for the rest of your life. Extra allowances are: a meal for the entire Community of monks, a meal for a specific number of monks, an invitation, a meal given by tickets, a meal given on a day of the half-month, a meal given on an Uposatha day, and a meal given on the day after an Uposatha day; these should be accepted as extra allowances.

(2) “Paṃsukūla cīvaraṃ nissāya pabbajjā, tat‍tha te yāvajīvaṃ ussāho karaṇīyo. Atireka lābho khomaṃ kappāsikaṃ. Kosey‍yaṃ. Kambalaṃ. Sāṇaṃ. Bhaṅgaṃ.”

The going-forth is on account of rag-robes. Therein effort is to be made by you for the rest of your life. If, through the power of your previous merit, there should accrue to you extra allowances: robes made of linen, cotton, silk, wool, coarse hemp, or a mixture of these, they may be used as extra allowances.

(3) “Rukkhamūlasenāsanaṃ nissāya pabbajjā. Tat‍tha te yāvajīvaṃ ussāho karaṇīyo. Atireka lābho vihāro. Aḍḍhayogo. Pāsādo. Hammiyaṃ. Guhā”

The going-forth is on account of a lodging at the foot of a tree. Therein effort is to be made by you for the rest of your life, dwelling at the foot of a tree. If, through the power of your previous merit, there should accrue to you extra allowances: a monastery, a curved house, a long house, a mansion, or a cave, these also may be used.

(4) “Pūtimuttabhesajjaṃ nissāya pabbajjā. Tat‍tha te yāvajīvaṃ ussāho karaṇīyo. Atireka lābho sappi. Navanītaṃ telaṃ. Madhu. Phānitaṃ”

The going-forth is on account of fermented urine as medicine. Therein effort is to be made by you for the rest of your life to maintain yourself with fermented urine as medicine. If, through the power of your previous merit, there should accrue to you extra allowances: ghee, fresh butter, oil, honey, or molasses, these may be used as extra allowances.
3-3.html.prevLabel: Instruction at the Conclusion of Higher Ordination

3-4.html.title: The Four Unperformables
3-4.html.body:
Now, listen to the Four Unperformables.

(1) “Upasampannena bhikkhunā methuno dhammo na patisevitabbo, antamaso tiracchāna gatāyapi. Yo bhikkhu methunaṃ dhammaṃ patisevati, assamaṇo hoti asakyaputtiyo. Seyyathāpi nāma puriso sīsacchinno abhabbo tena sarīra bandhanena jīvituṃ. Evameva bhikkhu methunaṃ dhammaṃ patisevitvā assamaṇo hoti asakyaputtiyo. Taṃ te yāvajīvaṃ akaraṇīyaṃ”

By a bhikkhu who has received Higher Ordination, sexual intercourse should not be engaged in, not even with an animal.
