If any bhikkhu engages in sexual intercourse, by that he is no recluse, no son of the Sakyan. Just as a man whose head has been cut off is incapable of living again by that body connection, even so, a bhikkhu who has engaged in sexual intercourse is no recluse, no son of the Sakyan. Therefore, by you, that sexual intercourse should not be done as long as life lasts.

(2) “Upasampannena bhikkhunā adinnaṃ theyya saṅkhātaṃ na ādātabbaṃ antamaso tiṇasalākaṃ upādāya. Yo bhikkhu pādaṃ vā pādārahaṃ vā atirekapādaṃ vā adinnaṃ theyyasaṃkhātaṃ ādiyati assamaṇo hoti asakyaputtiyo. Seyyathāpi nāma paṇḍupalāso bandhanā pamutto abhabbo haritatthāya. Evameva bhikkhu pādaṃ vā pādārahaṃ vā atirekapādaṃ vā adinnaṃ theyyasaṃkhātaṃ ādiyitvā assamaṇo hoti asakyaputtiyo. Taṃ te yāvajīvaṃ akaraṇīyaṃ.”

By a bhikkhu who has received Higher Ordination, from a blade of grass upwards, that which is not given by the owners should not be taken with a thievish intent. If any bhikkhu takes with a thievish intent a *pada* of wealth, or an object worth a *pada*, or an object worth more than a *pada*, he is no recluse, no son of the Sakyan. Just as a withered leaf detached from its stalk is incapable of becoming green again, even so, a bhikkhu who has taken with a thievish intent a *pada* of wealth, or an object worth a *pada*, or an object worth more than a *pada*, is no recluse, no son of the Sakyan. That, by you, should not be done as long as life lasts.

(3) “Upasampannena bhikkhunā sañcicca pāṇo jīvitā na voropetabbo antamaso kuntha kipillikaṃ upādāya. Yo bhikkhu sañcicca manussaviggahaṃ jīvitā voropeti. Antamaso gabbha pātanaṃ upādāya. Assamaṇo hoti asakyaputtiyo. Seyyathāpi nāma puthusīlā dvidhā bhinnā appaṭisandhikā hoti. Evameva bhikkhu sañcicca manussa viggahaṃ jīvitā voropetvā assamaṇo hoti asakyaputtiyo. Taṃ te yāvajīvaṃ akaraṇīyaṃ”

By a bhikkhu who has received Higher Ordination, from an ant upwards, a living being should not be intentionally deprived of life. If any bhikkhu intentionally deprives a human being of life, even by causing an abortion, that bhikkhu is no recluse, no son of the Sakyan. Just as a slab of rock split in two is incapable of being joined again, even so, a bhikkhu who has intentionally deprived a human being of life is no recluse, no son of the Sakyan. That, by you, should not be done as long as life lasts.

(4) “Upasampannena bhikkhunā uttarimanussa dhammo na ullapitabbo antamaso suññāgāre abhiramāmīti. Yo bhikkhu pāpiccho icchāpakato asantaṃ abhūtaṃ uttarimanussadhammaṃ ullapati jhānaṃ vā vimokkhaṃ vā samādhiṃ vā samāpattiṃ vā maggaṃ vā phalaṃ vā. Assamaṇo hoti. Asakya puttiyo. Seyyathāpi nāma tālo matthakacchinno abhabbo puna virūḷhiyā. Evameva bhikkhu pāpiccho icchāpakato asantaṃ abhūtaṃ uttarīmanussadhammaṃ ullapitvā assamaṇo hoti. Asakyaputtiyo. Taṃ te yāvajīvaṃ akaraṇīyaṃ”

By a bhikkhu who has received Higher Ordination, even by saying, “I dwell delighting in an empty dwelling,” a superhuman state should not be declared. If any bhikkhu, being of evil wishes, overcome by evil wishes, declares a non-existent, unreal superhuman state in himself, such as jhāna, or vimokkha, or samādhi, or samāpatti, or magga, or phala, by that he is no recluse, no son of the Sakyan. Just as a palm tree whose crown has been cut off is incapable of growing again, even so, a bhikkhu, being of evil wishes, overcome by evil wishes, having declared a non-existent superhuman state in himself, is no recluse, no son of the Sakyan. That, by you, should not be done as long as life lasts.
3-4.html.nextLabel: Giving Higher Ordination to Two or Three Persons at Once

3-5.html.title: Higher Ordination of Two or Three Persons at Once
3-5.html.body:
At that time, disputes arose among the Candidates for Higher Ordination regarding who should receive Higher Ordination first. When this was reported to the Buddha, He permitted the Higher Ordination of two or three persons with the same Preceptor by a single announcement, saying: “Anujānāmi bhikkhave dve tayo ekānusāvane kātuṃ. Tañca kho ekena upajjhāyena, natveva nānupajjhāyena”. However, the formal procedure for this is not found in the Vinaya Pali. In the formal procedure books of Burma, it is presented thus:

“Suṇātu me bhante saṅgho. Ayaṃ ca nāgo ayaṃ ca datto āyasmato tissassa upasampadāpekkhā, parisuddhā antarāyikehi dhammehi. Paripuṇṇimesaṃ pattacīvaraṃ. Nāgo ca datto ca saṅghaṃ upasampadaṃ yācanti. Āyasmatā tissena upajjhāyena. Yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ. Saṅgho nāgaṃ ca dattaṃ ca upasampādey‍ya āyasmatā tissena upajjhāyena. Esā ñatti.
Let the Sangha hear me, venerable sirs. This Candidate and this Datta are Candidates for Higher Ordination of the Venerable Tissa; they are pure of obstructing conditions. Their bowl and robes are complete. The Candidate and Datta request Higher Ordination from the Community, with the Venerable Tissa as Preceptor. If the Sangha is ready, the Community should confer Higher Ordination upon the Candidate and Datta, with the Venerable Tissa as Preceptor. This is the Motion.

Suṇātu me bhanto saṅgho. Ayaṃ ca nāgo ayaṃ ca datto āyasmato tissassa upasampadā pekkhā. Parisuddhā antarāyikehi dhammehi. Paripuṇṇimesaṃ pattacīvaraṃ. Nāgo ca datto ca saṅghaṃ upasampadaṃ yācanti. Āyasmatā tissena upajjhāyena. Saṅgho nāgaṃ ca dattaṃ ca upasampādeti. Āyasmatā tissena upajjhāyena. Yassāyasmato khamati nāgassa ca dattassa ca upasampadā. Āyasmatā. Tissena upajjhāyena. So tuṇhassa. Yassa nakkhamati. So bhāsey‍ya.
Let the Sangha hear me, venerable sirs. This Candidate and this Datta are Candidates for Higher Ordination of the Venerable Tissa. They are pure of obstructing conditions. Their bowl and robes are complete. The Candidate and Datta request Higher Ordination from the Community, with the Venerable Tissa as Preceptor. The Community confers Higher Ordination upon the Candidate and Datta, with the Venerable Tissa as Preceptor. Whosoever of the venerable ones approves of the Higher Ordination of the Candidate and Datta, with the Venerable Tissa as Preceptor, should keep silent. Whosoever does not approve, should speak.

Dutiyampi etamat‍thaṃ vadāmi. -pe-
I state this matter a second time. -etc.-

Tatiyampi etamat‍thaṃ vadāmi. -pe- so bhāsey‍ya.
I state this matter a third time. -etc.- Whosoever does not approve, should speak.

Upasampannā saṅghena nāgo ca datto ca āyasmatā tissena upajjhāyena. Khamati saṅghassa. Tasmā tuṇhī. Evametaṃ dhārayāmi.”
The Candidate and Datta have received Higher Ordination from the Community, with the Venerable Tissa as Preceptor. The Community approves. Therefore, it is Silent. Thus I bear this in mind.

3-6.html.title: Persons Ineligible for Higher Ordination
3-6.html.body: The thirteen persons—namely, the eleven types of persons such as the paṇḍaka mentioned above, the person who has committed a pārājika offense, and the person who has not completed twenty years of age—are ineligible for Higher Ordination. Even if the Act of Higher Ordination is performed, the Higher Ordination does not take effect for them. If those with other disqualifications are given Higher Ordination, the officiating Community of monks incurs an offense of wrong-doing (dukkaṭa). However, the Higher Ordination takes effect for them. All those who are designated as unfit for Going Forth (pabbajjā) are also unfit for Higher Ordination. Nevertheless, if those such as those with severed hands (hatthacchinna) and others are given Higher Ordination, the Higher Ordination takes effect for them. By that act, the officiating Community of monks and the teachers and preceptors incur an offense. Therefore, in the Monastic Procedures Commentary named Samantapāsādikā, it is stated: “Yesaṃ ca pabbajjā paṭikkhittā upasampadāpi tesaṃ paṭikkhittā va. Sace pana saṅgho upasampādeti sabbepi hatthacchinnādayo suvupasampannā, kārakasaṅgho pana ācariyupajjhāyā ca āpattito na muccati”.
3-6.html.prevLabel: Higher Ordination of Two or Three Persons Simultaneously
3-6.html.nextLabel: The Age of the Candidate for Higher Ordination

3-7.html.title: The Age of the Candidate for Higher Ordination
3-7.html.body:
The age of the candidate for Higher Ordination is a matter of particular importance. “Yo pana bhikkhu jānaṃ ūnavīsati vassaṃ puggalaṃ upasampādey‍ya so ca puggalo anupasampanno. Teca bhikkhu gārayhā idaṃ tasmiṃ pācittiyaṃ” — Thus, the Tathāgata has laid down an offense of Expiation (Pācittiya) for a Preceptor who confers Higher Ordination upon a person known to be under twenty years of age. For the offense of conferring Higher Ordination on other disqualified persons, only an offense of Wrongdoing (Dukkaṭa) is prescribed, not an offense of Expiation. Therefore, we state that the age of the candidate for Higher Ordination must be considered with special care. The age of the candidate for Higher Ordination should be calculated not from birth, but from the moment of conception. This is to be understood from the following:

“Yaṃ bhikkhave! mātukucchismiṃ paṭhamaṃ cittaṃ uppannaṃ. Paṭhamaṃ viññāṇaṃ pātubhūtaṃ. Tadupādāya sāvassa jāti. Anujānāmi bhikkhave gabbhavīsaṃ upasampādetuṃ”

This is to be known from the teaching of the Monastic Procedures. A person who spent ten months in the mother's womb may be given Higher Ordination when nineteen years and two months have elapsed since birth. A person who spent nine months in the mother's womb may be given Higher Ordination after nineteen years and three months have elapsed since birth. There is no offense if one confers Higher Ordination by mistake, thinking a person who has not completed twenty years has indeed completed them. However, the Higher Ordination is not valid for the candidate whose age was misunderstood. If he, after ten years of seniority, becomes a Preceptor and confers Higher Ordination on a pupil, the Higher Ordination is valid for the pupil provided there were enough other bhikkhus present to complete the quorum for the formal act, excluding him. For that person who lives as a monk without knowing that he was ordained under twenty years of age, such living is not an obstacle to heaven or liberation. Even if he has reached old age, if he comes to know for certain that he was ordained under-age, he must receive Higher Ordination again. Since presenting oneself as one who has received Higher Ordination after knowing the truth is a deception, it becomes an obstacle for him.

“Anāpatti ūnavīsativassaṃ paripuṇṇavīsati saññīti ettha kiñcāpi upasampādentassa anāpatti puggalo pana anupasampannova hoti. Sace pana so dasavassaccayena aññaṃ upasampādeti. Taṃ ce muñcitvā gaṇo pūrati. Supasampanno so pi ca yāva na jānāti. Tāvassa neva saggantarāyo na mokkhantarāyo. Ñatvā pana puna upasampajjitabbaṃ” — Thus states the Vinaya Commentary.
3-7.html.nextLabel: Bhikkhus Suitable to be a Preceptor

3-8.html.title: Monks Suitable to Be an Upajjhāya
3-8.html.body:
Not all are suitable to become an Upajjhāya and give novice ordination and Higher Ordination to disciples. A bhikkhu who, without being suitable, becomes an Upajjhāya and gives novice ordination and Higher Ordination to disciples incurs an offence of dukkata.

“Na bhikkhave! Bālena abyattena upasampādetabbo. Anujānāmi bhikkhave! Byattena bhikkhunā paṭibalena dasavassena vā atireka dasavassena vā upasampādetuṃ” This is the word of the Buddha.

“Bhikkhus, Higher Ordination should not be given by a foolish, inexperienced bhikkhu. Bhikkhus, I allow Higher Ordination to be given by a bhikkhu who is experienced, capable, and has ten years or more than ten years [since his Higher Ordination],” this is its meaning.

Monks suitable to be an Upajjhāya are called ‘parisūpatthāyaka bahussuta’. The qualities of a parisūpatthāyaka bahussuta are as follows:

Being proficient in both the Bhikkhu and Bhikkhunī Vibhaṅgas of the Vinaya; knowing the discernment of what is a valid act and what is not; knowing the Vassakkhandhaka; having mastered either the Mūlapaṇṇāsaka from the Majjhima Nikāya, or the Mahāvagga from the Dīgha Nikāya, or the three lower sections or the Mahāvagga from the Saṃyutta Nikāya, or half of the Aṅguttara Nikāya, or the Dhammapada with its commentary or the Jātaka book from the Khuddaka Nikāya; and having ten years since Higher Ordination—these are the qualities of a parisūpatthāyaka bahussuta.

After Higher Ordination:

A newly ordained bhikkhu must reside with his Upajjhāya, associating with him, in the same place where the Upajjhāya resides, until five years have passed. A novice bhikkhu who resides separated from his Upajjhāya incurs an offence of dukkata with each dawn. An Upajjhāya who, having given Higher Ordination to others, then abandons them, also incurs an offence of dukkata due to breaking the observance. Unless one is a ‘nissaya muttaka bahussuta’, a bhikkhu must reside with his Upajjhāya or an ācariya for life.

Having memorized both Prātimokṣas; being proficient in four recitations (bāṇavara) from the Sutta Piṭaka; having practiced some method of discourse to teach those who come to him; having practiced three discourses for the purpose of anumodanā (rejoicing) on auspicious and inauspicious occasions; knowing about Monastic Procedures such as Uposatha and Pavāraṇā; having practiced some path of meditation; and having completed five years since Higher Ordination—these are the qualities of a nissaya muttaka bahussuta.
3-8.html.prevLabel: The Age of the Higher Ordination Candidate

3-9.html.title: Taking Dependence
3-9.html.body:
A bhikkhu who has not been released from dependence due to some reason such as the passing away of his preceptor, if the preceptor's dependence is lost, that bhikkhu should live by taking dependence from a venerable, learned elder who is diligent in attendance. The bhikkhu taking dependence should approach the dependence-preceptor, arrange his upper robe over one shoulder, squat, and having paid homage, should take dependence by repeating the phrase “Ācāriyo me bhante hohi. Āyasmato nissāya vacchāmi” three times. The dependence-preceptor should accept it by saying “Pāsādikena sampādetha”. The words Sāhu, Lahu, Opāyikaṃ, Patirūpaṃ are also suitable for this purpose.

If a bhikkhu who has observed many rains retreats but lacks sufficient learning to be released from dependence cannot find an elder from whom to take dependence, he should take dependence from a younger, venerable, learned bhikkhu who is diligent in attendance. He should pay homage even to a teacher who is younger than himself. However, the word “Bhante” should not be used when addressing a junior. When an elder bhikkhu takes dependence from a junior bhikkhu, he should, in the presence of the dependence-preceptor, arrange his upper robe over one shoulder, squat, and having paid homage, say “Ācāriyo me āvuso hohi. Āyasmato nissāya vacchāmi” three times. When asking permission to enter a village, he should also squat, pay homage, and say “Gāmappavesanaṃ āpucchāmi ācariya”. The same procedure should be followed when asking questions.

Between the preceptor and the dependence-preceptor, the preceptor is primary. Dependence should be taken when one is separated from the preceptor. If, while the preceptor resides in another locality and one is living having taken dependence from another elder, the preceptor returns there, the dependence taken by the pupil ceases the moment he sees his preceptor. (It becomes null and void.) If the preceptor departs again, since the previously taken dependence has ceased, dependence must be taken again. When one receives Higher Ordination, the preceptor one has taken remains valid at all times. There is no need to take another. Dependence, however, must be taken again and again whenever it ceases.

3.html.title: The Procedure for Higher Ordination.
3.html.body:
The procedure for Higher Ordination is a grave Monastic Procedure in the Buddha's Dispensation. Unlike the act of going forth (pabbajja), it cannot be performed everywhere; it must be performed only within a consecrated boundary (sima). If it is performed in the Middle Country, it must be carried out by a Community of ten or more than ten bhikkhus, as stated: “Anujānāmi bhikkhave! dasavaggena vā atireka dasa vaggena vā gaṇena upasampādetuṃ”. If it is performed in the border districts, it may be carried out by a group of five bhikkhus with a Vinaya-expert as the fifth, as stated: “Anujānāmi bhikkhave! sabbapaccantimesu janapadesu vinayadharapañcamena gaṇena upasampādetuṃ”. The Blessed One Himself has designated the border districts.

They are as follows:-

“Puratthimāya disāya kajaṅgalaṃ nāma nigamo, tassā parena mahāsālā, tato’parā paccantimā janapadā, orato majjhe. Puratthimadakkhiṇāya disāya salalavatī nāma nadī, tato ‘Parā paccantimā janapadā, orato majjhe. Dakkhiṇāya disāya setakaṇṇikaṃ nāma nigamo, tato” parā paccantimā janapadā orato majjhe. Pacchimāya disāya thūnaṃ nāma b‍rāhmaṇa gāmo, tato’ parā paccantimā janapadā orato majjhe. Uttarāya disāya usīraddhajo nāma pabbato, tato’parā paccantimā janapadā orato majjhe.

Speaking from the perspective of the Middle Country: to the east, beyond the market town of Kajangala; to the south-east, beyond the river Salalavati; to the south, beyond the market town of Setakannika; to the west, beyond the brahmin village of Thuna; and to the north, beyond the mountain Usiraddhaja—these are the border districts. Since our country also belongs to the border districts, the procedure for Higher Ordination can be performed here with five bhikkhus. In performing a Higher Ordination, the majority of the duties fall upon the Act-proclaimer (kammacariya). When the candidate for Higher Ordination arrives at the boundary, if it is difficult to recite the Act-proclamation using the original names of the candidate and the Preceptor, the name Nāga should be assigned to the candidate and Tissa to the Preceptor. This authorization should be made by the Act-proclaimer as follows:

Discarding the former name of this candidate for Higher Ordination, I assign to him the name Nāga; and discarding the former name of the candidate's Preceptor, I assign to him the name Tissa for the ease of reciting the Act-proclamation. This should be known by the candidate for Higher Ordination, by the Preceptor, and by the Great Community.

After the authorization of names, the Act-proclaimer should speak to the candidate for Higher Ordination as follows:

Since the Protector of the World has stated, “Paṭhamaṃ upajjhaṃ gāhāpetabbo”: "O novice, who has come to receive Higher Ordination with the desire to cross the ocean of samsara and reach the city of Nibbana! You must first take a Preceptor."

When this is said, the candidate for Higher Ordination should sit in the squatting posture before the Preceptor and say the following sentence three times: “Upajjhāyo me bhante hohi”. The Preceptor should accept it by saying, “Pāsādikena sampādehi”.

Thereafter, the Act-proclaimer should speak as follows: "Venerable novice! Since the Blessed One, the Lord of the World, has stated, “Upajjhāṃ gāhāpetvā pattacīvaraṃ ācikkhitabbaṃ”, I shall now speak to you so that you may know the monastic designations regarding the bowl and robes. “Ayaṃ te patto”: Venerable novice, this black-colored, circular vessel is your bowl. “Ayaṃ saṃghāṭi”: This robe upon your shoulder, made of cut pieces, sewn double-layered, and dyed, is known in monastic usage as the outer robe (sanghati). “Ayaṃ uttarāsaṃgo”: This robe upon your body, made of cut pieces, sewn single-layered, and dyed, is known in monastic usage as the upper robe (uttarasanga). “Ayaṃ antaravāsako”: This robe which you are wearing is known in monastic usage as the inner robe (antaravasaka).

‘Gaccha: amumhi okāse tiṭṭhāhi. Venerable novice! Since the Tathagata has stated, “Anujānāmi bhikkhave! ekamantaṃ anusāsitvā saṅgha majjhe antarāyike dhamme pucchituṃ”—that the candidate for Higher Ordination should be instructed in a place apart from the Community, and since it is inappropriate to instruct you in the midst of the Community—go and stand in that place over there."

When this is said, the candidate for Higher Ordination should rise and, without turning his back to the Great Community, walk a short distance with hands joined in reverence and stand there. The Act-proclaimer should go there and instruct the candidate for Higher Ordination. Since it is stated, “Na bhikkhave! asammatena anusāsitabbo. Yo anusāsey‍ya āpatti dukkaṭassa”, one should not go to instruct without obtaining authorization. Now, the Act-proclaimer should authorize himself by reciting the following sentence:

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho, nāgo āyasmato tissassa upasampadāpekkho. Yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ ahaṃ nāgaṃ anusāsey‍yaṃ.

Thereafter, rising from the midst of the Community and approaching the candidate for Higher Ordination, he should instruct him while standing as follows:

Suṇasi nāga! aya te saccakālo, bhūtakālo, yaṃ jātaṃ taṃ saṅghamajjhe pucchante santaṃ atthiti vattabbaṃ. Asantaṃ natthiti vattabbaṃ. Mā kho vit‍thāsi, mā kho maṅku ahosi.

"O novice named Naga, who has dedicated your life to the Buddha's Dispensation to cross the ocean of samsara! This is the time for you to receive the supreme Higher Ordination morality, characterized by higher virtue in this most rare Dispensation of the All-Knowing One. This is the time to speak only the truth honestly, without hiding anything. This is the time to declare things as they are, without attempting to conceal the facts. If there is any disease such as leprosy or boils on your body, when you are questioned in the midst of the Community, you must say 'It is' if it exists. You must say 'It is not' if it does not exist. When questioned in the midst of the Community, do not be terrified with the fear that they are questioning you to do you harm. Do not be displeased or frown, thinking they are questioning whether non-existent things exist in order to disparage you."

“Evaṃ taṃ pucchissanti, santi te evarūpā ābādhā, kuṭṭhaṃ, gaṇḍo, kilāso, soso, apamāro, manussosi, purisosi, bhujissosi, anaṇosi, nasi rājabhaṭo, anuññātosi mātā pitūhi, paripuṇṇavīsativassosi, paripuṇṇaṃ te pattacīvaraṃ, kiṃ nāmosi, ko nāmo te upajjhāyoti.”

"Venerable novice! Since the Tathagata has allowed the questioning of the thirteen obstructive factors from the candidate for Higher Ordination, stating, “Anujānāmi bhikkhave! upasampādentena terasa antarāyike dhamme pucchituṃ”, they will question you in the midst of the Community during this preliminary stage of the Higher Ordination as follows: Do you have any of the diseases known as leprosy, boils, eczema, consumption, or epilepsy? Are you a human being? Are you a man? Are you a free man? Are you without debt? Are you not in royal service? Have you been permitted by your parents? Are you a full twenty years of age? Are your bowl and robes complete? What is your name? What is your Preceptor's name? Thus they will question you in the midst of the Community."

Having instructed him thus, leave the candidate for Higher Ordination standing there, and the Act-proclaimer should return to the midst of the Community and say:

“Suṇātu me bhante saṃgho, nāgo āyasmato tissassa upasampadāpekkho anusiṭṭho so mayā yadi saṃghassa pattallaṃ nāgo āgacchey‍ya”

Having said this sentence and obtained permission from the Community, summon the candidate for Higher Ordination to the midst of the Community. Thereafter, since it is stated, “Na bhikkhave! ayācitena upasampādetabbo. Yo upasampādey‍ya āpatti dukkaṭassa”, have the candidate for Higher Ordination request the Higher Ordination. At this point, the candidate for Higher Ordination should wear the robe over one shoulder, pay homage at the feet of the bhikkhus, sit in the squatting posture with hands joined in reverence, and say:

“Saṃghaṃ bhante upasampadaṃ yācāmi, ullumpatu maṃ bhante saṅgho anukampaṃ upādāya. Dutiyampi bhante saṅghaṃ upasampadaṃ yācāmi, ullumpatu maṃ bhante saṅgho anukampaṃ upādāya. Tatiyampi bhante saṅghaṃ upasampadaṃ yācāmi, ullumpatu maṃ bhaṇte saṅgho anukampaṃ upādāya.” Thus he should request Higher Ordination.

Then, the Act-proclaimer, having obtained authorization to question the obstructive factors through the formal act: “Suṇātu me bhante saṅgho, ayaṃ nāgo āyasmato tissassa upasampadāpekkho yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ ahaṃ nāgaṃ antarāyike dhamme pucchey‍yaṃ”, should question the obstructive factors as follows:

Suṇasi nāga! ayaṃ te saccakālo, bhūtakālo, yaṃ jātaṃ taṃ pucchāmi. Santaṃ atthiti vattabbaṃ. Asantaṃ natthiti vattabbaṃ. Santi te evarūpā ābādhā kuṭṭhaṃ? (natthi bhante) Gaṇḍo? (natthi bhante) Kilāso? (natthi bhante) Soso? (natthi bhante) Apamāro? (natthi bhante) Manussosi? (āma bhante) Purisosi? (āma bhante) Bhujissosi? (āma bhante) Anaṇosi? (āma bhante) Nasi rājābhaṭo? (āma bhante) Anuññātosi mātāpituhi? (āma bhante) Paripuṇṇa vīsati vassosi? (āma bhante) Paripuṇṇaṃ te pattacīvaraṃ? (āma bhante) Kiṃ nāmosi? (Ahaṃ bhante nāgo nāma) Ko nāmo te upajjhāyo? (Upajjhāyo me bhante āyasmā tissatthero nāma) After questioning the obstructive factors, the Higher Ordination should be performed using the Act-proclamation consisting of a motion and three announcements (natticatuttha-kamma).

Sub-topics

  • The Higher Ordination Act-proclamation
  • The Instruction at the Conclusion of Higher Ordination
  • The Four Requisites
  • The Four Forbidden Acts
  • Ordaining Two or Three Simultaneously
  • Persons Unfit for Higher Ordination
  • The Age of the Candidate for Higher Ordination

30-1.html.title: The Four Monastic Procedures.
30-1.html.body:
Monastic Procedures are of four types: Apalokana-kamma (Declaration Procedure), Ñatti-kamma (Motion Procedure), Ñatti-dutiya-kamma (Motion-and-one-Proclamation Procedure), and Ñatti-catuttha-kamma (Motion-and-three-Proclamations Procedure).

The procedure performed by assembling the Community within the boundary, taking the consent of those who cannot attend, and announcing the matter to the Community three times is called ‘Apalokana-kamma’ (Declaration Procedure). The procedure performed, as stated, with the approval of the Community, solely by a Motion, such as “Suṇātu me bhante saṅgho ajjupposatho paṇṇaraso yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ, saṅgho uposathaṃ kareyya”, is called ‘Ñatti-kamma’ (Motion Procedure). The procedure performed with the approval of the Community, with a Motion and one proclamation, is called ‘Ñatti-dutiya-kamma’ (Motion-and-one-Proclamation Procedure). The procedure performed with one Motion and three proclamations is called ‘Ñatti-catuttha-kamma’ (Motion-and-three-Proclamations Procedure).

In the procedural texts, the initial section from ‘Suṇātu me bhante saṅgho’ up to ‘esā ñatti’ is the Motion. The subsequent sections of text, such as “Suṇātu me bhante saṅgho”, are the proclamation.

Among these Monastic Procedures, what should be performed as an Apalokana-kamma must be done by declaration only, and not as a Ñatti-kamma or other such procedure. A Ñatti-kamma must be performed with a Motion. It must not be performed as an Apalokana-kamma or other such procedure. Among the Ñatti-dutiya-kamma procedures, there are those that should be performed by declaration and those that should not. The six procedures of boundary Authorizations (sīmā-sammuti), boundary revocation (sīmā-samūhanana), offering the Kathina robe (kaṭhina-dāna), revoking the Kathina (kaṭhinuddhāra), declaring a site for a hut (kuṭi vatthu desanā), and declaring a site for a dwelling (vihāra vatthu desanā) are weighty procedures (garu-kamma); therefore, they must be performed by reciting the Ñatti-dutiya-kamma text itself. They must not be performed by declaration. The remaining thirteen Authorizations, and the Authorizations for receiving a dwelling (senāsana-gāhaka-sammuti) and for offering a dead monk's robe (mataka-cīvara-dāna-sammuti), are light procedures (laghu-kamma); therefore, there is no fault in performing them by declaration. A Ñatti-kamma must absolutely not be performed as a Ñatti-catuttha-kamma. If there is an error in letters or words when reciting the procedural text, it is proper to recite it again and again for purification. Doing so is for the accomplishment of an unaccomplished procedure, and for the strengthening of an accomplished procedure. A Ñatti-catuttha-kamma must be performed with a Motion and three proclamations. It must not be performed as an Apalokana-kamma or other such procedure.

30-2.html.title: Failures of Monastic Procedures.
30-2.html.body:
A failure of a procedure (kamma-vipatti) refers to a defect in the formal act. A procedure performed incorrectly is not a valid act. Therefore, when performing Monastic Procedures, they must be conducted with absolute purity, ensuring no defect occurs at any point.

“Imāni cattāri kammāni katihākārehi vipajjanti? Pañcahākārehi vipajjanti, vatthuno vā ñattito vā anusāvanato vā sīmato vā parisato vā”.

As stated in the Parivāra, the four types of procedures mentioned above fail in five ways: regarding the basis (vatthu), the Motion (ñatti), the proclamation (anusāvana), the boundary (sīmā), and the assembly (parisa). These constitute unrighteous acts (adhamma-kamma).

“Sammukhā karaṇīyaṃ kammaṃ asammukhā karoti vatthuvipannaṃ adhammakammaṃ” etc. As stated in the Parivāra, if a procedure that should be performed in the presence of the individual is performed in their absence; if a procedure that requires questioning is performed without questioning; if a procedure that requires a confession is performed without a confession; if a verdict of mindfulness (sati-vinaya) is given to one who should receive a verdict of past insanity (amūḷha-vinaya); if amūḷha-vinaya is given to one who should receive an act of further misconduct (tassapāpiyyasikā-kamma); if tassapāpiyyasikā-kamma is performed on one who should receive an act of censure (tajjaniya-kamma); if tajjaniya-kamma is performed on one who should receive an act of subordination (niyassa-kamma); if niyassa-kamma is performed on one who should receive an act of banishment (pabbājanīya-kamma); if pabbājanīya-kamma is performed on one who should receive an act of reconciliation (paṭisāraṇiya-kamma); if paṭisāraṇiya-kamma is performed on one who should receive an act of suspension (ukkhepaniya-kamma); if ukkhepaniya-kamma is performed on one who should receive probation (parivāsa); if parivāsa is given to one who should receive a sending back to the beginning (mūlāya paṭikassana); if mūlāya paṭikassana is performed on one who should receive penance (mānatta); if mānatta is given to one who should receive rehabilitation (abbhāna); if abbhāna is performed for one who should receive Higher Ordination (upasampadā); if upasampadā is performed for one who should receive another act; if Uposatha is performed on a non-Uposatha day; if Pavāraṇā is performed on a non-Pavāraṇā day; if a eunuch (paṇḍaka) is given Higher Ordination; or if one who has gone over to another sect (titthiyapakkantaka), an animal, a matricide, a patricide, a killer of an Arahant, a violator of a nun, a schismatic, one who has shed the blood of a Buddha, a hermaphrodite (ubhatobyañjanaka), or one under twenty years of age is given Higher Ordination—these are unrighteous acts due to failure of the basis (vatthu-vipanna).

In times of danger, the following eight acts are suitable to be performed even in the absence of the individual: Higher Ordination of a nun via a messenger, the overturning of the bowl (pattanikkujjana), the authorization for one insane (ummattaka-sammuti), the authorization for a trainee (sekha-sammuti), the Brahma-punishment (brahmadanda), the act of public proclamation (pakāsanīya-kamma), and the act of non-salutation (avandiya-kamma). All other procedures must be performed in the presence of the individual concerned. If performed in their absence, those acts are unrighteous acts due to failure of the basis. They are acts contrary to the Monastic Procedures. In procedures requiring questioning, the questioning of the individual is the basis itself; if performed without questioning, the act becomes unrighteous due to failure of the basis. In performing acts like Higher Ordination for unsuitable persons, the unsuitability of those persons itself constitutes the failure of the basis.

“Pañcahākārehi ñattiyo kammāni vipajjanti. Vatthuṃ na parāmasati, saṅghaṃ na parāmasati, puggalā na parāmasati, ñattiṃ na parāmasati pacchā vā ñattiṃ ṭhapeti. Imehi pañcahākārehi ñattiyo kammāni vipajjanti”. As stated in the Parivāra, procedures fail regarding the Motion due to these five factors: not mentioning the basis, not mentioning the Community, not mentioning the individual, not mentioning the Motion, or placing the Motion last.

Therein, "not mentioning the basis" means reciting the Motion without including the name of the person for whom the act (such as Higher Ordination) is being performed. Where one should say, “Suṇātu me bhante saṅgho ayaṃ nāgo āyasmato tissassa upasampadāpekkho”, if the name of the candidate for Higher Ordination is omitted and the formula is recited as “Suṇātu me bhante saṅgho āyasmato tissassa upasampadāpekkho”, this is a failure to mention the basis.

Where one should say, “Suṇātu me bhante saṅgho ayaṃ nāgo āyasmato tissassa upasampadāpekkho”, if the word “saṅgho” is omitted and one says, “Suṇātu me bhante ayaṃ nāgo āyasmato tissassa upasampadāpekkho”, this is a failure to mention the Community.

"Not mentioning the individual" means omitting the name of the preceptor of the candidate for Higher Ordination. Where one should say, “Suṇātu me bhante saṅgho ayaṃ nāgo āyasmato tissassa upasampadāpekkho”, if one says, “Suṇātu me bhante saṅgho ayaṃ nāgo upasampadāpekkho”, this is a failure to mention the individual.

"Not mentioning the Motion" means omitting the Motion entirely and only reciting the proclamation. "Placing the Motion last" means reciting the proclamation first and then saying, “Esā ñatti”, followed by “Khamati saṅghassa tasmā tuṇhī evametaṃ dhārayāmi”.

“Pañcahākārehi anusāvaṇato kammāti vipajjanti. Vatthuṃ na parāmasati, saṅghaṃ na parāmasati, puggalaṃ na parāmasati. Sāvaṇaṃ hāpeti, akāle sāveti imehi pañcahākārehi anusāvaṇato kammāni vipajjanti”. As stated in the Parivāra, procedures fail regarding the proclamation due to these five factors: not mentioning the basis, not mentioning the Community, not mentioning the individual, omitting the proclamation, or proclaiming at the wrong time.

Here too, the failure to mention the basis and so forth should be understood as explained regarding the Motion. "Omitting the proclamation" means reciting only the Motion and leaving out the proclamation. It also includes two factors: mispronouncing letters and words. Not only the proclamation but also the Motion must be recited without error. By the bhikkhus reciting the procedural formula—

“Sithilaṃ danitañca dīgharassaṃ

Garukaṃ lahukañca niggahitaṃ,

Sambandhaṃ vavatthitaṃ vimuttaṃ

Dasadhā vyañjanabuddhiyāppabhedo.”

It must be performed considering these ten factors mentioned in the verse. Sithila refers to unaspirated characters. Dhanita refers to aspirated characters. Niggahīta refers to the nasal sound (bindu) which should be pronounced by sending air through the nose with the mouth neither fully open nor fully closed. Vimutta refers to characters that should be pronounced by opening the mouth and releasing the breath without tightening the vocal organs. These four factors must be specially considered when reciting the procedural formula. The Vinaya Commentary states that pronouncing an unaspirated sound as aspirated, an aspirated as unaspirated, a nasal as released, or a released as nasal causes the failure of the act (kamma-kopa), while errors in the other six factors do not cause the act to fail. Nevertheless, when performing Monastic Procedures, the formula should be recited such that purity is maintained regarding all ten factors. "Proclaiming at the wrong time" means reciting the proclamation before the Motion.

Procedures performed within the eleven types of defective boundaries fail due to boundary failure (sīmā-vipatti). The eleven defective boundaries have been explained previously in the section on the Monastic Procedures for defining boundaries.

“Dvādasahi ākārehi parisato kammāni vipajjanti” etc. The Parivāra declares that a procedure fails due to the assembly in twelve ways. Failure due to the assembly occurs through three factors: the required number of suitable bhikkhus not being present within the boundary, not taking the consent (chanda) of those bhikkhus from whom it should be taken, or the procedure being blocked by some present bhikkhus expressing dissent. These three factors, when applied to the four categories of procedures (those requiring a group of four, etc.), result in twelve ways an act fails due to the assembly.
30-2.html.nextLabel: How to Act Regarding Unrighteous Procedures

30-3.html.title: How to Proceed Regarding Unlawful Procedures
30-3.html.body:
The group-of-six bhikkhus began performing unlawful procedures in the midst of the Community of monks. The bhikkhus reported this matter to the Blessed One. Thereupon, the Tathāgata, saying, “Na bhikkhave adhammakammaṃ kātabbaṃ, yo karey‍ya āpatti dukkaṭassa”, laid down an offense of wrong-doing for those bhikkhus who perform unlawful procedures. Even then, without ceasing, those bhikkhus continued to perform unlawful procedures. The bhikkhus reported this to the Blessed One. Then the Tathāgata, by saying, “Anujānāmi bhikkhave adhammakamme kayiramāne paṭikkosituṃ”, authorized the bhikkhus assembled there to protest and prevent the unlawful procedure. When they did so, the group-of-six bhikkhus, being angry, threatened those bhikkhus. When this was reported to the Blessed One, by saying, “Anujānāmi bhikkhave diṭṭhimpi āvikātuṃ”, he authorized them to declare their view, saying, "This is an unlawful procedure; this is not acceptable to us."

When the group-of-six bhikkhus were performing unlawful procedures, the bhikkhus told the group-of-six bhikkhus themselves that it was not acceptable to them. Angered by this, the group-of-six bhikkhus threatened those bhikkhus. When this was reported to the Blessed One, by saying, Anujānāmi bhikkhave catūhi pañcahi paṭikkosituṃ, dvīhi tīhi diṭṭhiṃ āvikātuṃ, ekena adhiṭṭhātuṃ, he authorized four or five bhikkhus to protest, two or three to declare their view, and a single bhikkhu to make a mental resolution.

When the bhikkhu reciting the motion says, “Yassa nakkhamati so bhāsey‍ya”, if it is an unlawful procedure, the bhikkhus who do not approve must declare their view, saying, “Adhammakammaṃ etaṃ, na me taṃ khamati”. If one is afraid to declare it so that all may hear, he should declare it to a bhikkhu staying near him. If even that is not possible, he should resolve, “Na me taṃ khamati”.

If a bhikkhu who has arrived within the boundary to participate in the procedure protests, or at the very least expresses his disapproval to a bhikkhu near him, that procedure is not a valid procedure. A failure of the procedure occurs only if the opposition is expressed within the boundary, not elsewhere afterwards. The protest of unordained persons, those of different communion, ordained insane persons, bhikkhus suffering from such intense pain that they lose consciousness, or bhikkhus outside the boundary does not render the procedure invalid.

Here ends the book on Monastic Procedures compiled by the Venerable Acariya Rerukane Chandavimala Mahasthavira.

30.html.title: The Community Performing Monastic Procedures
30.html.body:
Catuvaggo pañcavaggo - dasa vīsati vaggiko

Atireka vīsati vaggo - pañca saṅghā vibhāvitā.

As it is stated, there are five types of the Community: a group of four, a group of five, a group of ten, a group of twenty, and a group of more than twenty.

With the exception of the procedure for rehabilitation (abbhāna), the procedure for Higher Ordination, and the procedure for the Invitation (pavāraṇā) of the Community, all other procedures may be performed by a group of four. With the exception of the procedure for rehabilitation and the procedure for Higher Ordination in the Middle Country, all other procedures may be performed by a group of five. With the exception of the procedure for rehabilitation, all Monastic Procedures may be performed by a group of ten. A group of twenty and a group of more than twenty may perform all Monastic Procedures.

Sub-topics

  • The Four Procedures.
  • Failure of Procedures.
  • How to conduct oneself regarding procedures not in accordance with the Dhamma
30.html.prevLabel: The procedural wording for the removal of boundaries and the Authorizations of boundaries.

4-1.html.title: Formal Resolve of the Bowl
4-1.html.body:
Since it has been declared: “Dasāhaparamaṃ atireka patto dhāretabbo. Taṃ atikkāmayato nissaggiyaṃ pācittiyaṃ” (An extra bowl may be kept for a maximum of ten days. For one who exceeds that, there is an offence of Nissaggiya Pacittiya), a bhikkhu who keeps an extra bowl for more than ten days incurs a Nissaggiya Pacittiya offence. Therefore, a bowl must either be formally resolved or formally assigned. An extra bowl is one that has not been so treated. A bowl that should be formally resolved as a bowl is one that is neither too small nor too large, but is of a suitable size for use and has been properly finished and colored. A bowl that does not meet these criteria incurs no offence if used as a mere utensil.

A bowl received at Higher Ordination must be formally resolved within ten days. If, while one bowl is formally resolved, a new bowl needs to be formally resolved, since a bhikkhu cannot keep two bowls, the old bowl must either be given to another or formally withdrawn. When formally withdrawing a bowl, if the bowl is nearby, take it in hand or touch it and say “Imaṃ pattaṃ paccuddharāmi” (I formally withdraw this bowl) three times. If the bowl is far away, considering its location, say “Etaṃ pattaṃ paccuddharāmi” (I formally withdraw that bowl) three times. When formally resolving a bowl, if the bowl is nearby, say “Imaṃ pattaṃ adhiṭṭhāmi” (I formally resolve this bowl) three times, and if it is far away, considering its location, say “Etaṃ pattaṃ adhiṭṭhāmi” (I formally resolve that bowl) three times.

Breaking the Formal Resolve

“Accheda dāna gāhehi-vibbhamā maraṇuḍḍhaṭā

Liṅga sikkhāhi chiddesu-pattodhiṭṭhāna mujjhati.”

As indicated by the above, the formal resolve of a bowl ceases due to nine reasons: seizure by thieves, giving to others, taking by trusted persons, abandoning the robes, death, formal withdrawal, change of gender, rejection of Higher Ordination, and becoming so holed that rice grains fall out. If the formal resolve is broken due to a hole, it must be repaired and formally resolved again. If not done so, a Nissaggiya Pacittiya offence is incurred after ten days.

Formal Assignment of a Bowl

If a bhikkhu who has formally resolved one bowl receives other bowls and wishes to keep them in his possession, he must formally assign them. If he keeps them without doing so, a Nissaggiya Pacittiya offence is incurred after ten days. Formal assignment should be done as follows: Take the bowl to a competent bhikkhu, squat, and say the phrase “Imaṃ pattaṃ tuyhaṃ vikappemi” (I formally assign this bowl to you) three times, and give it to that bhikkhu. Formal assignment is not a gift or an offering, but a Monastic Procedure. Therefore, that bhikkhu must return it, saying the phrase “Mayhaṃ santakaṃ paribhuñja vā vissajjehi vā yathāpaccayaṃ karohi” (Use what belongs to me, or dispose of it, or do as you deem fit) three times. This act is called formal return (paccuddhāra).

After the formal return, keeping and using that bowl for as long as desired incurs no offence. When formally assigning multiple bowls, one should say “Ime patte tuyhaṃ vikappemi” (I formally assign these bowls to you), and during the formal return, “Mayhaṃ santake paribhūñja vā vissajjehi vā yathā paccayaṃ karohi” (Use what belongs to me, or dispose of them, or do as you deem fit). If the bowl is not nearby for formal assignment, tell the other bhikkhu where your bowl is, and considering its location, say “Etaṃ pattaṃ tuyhaṃ vikappemi” (I formally assign that bowl to you), or if there are many bowls, “Ete patte tuyhaṃ vikappemi” (I formally assign those bowls to you).

Size of the Bowl

“Tayo pattassa vaṇṇā ukkaṭṭho patto majjhimo patto omako patto. Ukkaṭṭho nāma patto aḍḍhāḷhako danaṃ gaṇhāti. Catubhāgaṃ khādanaṃ tadupiyaṃ ca vyañjanaṃ. Majjhimo nāma patto nālikodanaṃ gaṇhāti. Catubhāgaṃ khādanaṃ tadupiyaṃ ca vyañjanaṃ. Omako nāma patto patthodanaṃ gaṇhāti. Catubhāgaṃ khādanaṃ tadupiyaṃ ca vyañjanaṃ” This is a passage from the Pārājikāpāḷi that describes the size of a bowl.

This Monastic Procedures discourse describes three types of bowls: the excellent bowl, the medium bowl, and the inferior bowl. The excellent bowl is one that can hold all of the following: rice well-cooked from two nāḷis of rice (a measure used in Magadha at that time), a mung bean curry cooked to a consistency that can be taken by hand, approximately one-fourth the amount of the rice, and other curries suitable for that rice. The medium bowl is one that can hold all of the following: rice cooked from one nāḷi of rice from Magadha, a mung bean curry one-fourth the amount of that rice, and other curries sufficient for that rice. The inferior bowl is one that can hold all of the following: rice cooked from half a nāḷi of rice from Magadha, a mung bean curry one-fourth the amount of that, and other curries.

If, when two nāḷis of rice from Magadha, one-fourth of that amount of mung bean curry, and other curries are placed in a bowl, the contents remain below the lower rim of the bowl, that bowl is too large for an excellent bowl and is therefore an unsuitable bowl. A bowl in which the prescribed amount of food for an inferior bowl overflows the rim is too small and thus unsuitable for formal resolve; it is also an unsuitable bowl. In the Vinaya commentary, nine types of bowls are mentioned: excellent-excellent, excellent, excellent-inferior, medium-excellent, medium, medium-inferior, inferior-excellent, inferior, and inferior-inferior. Of these, the excellent-excellent bowl and the inferior-inferior bowl are stated to be unsuitable bowls, while the remaining seven are suitable for formal resolve.

There are various opinions regarding the Magadha nāḷi. It can generally be considered to be about the size of the *seruva* currently in use here.

It should also be noted that, apart from an iron bowl and a clay bowl, as declared: “Anujānāmi bhikkhave! Dve patte ayopattaṃ mattikāpattaṃ” (Bhikkhus, I allow two bowls: an iron bowl, a clay bowl), any other type of bowl is unsuitable for formal resolve.
4-1.html.prevLabel: Methods of Formal Resolve and Formal Assignment

4-2.html.title: Adhiṭṭhāna of Robes
4-2.html.body:
“Niṭṭhitacīvarasmiṃ pana bhikkhunā ubbhatasmiṃ kaṭhine dasāhaparamṃ atireka cīvaraṃ dhāretabbaṃ. Taṃ atikkāmayato nissaggiyaṃ pācittiyaṃ” is a Vinaya rule; therefore, apart from special times when one is free to keep robes without making an adhiṭṭhāna or vikappana, if an extra robe is kept for more than ten days on any other day, it entails a Nissaggiya Pācittiya. An 'extra robe' refers to robes for which an adhiṭṭhāna or vikappana has not been made. There are nine kinds of robes: the saṅghāṭi, the uttarāsaṅga, the antaravāsaka, the vassikasāṭika (rain-cloth), the nisīdana (sitting-cloth), the pacchattharaṇa (covering-cloth), the kaṇḍupaṭicchādika (anti-itch cloth), the mukhapuñchana-coḷaka (mouth-wiping cloth), and the parikkhāra-coḷa (requisite-cloth). Of these, the saṅghāṭi, uttarāsaṅga, and antaravāsaka are the principal ones.

The saṅghāṭi is the double-layered robe, the uttarāsaṅga is the single-layered robe, and the antaravāsaka is the lower robe. Before making an adhiṭṭhāna for these, they must be dyed and marked with a kappabindu. The kappabindu should be placed on all four corners of the robe, or on three, or on two, or on one corner, in blue color, about the size of a peacock's eye. For the saṅghāṭi, one should resolve, “Imaṃ saṅghāṭiṃ adhiṭṭhāmi”; for the uttarāsaṅga, “Imaṃ uttarāsaṅgaṃ adhiṭṭhāmi”; and for the antaravāsaka, “Imaṃ antaravāsakaṃ adhiṭṭhāmi”. If one makes the adhiṭṭhāna while they are outside one's reach, one should consider their location and resolve: Etaṃ saṅghāṭiṃ adhiṭṭhāmi, etaṃ uttarāsaṅgaṃ adhiṭṭhāmi, etaṃ antaravāsakaṃ adhiṭṭhāmi.

Sizes of Robes

Robes that are either smaller or larger than the prescribed size cannot be designated as saṅghāṭi, uttarāsaṅga, or antaravāsaka. According to the final section, the saṅghāṭi and uttarāsaṅga, measured by the hand of the bhikkhu making the adhiṭṭhāna, should be four cubits and one short cubit in length, and two cubits and one short cubit in width. The antaravāsaka should be four cubits and one short cubit in length, and two cubits in width. If they are smaller than these sizes, they cannot be used for the adhiṭṭhāna of the three robes (ticīvarādhiṭṭhāna). There is no fault if they are larger. However, every robe must be smaller than the Sugata-cīvara (the robe of the Fortunate One).

Breaking the Adhiṭṭhāna of Robes

The ticīvarādhiṭṭhāna is also broken by the nine reasons stated for breaking the adhiṭṭhāna of the bowl. The adhiṭṭhāna is broken by a hole occurring in the saṅghāṭi and uttarāsaṅga, within a span from both ends of the length, and within eight inches from both ends of the width, if the hole is about the size of a little finger's nail. For the antaravāsaka, it is broken by a hole of the stated size occurring within a span from both ends of the length, and within four inches from both ends of the width. If not even a single thread remains in the middle of the hole, the adhiṭṭhāna is not broken. A robe whose adhiṭṭhāna has thus been broken must be mended and repaired, and then resolved again within ten days.

4-3.html.title: Adhiṭṭhāna of Supplementary Robes
4-3.html.body:
A bhikkhu may only make adhiṭṭhāna of one robe of each type, namely saṅghāṭi, uttarāsaṅga, and antaravāsaka, and not two or three of the same type. Therefore, those who wish to use more robes should make adhiṭṭhāna of them as supplementary robes (parikkhāracola). When making adhiṭṭhāna of a single robe, you should say “imaṃ cīvaraṃ parikkhāra coḷaṃ adhiṭṭhāmi” three times and make adhiṭṭhāna. If there are more than one robe, gather them all together and you should say “imāni cīvarāni parikkhāra coḷāni adhiṭṭhāmi”. When making adhiṭṭhāna of robes that are outside your reach, you should say “etaṃ cīvaraṃ parikkhāra coḷaṃ adhiṭṭhāmi” or “etāni cīvarāni parikkhāra coḷāni adhiṭṭhāmi”.

“Niṭṭhitacīvarasmiṃ pana bhikkhunā ubbhatasmiṃ kaṭhine, ekarattaṃ pi ce bhikkhu ticīvarena vippavaseyya aññatra bhikkhu sammutiyā. Nissaggiyaṃ pācittiyaṃ”

For bhikkhus who find it difficult to carry and use the three robes wherever they go, so as not to incur an offense by the training rule stated above, there is no fault in not making adhiṭṭhāna of any of them by the names saṅghāṭi, uttarāsaṅga, or antaravāsaka, but rather making adhiṭṭhāna of all of them solely by the name of supplementary robes (parikkhāracola). Currently, many bhikkhus use robes in this manner.

Excluding cloths that are requisites for the dwelling, such as pillowcases and bedsheets, if any type of cloth that is not smaller than a cubit in length and a span in width is kept for more than ten days out of season without being made adhiṭṭhāna of or vikappana, it incurs a Nissaggiya Pācittiya offense. Therefore, one should accustom oneself to making adhiṭṭhāna of or vikappana of all pouches, handkerchiefs, shoulder-cloths, and any other pieces of cloth received, as ‘supplementary robes’ (parikkhāracola).

4-4.html.title: Robe Designation by Proxy
4-4.html.body:
Take the robe to be designated by proxy, sit squatting near a bhikkhu, and saying “imaṃ cīvaraṃ tuyhaṃ vikappemi” three times, give the robe to the other bhikkhu. Doing so is designation by proxy. The bhikkhu who has received it should say “mayhaṃ santakaṃ paribhuñja vā vissajjehi vā yathāpaccayaṃ karohi” three times and return it. That is the withdrawal (paccuddhāra). When there is more than one robe, you should say “imāni cīvarāni tuyhaṃ vikappemi”. During the withdrawal, you should say “mayhaṃ santakāni paribhuñja vā vissajjehi vā yathāpaccayaṃ karohi”. When designating by proxy robes that are outside one's reach, you should state their location and say “etaṃ cīvaraṃ tuyhaṃ vikappemi”, and if there are many robes, you should say “etāni cīvarāni tuyhaṃ vikappemi”. The withdrawal is as before.

There is no offense if pillowcases, bedsheets, etc., are not designated. When designating a robe as a supplementary cloth (parikkhāra-coḷa), if it is one, you should designate it by saying “imaṃ cīvaraṃ parikkhāracolaṃ adhiṭṭhāmi”, and if there are many, by saying “imāni cīvarāni parikkhāracolāni adhiṭṭhāmi”. When designating robes that are outside one's reach, you should designate them by saying “etaṃ cīvaraṃ parikkhāra-colaṃ adhiṭṭhāmi”, and “etāni cīvarāni parikkhāracolāni adhiṭṭhāmi”.

4.html.title: Adhiṭṭhāna and Vikappana Procedures
4.html.body:
Sub-topics

  • Bowl Adhiṭṭhāna
  • Robe Adhiṭṭhāna
  • Accessory Cloth Adhiṭṭhāna
  • Robe Vikappana

5-1.html.title: Confession of Common Offences
5-1.html.body:
“Na bhikkhave! sabhāgāpatti desetabbā. Yo desey‍ya āpatti dukkaṭassa. Na bhikkhave! sabhāgāpatti paṭiggahetabbā. Yo paṭiggaṇhey‍ya āpatti dukkaṭassa.”

According to these Buddha-provisions, an offence of wrong-doing is incurred by confessing or accepting a common offence. A “common offence” refers to an offence that both the confessor and the recipient have incurred. If a bhikkhu who has fallen into a certain offence, such as eating at the wrong time, confesses that same offence in the presence of a bhikkhu who has also fallen into that same offence, it is a confession of a common offence. If a bhikkhu who has fallen into the offence of eating at the wrong time confesses that offence along with other offences in the presence of a bhikkhu who has also fallen into the offence of eating at the wrong time, saying: “Ahaṃ bhante! sambahulā āpattiyo āpajjiṃ. Tā tumha mūle paṭidesemi”, he becomes pure from all the confessed offences.

It is not that he does not become pure from those offences because of the rule that a common offence should not be confessed. Rather, what occurs is that he becomes pure from the previous offences but incurs a new offence of wrong-doing. If the bhikkhu who accepted those offences subsequently confesses his own offence of eating at the wrong time, the offence incurred by accepting a common offence, and other offences to that same first bhikkhu, saying: “Sambahulā āpattiyo āpajjiṃ tā tumhamūle paṭidesemi”, the first bhikkhu—having already been purified of the offence of eating at the wrong time—does not fall into the fault of confessing a common offence. By confessing in this manner, that bhikkhu becomes pure from all offences remediable by confession. The first bhikkhu can then confess the offence incurred by the confession of a common offence to the second bhikkhu and become pure. Therefore, when confessing to one another, if there are common offences, the bhikkhu who confessed first must confess once more.
5-1.html.nextLabel: Formulae for the Confession of Offences

5-2.html.title: Formulations Used for Confessing Offenses
5-2.html.body:
First, the junior bhikkhu should confess his offenses.

Junior Bhikkhu

-

Ahaṃ bhante! Sabbāpattiyo āvikaromi.

(This is the declaration of grave offenses.)

Senior Bhikkhu

-

Sādhu. Sādhu!

Junior Bhikkhu

-

Ahaṃ bhante! Sambahulā āpattiyo āpajjiṃ. Tā tumhamūle paṭidesemi.

Senior Bhikkhu

-

Passasi āvuso! Tā āpattiyo?

Junior Bhikkhu

-

Āma bhante! Passāmi.

Senior Bhikkhu

-

Āyatiṃ āvuso! Saṃvareyyāsi.

Junior Bhikkhu

-

Sādhu suṭṭhu bhante! Saṃvarissāmi

(Confession of offenses by the Senior Bhikkhu)

Senior Bhikkhu

-

Ahaṃ āvuso! Sabbāpattiyo āvikaromi.

Junior Bhikkhu

-

Sādhu bhante! Sādhu.

Senior Bhikkhu

-

Ahaṃ āvuso! Sambahulā āpattiyo āpajjiṃ. Tā tuyhamūle paṭidesemi.

Junior Bhikkhu

-

Passatha bhante! Tā āpattiyo?

Senior Bhikkhu

-

Āma āvuso! Passāmi.

Junior Bhikkhu

-

Āyatiṃ bhante! Saṃvareyyātha.

Senior Bhikkhu

-

Sādhu suṭṭhu ahaṃ āvuso! Saṃvarissāmi.

Junior Bhikkhu

-

Sādhu bhante! Sādhu.

When offenses have been confessed to each other in this manner, a dukkata offense remains for the bhikkhu who confessed first, due to confessing a sabhāgāpatti (an offense shared in common). If there are many sabhāgāpatti, a dukkata offense may arise for each such offense. Therefore, one should confess a second time in this manner.

Junior Bhikkhu

-

Ahaṃ bhante! Sambahulā āpattiyo āpajjiṃ. Tā tumhamūle paṭidesemi.

Senior Bhikkhu

-

Passasi āvuso! Tā āpattiyo?

Junior Bhikkhu

-

Āma bhante! Passāmi.

Senior Bhikkhu

-

Āyatiṃ āvuso! Saṃvareyyāsi.

Junior Bhikkhu

-

Sādhu suṭṭhu bhante! Saṃvarissāmi.

Senior Bhikkhu

-

Sādhu Sādhu.

Since the declaration of grave offenses was made during the first confession, there is no benefit in repeating it during the second confession.

5-3.html.title: Relinquishing Nissaggiya.
5-3.html.body:
If there are bowls, robes, etc., that have become nissaggiya due to not being determined (adhiṭṭhāna) or not being made use of (vikappa), purity is not attained even if the offenses are declared without relinquishing them. Therefore, in order to purify oneself from those offenses, one must relinquish the nissaggiya object and declare the offense. A bhikkhu who uses nissaggiya robes, etc., without relinquishing them incurs a dukkata offense each time he uses them. It should be understood that relinquishing is not a gift or an offering, but a Monastic Procedure. A nissaggiya object can be relinquished to the Community of monks, to a group (gaṇa), or to an individual. Since relinquishing to an individual is easier, only the method of relinquishing to an individual is presented here.

Take a robe that has become nissaggiya by exceeding ten days without being determined or made use of to one bhikkhu, sit squatting, take the robe in hand, and say the statement: “Idaṃ me bhante! Cīvaraṃ dasāhātikkantaṃ nissaggiyaṃ. Imāhaṃ āyasmato nissajāmi”, then give the robe and declare the offense. After the offense is declared, the bhikkhu who received it should return it, saying: “Imaṃ cīvaraṃ āyasmato dammi”. If there are many robes, one should say: “Imāni me bhante cīvarāni dasāhātikkantāni. Nissaggiyāni imānāhaṃ āyasmato nissajāmi”. When returning them, one should say: “Imāni cīvarāni āyasmato dammi”.

When relinquishing robes that are outside one's reach, one should say: “Etaṃ me āvuso! Cīvaraṃ dasāhātikkantaṃ nissaggiyaṃ, etāhaṃ āyasmato nissajāmi”. When returning it, one should say: “Etaṃ cīvaraṃ āyasmato dammi”. If there is more than one robe, one should say: “Etāni me bhante! Cīvarāni dasāhātikkantāni nissaggiyāni” and “Etānāhaṃ āyasmato nissajāmi”, and “Etāni cīvarāni āyasmato dammi”.

A robe that has become nissaggiya by being separated overnight (rattivitvuttha) should be relinquished by saying: “Idamme bhante! Cīvaraṃ ratti vippavutthaṃ aññatra bhikkhu sammutiyā nissaggiyaṃ. Imāhaṃ āyasmato nissajāmi”.

An extra bowl kept for more than ten days should be relinquished by saying: “Ayamme patto dasāhātikkanto nissaggiyo. Imāhaṃ āyasmato nissajāmi”. The method for relinquishing other nissaggiya objects should be looked up in the Vinaya books. These can be easily found in the Kudusika.

5.html.title: Confession of Offences
5.html.body:
‘Confession of Offences’ means the declaration for the purpose of purification from offences. Not appearing as a bhikkhu, abandoning bhikkhuhood and becoming a novice or a layperson, is the confession of a pārājikāpatti. Completing the vatta and mānatta and performing the abbhāna procedure is the confession of a saṅghādisesāpatti. By confessing to one bhikkhu, one can be purified from offences such as thullaccaya and pācittiya. Here, ‘Confession of Offences’ refers to the confession of offences such as thullaccaya. Because the training rules for bhikkhus are very numerous, and because there are also rules that can be transgressed unintentionally, bhikkhus often incur offences. Sometimes they incur offences even without knowing it.

One can easily incur the two grave offences, sukkhavissaṭṭhi and kāyasaṃsagga. When performing Monastic Procedures for purification from a grave offence, if the incurred offence has not been declared to a bhikkhu, one must undergo parivāsa for the number of days that have passed. A bhikkhu who declared the offence to another bhikkhu on the very day it was incurred can be purified by completing the mānatta for six days and performing the abbhāna procedure. It is good to declare grave offences on all occasions of confessing offences, both to avoid having to undergo vatta for grave offences and to reduce the number of days for completing the vatta. When doing so, if one definitively knows the grave offence one has incurred, it is good to declare it by naming that specific offence. By one who does not definitively know how many grave offences one has incurred, with the intention of ‘I declare all the offences I have incurred’—

one should first declare the offences by saying either “Sabbāpattiyo āvikaromi” or “Sabbāpattiyo ārocayāmi”, and then proceed with the confession of other offences.

Sub-topics

  • Confession of Shared Offences
  • Phrases Used in Confessing Offences
  • Confession of Nissaya.

6-1.html.title: The Nine Uposathas.
6-1.html.body:
There are three Uposathas according to the days: the Cātuddasī Uposatha, the Paṇṇarasī Uposatha, and the Sāmaggī Uposatha.

There are three Uposathas according to the individuals performing the act: the Saṅgha Uposatha, the Gaṇa Uposatha, and the Puggala Uposatha.

There are three Uposathas according to the manner of performance: the Suttuddesa Uposatha, the Pārisuddhi Uposatha, and the Adhiṭṭhāna Uposatha. Thus, there are nine Uposathas.

Among the nine kinds of Uposathas, the "Cātuddasī Uposatha" is the Uposatha performed on the fourteenth day of the fortnight. It is performed in the third and seventh fortnights of the seasons (which do not have fifteen days). The 'Paṇṇarasī Uposatha' is the Uposatha performed on the fifteenth day of the fortnight. The 'Sāmaggī Uposatha' is the Uposatha performed on the day the divided Community of monks was reconciled, commemorating that reconciliation. The 'Saṅgha Uposatha' is the Uposatha performed by four or more bhikkhus. The 'Gaṇa Uposatha' is the Uposatha performed by three bhikkhus. The 'Puggala Uposatha' is the Uposatha performed by two bhikkhus or one bhikkhu. The 'Suttuddesa Uposatha' is the Uposatha performed by way of reciting the Pātimokkha. The 'Pārisuddhi Uposatha' is the Uposatha performed by way of declaring one's purity.
6-1.html.nextLabel: Matters to be known concerning the Saṅgha Uposatha

6-2.html.title: Matters to be known concerning the Community's Uposatha
6-2.html.body: Since it has been declared: “Na bhikkhave! Saṅghamajjhe anajjhiṭṭhena pātimokkhaṃ uddisitabbaṃ. Yo uddiseyya. Āpatti dukkaṭassa”, after the Community of monks has assembled within the sīmā, for the recitation of the Pātimokkha, an invitation should be made by a bhikkhu who has received Authorization, either to the senior thera of the Community or to another thera skilled in Vinaya. The thera who has received the invitation, before reciting the Pātimokkha, should question the bhikkhu who made the invitation concerning the Vinaya. This too should be done after obtaining Authorization. The bhikkhu who made the invitation should also resolve the Vinaya questions after obtaining Authorization. 'Authorization to Question the Vinaya and Authorization to Answer' can also be obtained by oneself. Obtaining it by oneself is the current custom. The method of making invitations, etc., will be shown below. Therein, the sentences will be presented using the names Tissa, Datta. When performing the Uposatha, the names of the respective individuals should be used in those places.
6-2.html.nextLabel: The Method of Performing the Community's Uposatha

6-3.html.title: Method for Performing the Sangha Uposatha
6-3.html.body:
Making the Invitation

“Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho. Yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ ahaṃ āyasmantaṃ (tissaṃ) pātimokkhuddesaṃ ajjhesey‍yaṃ.” Having obtained the authorization in this manner, make the invitation by reciting the following sentence three times:

Saṅgho bhante! āyasmantaṃ (tissaṃ) pātimokkhuddesaṃ ajjhesati. Uddisatu āyasmā (tisso) pātimokkhaṃ.

Giving the Response

Āma uddisissāmahaṃ āvuso.

Authorization of the Questioner of the Discipline

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho. Yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ. Ahaṃ āyasmantaṃ (dattaṃ) vinayaṃ pucchey‍yaṃ.

Authorization of the Answerer

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho. Yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ. Ahaṃ āyasmatā (tissena) vinayaṃ puṭṭho vissajjey‍yaṃ.

Interrogating the Discipline

“Sammajjanī padipo ca

Udakaṃ āsanena ca

Uposathassa etāni

Pubbakaraṇanti vuccati”

Have the four preliminary duties to be performed before the Community assembles in the Uposatha hall—namely, sweeping the Uposatha hall, lighting the lamps, setting out the water, and preparing the seats—been completed?

Answer: Since there is currently sunlight, there is no need to light the lamps. The other three duties have been completed.

“Chandapārisuddhi utukkhānaṃ

Bhikkhu gaṇanā ca ovādo

Uposathassa etāni

Pubbakiccanti vuccati”

In order to complete the preliminary obligations as mentioned, has the conveyance of the consent and purity (chanda-pārisuddhi) been announced to the Community?

Answer: Since all the bhikkhus within the boundary are assembled here, there is no conveyance of consent and purity.

Question: The seasons are three: the cold season, the hot season, and the rainy season. Of these, which is the current season?

Give the answer.

Question: In each season there are eight fortnights. In this current season, how many fortnights have passed? How many fortnights remain? Which fortnight is this current one?

Give the answer.

Question: How many bhikkhus are currently assembled within this boundary?

State the number.

Question: Has the request made by the bhikkhunīs for the sake of receiving exhortation been announced to the Community?

Answer: Since there are no bhikkhunīs at this time, there is no such request.

Question: These four are called preliminary obligations because they must be performed after the Community has assembled within the boundary but before the recitation of the Pātimokkha.

“Uposatho yāvatikā ca bhikkhu

Kammappattā sabhāgāpattiyo ca

Na vijjanti vajjanīyā ca puggalā

Tasmiṃ na honti pattakallanti vuccati”

As declared by the Commentators, the appropriate time for the Uposatha procedure must be endowed with four factors. Although the Uposatha is one by general characteristic, it is threefold by way of days: the fourteenth, the fifteenth, and the day of reconciliation (sāmaggī); threefold by way of participating individuals: the Community, a group, and an individual; and threefold by way of the manner of performance: recitation of the Suttas (suttuddesa), declaration of purity (pārisuddhi), and determination (adhiṭṭhāna). Of these, what kind of Uposatha is this being performed, in terms of the day?

Give the answer.

Question: What kind of Uposatha is it in terms of the participating individuals?

Give the answer.

Question: What kind of Uposatha is it in terms of the manner of performance?

Give the answer.

Question: When it is said to be a 'recitation of the Suttas' (suttuddesa) Uposatha, a group of no fewer than four bhikkhus must remain within one boundary without leaving the proximity (hatthapāsa). Are that many bhikkhus present here?

Answer, "Yes."

Question: Are the assembled bhikkhus free from common offenses (sabhāgāpatti)?

Give the answer.

Question: Have the twenty-one types of excluded persons, such as laypeople, been removed from the proximity of the Community?

Answer, "Yes."

Then, the Elder reciting the Pātimokkha should speak as follows:

This procedure endowed with these four factors is called 'Timely'. Pubbakaraṇa pubbakiccāni samāpetvā desitāpattikassa samaggassa bhikkhusaṅghassa anumatiyā pātimokkhaṃ uddisituṃ ārādhanaṃ karoma.

When this is said, the Community should give the Sadhu-exclamation. Then, having stated the Formal Statement (ñatti), the Pātimokkha should be recited.

A Special Note

“Pubbakaraṇa pubbakiccāni samāpetvā” —and so forth—is a passage found in the Pātimokkha, but it is not seen in the Vinaya Pali or the Commentaries. Even in the Kaṅkhāvitaraṇī Commentary, which explains the Pātimokkha, there is no mention of this passage. From this, it is to be understood that this passage was included in the Pātimokkha at a later period. The first part of it is found in various manuscripts as “Pubbakaraṇa pubbakiccāni samāpetvā” and “Samādapetvā”.

The meaning of “Pātimokkhaṃ uddisituṃ ārādhanaṃ karoma” is misunderstood by many. It is not something said by the bhikkhu who invites the recitation of the Pātimokkha, but rather by the Elder who is actually reciting the Pātimokkha. Therefore, the meaning of “Ārādhanaṃ karoma” should be taken as 'I endeavor' to recite the Pātimokkha. It should not be taken as 'we request the recitation of the Pātimokkha'. The author of the Vinayālaṅkāra-ṭīkā expresses a dislike for that passage. The author of the Vinayālaṅkāra-ṭīkā states that one should say: “Pubbakaraṇa pubbakiccāni sammā niṭṭhāpetvā pattakallaṅge samānīte saṅghassa anumatiyā pātimokkhaṃ uddissāma”. It should be noted that however this is said, it does not cause any harm to the Monastic Procedure.
6-3.html.prevLabel: Facts to be known about the Sangha Uposatha

6-4.html.title: Pārisuddhi Uposatha.
6-4.html.body:
The Uposatha performed when a sufficient number of bhikkhus for the aforementioned Sangha Uposatha, or Suttuddesa Uposatha, have not assembled within the sīmā, but only three or two bhikkhus have assembled within the sīmā, is the Pārisuddhi Uposatha. If three bhikkhus have assembled within the sīmā, having performed the preliminary duties and preliminary acts, one bhikkhu shall lay the Motion thus: “Suṇantu me āyasmantā ajjuposatho (paṇṇaraso, cātuddiso) yadāyasmantānaṃ pattakallaṃ mayaṃ aññamaññaṃ pārisuddhi uposathaṃ karey‍yāma”. First, the senior bhikkhu, having arranged his outer robe over one shoulder, sat in the squatting posture, and paid homage, should say three times: “Parisuddho ahaṃ āvuso parisuddhoti maṃ dhāretha”. The remaining two should likewise say three times: “Parisuddho ahaṃ bhante parisuddhoti maṃ dhāretha”.

If two bhikkhus have assembled within the sīmā, having completed all preliminary acts, without laying a Motion, they should declare their purity to each other as stated.

Furthermore, on the day of the Great Pavāraṇā, when bhikkhus who have observed the Rains-residence earlier are performing Pavāraṇā, bhikkhus who have observed the Rains-residence later, and bhikkhus who have not observed the Rains-residence, and bhikkhus whose Rains-residence was broken, should participate in the Pavāraṇā Monastic Procedure by giving their bodily presence, and declare their purity by saying: “Parisuddho ahaṃ bhante parisuddhoti maṃ dhāretha”. When bhikkhus who have observed the Rains-residence later are performing Pavāraṇā, those who observed it earlier, and those who did not observe it, and those whose Rains-residence was broken, should declare their purity as stated. Furthermore, after the recitation of the Pātimokkha has concluded, before those bhikkhus depart from the sīmā, bhikkhus who arrived later should also declare their purity to the bhikkhus who performed the Suttuddesa Uposatha.
6-4.html.prevLabel: Method of Performing the Sangha Uposatha

6-5.html.title: The Adhitthāna Uposatha.
6-5.html.body: If, on the Uposatha day, only one bhikkhu is present, having gone to the sīmā and completed all preliminary duties, and considering that no other bhikkhus will arrive to perform the Uposatha, if it is a fourteenth-day Uposatha, he should recite three times: “Ajja me cātuddaso uposathoti adhiṭṭhāmi”; or if it is a fifteenth-day Uposatha, he should recite three times: “Ajja me uposatho paṇṇarasoti adhiṭṭhāmi”. This is called the Adhitthāna Uposatha.

6-6.html.title: Persons to be Excluded.
6-6.html.body:
A lay person, a bhikkhuni, a sikkhamana, a samanera, a samaneri, one who has rejected the training, one who has fallen into a parajika offence, one on whom the ukkhepaniya kamma (act of suspension) has been performed for not seeing an offence, one on whom the ukkhepaniya kamma has been performed for not making amends for an offence, one on whom the ukkhepaniya kamma has been performed for not giving up a wicked view, a eunuch, one who dwells in stolen communion, one who has gone over to another sect, an animal, a matricide, a parricide, a slayer of an Arahant, one who has violated a bhikkhuni, a schismatic of the Community, one who has drawn blood from a Buddha, and a hermaphrodite — these twenty-one persons are to be excluded from the boundary (atthapasa) during the Observance of Uposatha.

The term ‘atthapasa’ refers to two and a half cubits. Those persons are to be excluded from the boundary (atthapasa) of the Community of monks (Sangha) observing Uposatha. If, among these twenty-one persons, the three suspended ones (ukkhittaka) are present within the boundary (atthapasa) during the Observance of Uposatha, all bhikkhus incur a pacittiya offence. If the others are present during the Observance of Uposatha, a dukkata offence is incurred.

6.html.title: Observing Uposatha
6.html.body:
There are three seasons in a year: Hemanta (winter), Gimhana (summer), and Vassana (rainy). Each season has four months. Each month has thirty days; some months have twenty-nine days. There are two fortnights in a month, called the waxing and waning halves. Each fortnight has fifteen days; some fortnights also have fourteen days. There are twelve months in a year: Il (October-November), Unduvap (November-December), Duruthu (December-January), Navam (January-February), Medin (February-March), Bak (March-April), Vesak (April-May), Poson (May-June), Esala (June-July), Nikini (July-August), Binara (August-September), and Vap (September-October). Each month has two Uposatha days. 'Uposatha' refers to the last day of a fortnight. The division of the twelve months into seasons is as follows.

“Kantikassa ca kālamhā

Yāva egguṇapuṇṇamā

Hemantakālo viññeyyo

Aṭṭha honti uposathā.”

The four months from the first day of the waning half of Il (October-November) up to the full moon of Medin (February-March) constitute the Hemanta season. There are eight Uposatha days in it.

“Egguṇassa ca kālamhā

Yāva āsaḷhapuṇṇamā

Gimhakāloti viññeyyo

Aṭṭha honti uposathā”

The period from the first day of the waning half of Medin (February-March) up to the full moon of Esala (June-July) constitutes the Gimhana season. There are eight Uposatha days in it.

“Āsaḷhassa ca kālamhā

Yāva kattikapuṇṇamā

Vassakāloti viññeyyo

Aṭṭha honti uposathā.”

The four months from the first day of the waning half of Esala (June-July) up to the full moon of Il (October-November) constitute the Vassana season. There are eight Uposatha days in it.

“Utūnaṃ pana tiṇṇaṃ

Pakkhe tatiya sattame

Cātuddasoti pātimokkhaṃ

Uddisanti nayaññuto.”

In all three seasons, the third and seventh Uposatha days are the Cātuddasī (fourteenth-day Uposatha). Out of the twenty-four Uposatha days in a year, six are Cātuddasī, and eighteen are Paṇṇarasī (fifteenth-day Uposatha).

'Observing Uposatha' refers to the proper procedure to be carried out by bhikkhus on the Uposatha day. That is: if four or more bhikkhus are assembled, the recitation of the Pātimokkha; if three or two bhikkhus are present, the declaration of purity; and if only one bhikkhu is present, the determination, "Ajjatagge uposatho" (Today is the Uposatha day).

Sub-topics

  • The Nine Uposathas.
  • Matters to be Known Regarding the Saṅgha Uposatha
  • Method of Performing the Saṅgha Uposatha
  • The Pārisuddhi Uposatha.
  • The Adhiṭṭhāna Uposatha.
  • Persons to be Excluded.

7-1.html.title: Going with a Sattāhakaraniya.
7-1.html.body:
When going forth from the place of Rains Residence with a sattāhakaraniya, one should go having resolved, while still within the boundary of the Rains Residence, "I shall go forth for this reason requiring a sattāhakaraniya, and I shall return within seven days for this reason." If a bhikkhu, having made such a resolve before departing, forgets it at the time of going, there is no fault. The teachers say that for one who goes without making any such resolve whatsoever, the Rains Residence is broken (vassaccheda). The teachers also state that for one who goes forth without any invitation or similar reason, but having resolved, "I shall return within a week," an offense occurs, but the Rains Residence is not broken (vassaccheda).

“Anto upacāra sīmāyaṃ ṭhiteneva sattāha karaṇīya nimittaṃ sallakkhetvā. Iminā nimittena gantvā anto sattāhe āgamissāmīti ābhogaṃ katvā gantuṃ vaṭṭati. Purimakkhaṇe ābhogaṃ katvā gamanakkhaṇe vissaritvā gatepi doso natthi. Sakaraṇīyo pakkamatīti vuttattā sabbathā pana ābhogaṃ akatvā gatassa vassacchedo ti vadanti. Yo pana sattāhakaraniya nimittābhāvepi sattāhabbhantare āgamissāmīti ābhogaṃ katvā gantvā, sattāhabbhantare āgacchati tassa āpattiyeva vassacchedo natthi. Sattāhassa sannivattattāti vadanti. Vīmaṃsitvā gahetabbaṃ” is a passage from the Vimativinodanī Ṭīkā.

A bhikkhu who has gone forth with a sattāhakaraniya must return to the place of Rains Residence before dawn on the seventh day, without exceeding seven days. If he departed from the boundary of the Rains Residence on a Sunday, he must return by Saturday, before the following Sunday dawns. If he remains outside until Sunday, the Rains Residence is broken (vassaccheda). A bhikkhu who has gone forth with a sattāhakaraniya is permitted not to exceed seven days outside, but to return within seven days. If the day of Pavāraṇā happens to be the seventh day, and a bhikkhu who has gone forth with a sattāhakaraniya performs Pavāraṇā somewhere else and does not return to the place of Rains Residence, the Rains Residence is not broken (vassaccheda). This is because for such a bhikkhu, the Rains period does not extend beyond the seventh day.

A Special Point

Pavāraṇā is a Monastic Procedure (vinaya-karma) appointed to be performed on that day, not an ending of the Rains Residence. Since the Uposatha day on which Pavāraṇā is performed also belongs to the Rains period, one should not depart from the place of Rains Residence on that day, thinking, "The Rains Residence has ended with Pavāraṇā." One must reside there on that day as well. Even on that day, if one departs from the place of Rains Residence having abandoned the intention to reside there, the Rains Residence may be broken (vassaccheda).
7-1.html.nextLabel: Reasons for Going with a Sattāhakaraniya.

7-2.html.title: Matters for which one may go with a seven-day allowance.
7-2.html.body:
It does not appear in the Monastic Procedures that one should go with a seven-day allowance for any reason such as pleasure trips, pilgrimage trips, visiting relatives, or visiting friends. “Dhamma savanaṭṭhāya animantitenā gantuṃ na vaṭṭati uddesa paripucchādīnaṃ atthāyāpi gantuṃ na labhati” In the Vinaya commentary, it is stated that one should not go without an invitation even for hearing the Dhamma, learning, or questioning. “Ācariyaṃ passissāmīti gantuṃ labhati” it is stated that one may go to see one's teacher.

Anujānāmi bhikkhave! sattannaṃ sattāhakaraṇīyena appahite’pi gantuṃ, pageva pahite. Bhikkhussa bhikkhuniyā sikkhamānāya sāmaṇerassa sāmaṇeriyā mātuyā ca pitussa ca. Anujānāmi bhikkhave imesaṃ sattannaṃ sattāhakaraṇīyena appahite’pi gantuṃ, pageva pahite. Sattāhaṃ sannivatto kātabbo.
