Thus, the Tathagata has allowed that one may go with a seven-day allowance for these seven: a bhikkhu, a bhikkhunī, a probationer, a novice, a novice nun, one's mother, and one's father, even without an invitation.

“Anujānāmi bhikkhave! Saṅghakaraṇīyena gantuṃ sattāhaṃ sannivatto kātabbo” it has been declared that one may go for a Community-procedure and stay for a week, even without an invitation. A Community-procedure refers to work such as the renovation of monasteries, stupas, or monastic dwellings. This includes finding workers and materials for such tasks. Since every bhikkhu is included in the Community, going for matters concerning bhikkhus' personal dwellings is also considered going for a Community-procedure. For the work of others, apart from the individuals mentioned above, one should go with a seven-day allowance only if there is an invitation. In case of danger, leaving the place where one has entered the Rains-residence, even if it results in the breaking of the Rains-residence, does not incur an offense. Similarly, if one goes to see one's teacher and stays there as per the teacher's instruction, breaking the Rains-residence does not incur an offense.

7.html.title: Entering the Vassa
7.html.body:
There are two types of entering the Vassa: the First Vassa and the Second Vassa. On the full moon day of Esala, which is the eighth Uposatha of the summer season, having observed the Uposatha, one should enter the First Vassa on the first day of the waning moon. On the full moon day of Nikini, which is the second Uposatha of the rainy season, having observed the Uposatha, one should enter the Second Vassa on the first day of the waning moon. For a bhikkhu who does not enter the Vassa on one of these two days, an offense of dukkata is incurred, according to this Buddha-enactment: “Na bhikkhave! Vassam na upagantabbam, yo na upagaccheyya apatti dukkatassa.”

On the day of entering the Vassa, having tidied the monastery where one will reside for the Vassa, in the evening, having offered flowers and lamps, paid homage to the Triple Gem, recited paritta, and transferred merit to the devas, one should enter the Vassa by saying “Imasmim vihare imam temasam vassam upemi” once, twice, or thrice.

If a bhikkhu residing in a certain monastery with the intention of "I will reside here for the Vassa" forgets to declare the Vassa by saying the phrase “Imasmim vihare imam temasam vassam upemi” due to being engaged in activities such as teaching Dhamma, the entering of the Vassa is still accomplished because the intention to enter the Vassa exists. If he resides without breaking the Vassa, there is no impediment for that bhikkhu to perform the Pavarana.

As it has been declared “Na bhikkhave! Asenasanikena vassam upa gantabbam. Yo upagaccheyya apatti dukkatassa,” one must enter the Vassa only after finding a dwelling with a roof and a door. If there is no dwelling for entering the Vassa, and no supporter to have one built, then one must find helpers and have a dwelling built. If helpers cannot be found, then one must build it oneself. One cannot avoid offense by not entering the Vassa on the pretext of not having a dwelling.

Sub-topics

  • Going on Account of a Seven-Day Allowance.
  • Reasons for Going on Account of a Seven-Day Allowance.
7.html.nextLabel: Going on Account of a Seven-Day Allowance.

8-1.html.title: Three-Time Sangha Pavāraṇā
8-1.html.body:
After the Community has assembled within the sīmā, one bhikkhu shall obtain the Authorization for the Requestor with the statement “Suṇātu me bhante saṅgho. Yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ. Ahaṃ āyasmantaṃ (tissaṃ) pavāraṇā ñattiṭhapanaṃ ajjhesey‍yaṃ”, and then, saying “Saṅgho bhante āyasmantaṃ (tissaṃ) pavāraṇā ñattiṭhapanaṃ ajjhesati. Ṭhapetu bhante āyasmā (tisso) pavāraṇā ñattiṃ” three times, invite the Sangha Thera or another learned elder to lay down the Pavāraṇā Motion.

Thereupon, the invited elder shall reply “Āma ṭhapemi āvuso” and, saying the statement “Suṇātu me bhante saṅgho. Yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ ahaṃ āyasmantaṃ (nāgaṃ) vinayaṭa pucchey‍yaṃ”, lay down the Motion for questioning on Monastic Procedures.

The bhikkhu who made the invitation shall lay down the Motion for answering on Monastic Procedures with “Suṇātu me bhante saṅgho. Yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ ahaṃ āyasmatā (tissena) vinayaṭa puṭṭho vissajjey‍yaṃ”. Thereupon, the questioning and answering on Monastic Procedures shall be carried out as follows.

Question:-

“Sammajjanī padīpo ca - udakaṃ āsanena ca

Pavāraṇāya etāni - pubbakaraṇanti vuccati”

As declared by the Commentarial Masters, are these four preliminary duties—sweeping, lighting lamps, providing drinking water and water for use, and arranging seats—which must be done before the Community assembles for the Pavāraṇā, completed?

Answer:- As there is sunlight now, lighting lamps is unnecessary. The rest are completed.

Q: “Chandapavāraṇā utukkhānaṃ - bhikkhu gaṇanā ca ovādo

pavāraṇā ya etāni - pubbakiccanti vuccati”.

As declared by the Commentarial Masters, have the brought-in declarations of consent (chandapavāraṇā) been made known to the Community, and are they completed?

A: As all bhikkhus within the sīmā are assembled here, maintaining physical presence without deficiency, there is no need to bring in declarations of purity (chandapārisuddhi).

Q: The seasons are threefold: winter, summer, and rainy season. Among these seasons, which season has now arrived?

A: The season that has now arrived is the rainy season.

Q: Each season has eight fortnights. How many fortnights have passed in this current rainy season? How many fortnights remain? Which fortnight is this current fortnight?

A: Five fortnights have passed in this rainy season. Two fortnights remain. The current fortnight is the sixth fortnight.

Q: What is the number of bhikkhus now assembled within this sīmā?

Provide the answer.

Q: Has the request made by the bhikkhunīs for receiving admonition been made known to the Community and completed?

A: As there are no bhikkhunīs at this time, there is no such request.

Q:

“Pavāraṇā yāvatikā ca bhikkhu

kammappattā sabhāgāpattiyo ca

na vijjanti vajjanīyā ca puggalā

tasmiṃ na honti pattakallanti vuccati”

As declared by the Commentarial Masters, although Pavāraṇā is generally one, it is ninefold in terms of specific characteristics: three by day, namely Cātuddasī, Paṇṇarasī, Sāmaggī; three by performing individuals, namely Saṅgha, Gaṇa, Puggala; and three by mode of performance, namely Tevācika, Dvevācika, Ekavācika. Which Pavāraṇā, by day, is this Pavāraṇā that is now to be performed?

A:

It is the Paṇṇarasī Pavāraṇā.

Q:

Which Pavāraṇā, by performing individuals, is it?

A:

It is the Saṅgha Pavāraṇā.

Q:

Which Pavāraṇā, by mode of performance, is it?

A:

It is the Tevācika Pavāraṇā.

Q:

When it is said Tevācika Pavāraṇā, a minimum of five bhikkhus or more are required for it. Are that many bhikkhus now assembled here?

A:

Yes, they are.

Q:

Are all bhikkhus free from similar offenses (sabhāgāpatti)?

A:

The bhikkhus have purified themselves by confessing their offenses to one another.

Q:

Have the twenty-one objectionable individuals been excluded from the Community's presence and is this completed?

A:

Yes, they have.

Thereupon, the elder who questions on Monastic Procedures shall say: “Pubbakaraṇa pubbakiccāni samāpetvā desitāpattikassa samaggassa bhikkhusaṅghassa anumatiyā pavāraṇā ñattiṃ ṭhapetuṃ ārādhanā karoma”. The bhikkhus assembled shall respond to this by giving their assent (sādhukāra).

8-2.html.title: The Motion for Pavāraṇā
8-2.html.body:
“Suṇātu me bhante saṅgho. Ajja pavāraṇā paṇṇarasī. Yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ, saṅgho tevācikaṃ pavārey‍ya.”

After the Motion has been laid thus, the senior bhikkhu of the Community, having arranged his robe over one shoulder, sitting in the squatting posture, and having paid homage, [should say]:-

“Saṅghaṃ avuso pavāremi. Diṭṭhena vā sutena vā parisaṃkāya vā vadantu maṃ Āyasmanto anukampaṃ upādāya passanto paṭikarissāmi. Dutiyampi āvuso saṅghaṃ pavāremi -pe- tatiyampi āvuso saṅghaṃ pavāremi -pe- passanto paṭikarissāmi.”

Pavāraṇā should be performed thus. When the senior bhikkhu of the Community performs Pavāraṇā, the others should not sit on their seats, but should sit in the squatting posture. After the Great Elder has performed Pavāraṇā, the remaining bhikkhus should perform Pavāraṇā in order of seniority. The junior bhikkhus should remain in the squatting posture until the senior bhikkhus have performed Pavāraṇā. After each has performed his own Pavāraṇā, it is permissible to sit on one's seat. The newly ordained bhikkhus should perform Pavāraṇā thus:

Saṅghaṃ bhante pavāremi. Diṭṭhena vā sutena vā parisaṃkāya vā vadantu maṃ āyasmanto anukampaṃ upādāya passanto paṭikarissāmi. Dutiyampi bhante saṅghaṃ -pe- tatiyampi bhante saṅghaṃ -pe- paṭikarissāmi.

Among the Community performing Pavāraṇā, if there are bhikkhus who have observed the Rains Retreat later, or who have not observed the Rains Retreat, or whose Rains Retreat has been broken, they should announce their purity. If there are bhikkhus who observed the Rains Retreat earlier at a place where bhikkhus who observed the Rains Retreat later are performing Pavāraṇā, they too should announce their purity.

On the day of the Great Pavāraṇā, if among the bhikkhus assembled within a sīmā, those who observed the Rains Retreat later are numerous, the Motion for Pavāraṇā should not be laid, but the Pātimokkha should be recited. Thereafter, without laying a Motion, those who observed the Rains Retreat earlier should perform Pavāraṇā to those bhikkhus. If both groups are equal in number, the Motion for Pavāraṇā should be laid, and those who observed the Rains Retreat earlier should perform Pavāraṇā. Those who observed the Rains Retreat later should announce their purity to those bhikkhus. The same procedure should be followed on the day of the later Rains Retreat Pavāraṇā. If on the day of the Great Pavāraṇā, only two bhikkhus, one who observed the Rains Retreat earlier and one who observed it later, have gathered in one sīmā, the bhikkhu who observed the Rains Retreat earlier should perform Pavāraṇā to the other bhikkhu. The bhikkhu who observed the Rains Retreat later should announce his purity.

8-3.html.title: Pavāraṇā of a Group of Four
8-3.html.body:
If the assembly gathered within the sīmā on the day of Pavāraṇā consists of four bhikkhus, each bhikkhu should perform Pavāraṇā before the remaining three. This is a group Pavāraṇā. It must be performed by establishing a Motion. The Motion is as follows:

Suṇantu me āyasmanto ajja pavāraṇā (paṇṇarasī) yadāyasmantānaṃ pattakallaṃ mayaṃ aññamaññaṃ pavārey‍yāma.

Having established the Motion thus, the senior bhikkhu, having draped his robes over one shoulder, squatting, and having paid homage, [should perform Pavāraṇā]:

“Ahaṃ āvuso āyasmante pavāremi. Diṭṭhena vā sutena vā parisaṅkāya vā vadantu maṃ āyasmanto anukampaṃ upādāya passanto paṭikarissāmi. Dutiyampi ahaṃ āvuso -pe- tatiyampi ahaṃ āvuso āyasmante pavāremi -pe- passanto paṭikarissāmi” Thus

Pavāraṇā should be performed. By the junior bhikkhus -

“Ahaṃ bhante āyasmante pavāremi. Diṭṭhena vā sutena vā parisaṅkāya vā vadantu maṃ āyasmanto anukampaṃ upādāya passanto paṭikarissāmi. Dutiyampi -pe- tatiyampi -pe- paṭikarissāmi” Thus Pavāraṇā should be performed.
8-3.html.nextLabel: Pavāraṇā of an Individual of Four

8-4.html.title: Individual Pavāraṇā for a Small Group
8-4.html.body:
The Pavāraṇā performed when there are three or two bhikkhus is Individual Pavāraṇā. If there are three bhikkhus assembled within the sīmā, a competent bhikkhu should—

“Suṇantu me āyasmantā ajja pavāraṇā paṇṇarasi yadāyasmantānaṃ pattakallaṃ mayaṃ aññamaññaṃ pavāreyyāma.”

Thus, the Motion should be laid. Thereupon, the senior bhikkhu, squatting and clasping his hands in añjali, should—

Ahaṃ āvuso āyasmante pavāremi. Diṭṭhena vā sutena vā parisaṅkāya vā vadantu maṃ āyasmantā anukampaṃ upādāya passanto paṭikarissāmi. Dutiyam-pi -pe- tatiyam-pi -pe- paṭikarissāmi.

Thus, you should perform Pavāraṇā towards the other two bhikkhus. The junior bhikkhus should perform Pavāraṇā by saying, “Ahaṃ bhante,” and so forth.

These two words, “Āyasmanto—Āyasmantā,” should be especially kept in mind. Āyasmanto is plural. Āyasmantā is dual. In the Buddha's Dispensation, the only word used in the dual sense is “Āyasmantā.”

If there are only two bhikkhus, without laying a Motion, the senior bhikkhu, having draped his outer robe over one shoulder, squatting, and clasping his hands in añjali, should address the other bhikkhu thus: “Ahaṃ āvuso āyasmantaṃ pavāremi. Diṭṭhena vā sutena vā parisaṅkāya vā vadatu maṃ āyasmā anukampaṃ upādāya. Passanto paṭikarissāmi. Dutiyam-pi -pe- tatiyam-pi -pe- paṭikarissāmi.” The junior bhikkhu should perform Pavāraṇā towards the senior bhikkhu using the same sentence, substituting ‘bhante’.
8-4.html.prevLabel: Group Pavāraṇā for a Small Group

8-5.html.title: Adhiṭṭhāna Pavāraṇā
8-5.html.body: If, on the day of Pavāraṇā, there is only one bhikkhu, that bhikkhu should go to the sīmā, sweep, set out water, arrange seats, light lamps, and sit there. If, while he is sitting, other bhikkhus arrive, he should perform Pavāraṇā with those bhikkhus. If they do not arrive, he should say “Ajja me pavāraṇā (paṇṇarasīti) adhiṭṭhāmi” three times. This is Adhiṭṭhāna Pavāraṇā.

8-6.html.title: Dvievācika-ādi Pavāraṇā
8-6.html.body: The Pavāraṇā performed by uttering the Pavāraṇā statement once is 'Ekavācika Pavāraṇā'. The Pavāraṇā performed by uttering the Pavāraṇā statement twice is 'Dvievācika Pavāraṇā'. The Pavāraṇā performed by bhikkhus who have observed the Rains Retreat together, as a group, by uttering it once, is 'Samānavassika Pavāraṇā'. These methods of performing Pavāraṇā are necessary in times when there are many bhikkhus. As there are not so many bhikkhus at present, occasions for performing these Pavāraṇās do not arise. Therefore, they are not presented here. Those who wish to know should refer to the Mahāvagga Pavāraṇakkhandhaka.
8-6.html.nextLabel: The Monastic Procedure of Spreading the Kaṭhina Robe

8.html.title: Pavāraṇā
8.html.body:
Pavāraṇā is twofold: 'rejection' and 'invitation'. The Pavāraṇā mentioned in this training rule: “yo pana bhikkhu bhuttāvi pavārito anatirittaṃ khādanīyaṃ vā bhojanīyaṃ vā khādeyya vā bhuñjeyya vā pācittiyaṃ” is rejection. This Pavāraṇā, which is to be performed on the Uposatha day concluding the Rains-residence, is not a rejection like the Pavāraṇā of lay devotees observing precepts. The Pavāraṇā intended here is the request made to the Community of monks, or to a group, or to an individual, to admonish and instruct oneself concerning any fault seen, heard, or suspected from these three causes. This should not be mistaken for the termination of the Rains-residence.

Pavāraṇā, according to the days, is threefold: Cātuddasī Pavāraṇā, Paṇṇarasī Pavāraṇā, and Sāmaggī Pavāraṇā.

According to the performing individuals, it is threefold: Sangha Pavāraṇā, Gaṇa Pavāraṇā, and Puggala Pavāraṇā.

According to the manner of performance, it is threefold: Tevācika Pavāraṇā, Dvevācika Pavāraṇā, and Ekavācika Pavāraṇā. Thus, Pavāraṇā is ninefold.

The Uposatha day concluding the Rains-residence is always a Paṇṇarasī day, as it is the sixth Uposatha of the rainy season. Mostly, Pavāraṇā is performed on that day. If there is a need, the Community of monks may postpone the Pavāraṇā from that day to the seventh or eighth Uposatha day of the rainy season. A Cātuddasī Pavāraṇā occurs if the Pavāraṇā is performed on the seventh Uposatha day of the rainy season. The Pavāraṇā performed on the eighth Uposatha day of the rainy season is also a Paṇṇarasī Pavāraṇā.

If a suitable reason for a Sāmaggī Uposatha arises within the twenty-nine days from the new moon of Vap month until the full moon of Il month, a Sāmaggī Pavāraṇā should be performed. It is not performed at any other time. For a Sangha Pavāraṇā, four bhikkhus are not sufficient, just as for a Sangha Uposatha. At least five bhikkhus must assemble for a Sangha Pavāraṇā to be performed.

Sub-topics

  • Tevācika Sangha Pavāraṇā
  • Pavāraṇā Motion
  • Tevācika Gaṇa Pavāraṇā
  • Tevācika Puggala Pavāraṇā
  • Adhiṭṭhāna Pavāraṇā
  • Dvevācikādi Pavāraṇā
8.html.prevLabel: Matters to be gone to for a seven-day period.

9-1.html.title: Formal Act Statement
9-1.html.body:
The Formal Act Statement is as follows:—

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho. Idaṃ saṅghassa kaṭhinadussaṃ uppannaṃ. Yadi saṅghassa pattakallaṃ. Saṅgho imaṃ kaṭhinadussaṃ (tissassa) bhikkhuno dadey‍ya kaṭhinaṃ at‍tharituṃ. Esā ñatti.

Suṇātu me bhante! saṅgho. Idaṃ saṅghassa kaṭhinadussaṃ uppannaṃ. Saṅgho imaṃ kaṭhinadussaṃ (tissassa) bhikkhuno deti kaṭhinaṃ at‍tharituṃ. Yassāyasmato khamati imassa kaṭhinadussassa (tissassa) bhikkhuno dānaṃ kaṭhinaṃ at‍tharituṃ. So tuṇhassa. Yassa nakkhamati so bhāsey‍ya.

Dinnaṃ idaṃ saṅghena kaṭhinadussaṃ (tissassa) bhikkhuno kaṭhinaṃ at‍tharituṃ khamati saṅghassa, tasmā tuṇhī. Evametaṃ dhārayāmi.

If the Kathina cloth given to a bhikkhu by the Community in this manner is a cloth or robe that has been cut, sewn, and dyed by the donors themselves and prepared so as to be suitable for determination, then, while seated within the boundary where the Rains-residence was observed, mark it with the bindu. If it is a double-layered robe (sanghati), relinquish the old sanghati and determine the new one by reciting three times: “Imaṃ saṅghāṭiṃ adhiṭṭhāmi”. If it is an upper robe (uttarasanga), relinquish the old uttarasanga and determine the new one by reciting three times: “Imaṃ uttarāsaṅgaṃ adhiṭṭhāmi”. If it is an inner robe (antaravasaka), relinquish the old antaravasaka and determine the new one by reciting three times: “Imaṃ antaravāsakaṃ adhiṭṭhāmi”.

Then, taking that robe in hand, if it is the sanghati, recite three times: “Imāya saṅghāṭiyā kaṭhinaṃ attharāmi”. If it is the uttarasanga, recite: “Iminā uttarāsaṅghena kaṭhinaṃ attharāmi”. If it is the antaravasaka, recite: “Iminā antaravāsakena kaṭhinaṃ attharāmi”. Reciting thus constitutes the Monastic Procedure for Spreading the Kathina. When this is performed, that bhikkhu receives the five benefits, such as wandering without having asked for permission (anamantacara), as decreed by the Buddha. Subsequently, that bhikkhu should have the spreading of the Kathina cloth approved.

Perform it in this way:—

The bhikkhu should arrange his robe over one shoulder, place his hands in the gesture of reverence (anjali), and if he is the senior-most monk (Sanghatthera), he should say three times to the Community: “Atthataṃ āvuso saṅghassa kaṭhinaṃ dhammiko kaṭhinatthāro anumodatha”. If there are elders present, he should say: “Atthataṃ bhante saṅghassa kaṭhinaṃ dhammiko kaṭhinatthāro anumodatha”. The bhikkhus who are to approve should arrange their robes over one shoulder, sit in the squatting posture (ukkutika), place their hands in the gesture of reverence, and approve the spreading of the Kathina by reciting: “Atthataṃ bhante saṅghassa kaṭhinaṃ dhammiko kaṭhinatthāro anumodāmi”. Thereafter, the bhikkhus who have approved also gain the five benefits. If the Kathina cloth is an unfinished cloth—

“Pubbakaraṇaṃ sattahi dhammehi saṅgahitaṃ, dhovanena vicāraṇena chedanena bandhanena sibbanena rajanena kappakaraṇena”

The seven preliminary duties—namely: washing, marking for cutting, cutting, tacking, sewing, dyeing, and marking with the bindu—must be completed on that very day, and the spreading of the Kathina must be performed on that same day.

“Kaṭhinaṃ kena dinnaṃ vaṭṭati? Yena kenaci devena vā manussena vā pañcannaṃ vā sahadhammikānaṃ aññatarena dinnaṃ vaṭṭati” According to the Commentary, a cloth given by any human, or a deity, or any of the five classes of co-religionists (bhikkhu, bhikkhuni, samanera, samaneri, or sikkhamana) is suitable for the Kathina.

If a lay devotee who does not know how to give the Kathina asks how it should be given, they should be told that a cloth sufficient for one of the three robes should be given at sunrise with the words, "We give this Kathina cloth," and that needles, thread, and dye for its preparation, as well as almsfood for the bhikkhus participating in it, should be provided. This is stated in the Vinaya Commentary. Regarding the giving of the Kathina, there is not even a single word mentioned in the Vinaya Pali stating that specifically a cloth must be given or a finished robe must be given. The Commentary's statement that 'a cloth sufficient for one of the three robes should be given' is not because a finished robe should not be given, but according to the custom prevalent at that time. In ancient times, the sewing of robes was done by the bhikkhus. Donors offered only the cloth for the robes.

“Saṅghena pana kassa dātabbaṃ? Yo jiṇṇa cīvaro hoti. Sace bahu chinnacīvarā. Buḍḍhassa dātabbaṃ. Buḍḍhesupi yo mahāpariso tadaheva cīvaraṃ katvā attharituṃ sakkoti. Tassa dātabbaṃ. Sace buḍḍho na sakkoti navakataro sakkoti. Tassa dātabbaṃ. ‘Api ca saṅghena mahātherassa saṅgahaṃ kātuṃ vaṭṭati. Tasmā tumhe bhante gaṇhatha. Mayaṃ katvā dassāmi’ti vattabbaṃ”

As stated in the Commentary, the cloth offered for the Kathina by a donor should be given by the Community to that bhikkhu among the group whose robes are worn out. If there are no such bhikkhus, or if many have worn-out robes, the cloth should be given to the senior-most (vuddha). Among the seniors, it should be given to one who has a large following capable of completing the cutting, sewing, and dyeing of the robe on that very day to spread the Kathina. If the senior is not capable but a junior is capable, the Kathina cloth should be given to the junior. It is proper for the Community to show regard to the Chief Elder (Mahathera). Therefore, the Community should say to the Great Elder, "Venerable Sir, please accept it; we shall prepare it for you."
9-1.html.prevLabel: The Monastic Procedure for Spreading the Kathina
9-1.html.nextLabel: Requisites Offered with the Kathina Robe

9-10.html.title: The Eight Topics
9-10.html.body:
Although the spreading of the Kathina is performed by one bhikkhu, it also pertains to the bhikkhus who rejoice in it. Therefore, in the Parivāra Pāli, it was declared: “Dvinnaṃ puggalānaṃ atthataṃ hoti kaṭhinaṃ, atthārakassa ca anumodakassa ca”, meaning ‘The Kathina is spread for two persons: the spreader and the rejoicer.’

There are eight conditions by which that Kathina-spreading procedure, which pertains to both the spreader and the rejoicer, ceases, loses its validity, or becomes annulled. The annulment of the Kathina-spreading is called ‘Kaṭhinuddhāra’ or ‘Kaṭhinubbhāra’. Upon the occurrence of Kaṭhinuddhāra, the five benefits (pañcānisamsa) obtained from the Kathina-spreading are also lost.

“Aṭṭhimā bhikkhave mātikā kaṭhinassa ubbhārāya, pakkamanantikā, niṭṭhānantikā, sanniṭṭhānantikā, nāsanantikā, savanantikā, āsāvacchedikā, sīmātikkantikā, sahubbhārāti”

Thus, in the Kathinakkhandhaka of the Mahāvagga Pāli, eight topics concerning the annulment of the Kathina (Kaṭhinuddhāra) have been declared. What is indicated by ‘mātikaṃ jānāti’ among the qualifications of a person suitable for Kathina-spreading is the knowledge of these eight topics. The topics should be known as follows:

1. Pakkamanantikā (Ending with Departure).

If a bhikkhu, having completed his robe, takes it and departs from the place where he observed the rains-residence with no intention of returning, then the annulment of the Kathina (Kaṭhinuddhāra) occurs for that bhikkhu as soon as he crosses the boundary of the precincts. Thereafter, that bhikkhu loses the five benefits (pañcānisamsa) obtained from the spreading of the Kathina. This annulment of the Kathina is called ‘Pakkamanantika’ because it ends with departing from the monastery.

2. Niṭṭhānantikā (Ending with Completion).

If a bhikkhu, having received cloth at the place where he observed the rains-residence, takes it and goes to another monastery, and outside the boundary of the rains-residence, he resolves, “I will not return to the monastery where I observed the rains-residence,” then the validity of the Kathina-spreading (Kaṭhinatthāra) for that bhikkhu lasts only until he completes making a robe from the cloth he took. As soon as the work on the robe is completed, the annulment of the Kathina (Kaṭhinuddhāra) occurs for that bhikkhu. This annulment of the Kathina is called ‘Niṭṭhānantika’ because it ends with the completion of the robe.

3. Sanniṭṭhānantikā (Ending with Resolution).

If a bhikkhu, having taken the cloth he received from the place where he observed the rains-residence and departed from the monastery, resolves, “I will not return to the place where I observed the rains-residence, nor will I make this robe,” then the annulment of the Kathina (Kaṭhinuddhāra) occurs for that bhikkhu. This annulment of the Kathina is called ‘Sanniṭṭhānantika’ because it ends with the resolution.

4. Nāsanantikā (Ending with Loss).

If a bhikkhu, having received robe-cloth at the place where he observed the rains-residence and departed, resolves there, “I will make this robe right here, and I will not return to the place where I observed the rains-residence,” and while he is making that robe, the robe is lost by theft or in any other way, then the annulment of the Kathina (Kaṭhinuddhāra) occurs for that bhikkhu. This annulment of the Kathina is called ‘Nāsanantikā’ because it ends with the loss.

5. Savanantikā (Ending with Hearing).

A bhikkhu for whom the Kathina has been spread takes the cloth he received and departs with the intention of returning to the monastery later. There, the bhikkhu thinks, ‘I will make this robe right here.’ The bhikkhu makes the robe there. While that bhikkhu is staying, having completed the work on the robe, the news reaches him that ‘the Kathina has been annulled by the Community of monks in the monastery.’ Thereupon, the annulment of the Kathina (Kaṭhinuddhāra) also occurs for that bhikkhu. This annulment of the Kathina is called ‘Savanantikā’ because it ends with hearing the news.

6. Āsāvacchedikā (Ending with the Cutting Off of Hope).

A bhikkhu for whom the Kathina has been spread, not having received robes at the place where he observed the rains-residence, departs with the hope of receiving robes. Outside the monastery where he observed the rains-residence, that bhikkhu thinks, “I will not return to the place where I observed the rains-residence; I will stay here and hope for robes.” That bhikkhu stays there, reaching the place where he hopes for robes. If robes are not received from the place of hope, and that bhikkhu’s hope is cut off, then the annulment of the Kathina (Kaṭhinuddhāra) occurs for that bhikkhu. This annulment of the Kathina is called ‘Āsāvacchedikā’ because it occurs by the cutting off of hope.

7. Sīmātikkantikā (Ending with Transgression of the Boundary).

A bhikkhu for whom the Kathina has been spread takes the robe-cloth and departs with the intention of returning to the monastery. He makes the robe there. With the intention that ‘after the work on the robe is finished, that bhikkhu will return to the monastery,’ he stays there until the end of the Kathina annulment period. The annulment of the Kathina (Kaṭhinuddhāra) occurs for that bhikkhu by the expiration of the time. This annulment of the Kathina is called ‘Sīmātikkantikā’ because it occurs by transgressing the boundary, which is the last day of the robe period.

8. Sahubbhārā (Simultaneous Annulment).

A bhikkhu for whom the Kathina has been spread takes the robe and departs with the intention of returning to that monastery. He completes the robe and spends time there with the hope of returning, and then returns to the monastery at the time when the bhikkhus in the monastery annul the Kathina. The annulment of the Kathina (Kaṭhinuddhāra) occurs for that bhikkhu simultaneously with the annulment for the bhikkhus in the monastery. This annulment of the Kathina is called ‘Sahubbhārā’ because it occurs simultaneously with the annulment for the bhikkhus in the monastery. Details regarding the annulments of the Kathina should be known from the Kaṭhinakkhandhaka.

9-11.html.title: Two Obstacles
9-11.html.body:
There are two obstacles to be known concerning the kaṭhina: the obstacle of dwelling (āvāsa-paḷibodha) and the obstacle of robes (cīvara-paḷibodha). The obstacle of dwelling is the necessity to reside in the monastery where the kaṭhina was spread, and if one departs from it, to maintain the expectation of returning. The obstacle of robes is that the bhikkhu's robe is not yet made or not yet finished, or that there is an expectation of obtaining a robe.

“Katame ca bhikkhave! dve kaṭhinassa paḷibodhā? Āvāsapaḷibodho ca cīvarapaḷibodho ca kathañca bhikkhave! āvāsapaḷibodho hoti? Idha bhikkhave! bhikkhu vasati vā yasmiṃ āvāse sāpekkhā vā pakkamati paccessati evaṃ kho bhikkhave! āvāsapaḷibodho hoti. Kathaṃ ca bhikkhave! cīvarapaḷibodho hoti? Idha bhikkhave! bhikkhuno cīvaraṃ akataṃ vā hoti vippakataṃ vā cīvarāsā vā anupacchinnā evaṃ kho bhikkhave! cīvarapaḷibodho hoti. Ime kho bhikkhave! kaṭhinassa dve paḷibodhā.”

Thus, the two obstacles are declared in the Kaṭhinakkhandhaka. The obstacle of dwelling (āvāsa-paḷibodha) persists as long as there is an expectation of residing in the monastery where the kaṭhina was spread. The obstacle of dwelling ceases when the expectation regarding the monastery is abandoned. The obstacle of robes (cīvara-paḷibodha) persists as long as there is a desire to make or obtain a robe. The obstacle of robes ceases when the robe is made, or when the cloth is destroyed, or when the expected robe is not received and the expectation is broken. The aforementioned eight types of kaṭhinoddhāra (removal of the kaṭhina) occur when these two obstacles are cut off. For a bhikkhu who has cut off the obstacle of dwelling and departed from the monastery, the validity of the kaṭhina-spreading remains until the obstacle of robes is cut off. After the obstacle of robes is cut off, that validity ends. Thereafter, that bhikkhu does not have the five advantages (pañcānisaṃsa). For a bhikkhu whose obstacle of robes has been cut off, the kaṭhinoddhāra occurs simultaneously with the abandonment of the expectation regarding the monastery.

9-12.html.title: Monastic Procedure for Removing the Kathina
9-12.html.body: If the Kathina has been spread in a certain monastery, all robes arising in that monastery until the end of the winter season belong to the bhikkhus who spread the Kathina. They do not belong to others. Therefore, if some faithful person wishes to offer many robes to the Saṅgha so that they may also belong to other bhikkhus, and requests the Saṅgha to perform the removal of the Kathina, the Saṅgha must perform the removal of the Kathina. Thereafter, all robes received by that monastery must be distributed equally among all bhikkhus.
9-12.html.nextLabel: Formal Announcement for Removing the Kathina

9-13.html.title: The Formal Announcement for the Revocation of the Kathina
9-13.html.body:
The formal announcement for the revocation of the Kathina is as follows:

“Suṇātu me bhante! Saṅgho. Yadi Saṅghassa pattakallaṃ, saṅgho kaṭhinaṃ uddhareyya. Esā ñatti.

Suṇātu me bhante! Saṅgho. Saṅgho kaṭhinaṃ uddharati. Yassāyasmato khamati kaṭhinassa ubbhāro. So tuṇhasssa. Yassa nakkhamati so bhāseyya. Ubbhataṃ Saṅghena kaṭhinaṃ. Khamati Saṅghassa. Tasmā tuṇhī. Evametaṃ dhārayāmi.”

If the benefits based on the revocation of the Kathina are greater than the benefits based on the spreading of the Kathina, then the Kathina should be revoked. If the benefits based on the revocation are few, it should not be revoked. For the sake of preserving the donor's faith, the Kathina should also be revoked when the benefits are equal. Opposing a righteous revocation of the Kathina incurs an offense.
9-13.html.prevLabel: The Monastic Procedure for the Revocation of the Kathina

9-14.html.title: The Benefits of Kathina
9-14.html.body:
To bhikkhus who have spread the Kathina, there are five benefits received according to the Buddha's injunction due to that Monastic Procedure. This is called ‘Kaṭhināniṃsa’ (Benefits of Kathina). Furthermore, the robes and other requisites received due to the Kathina robe are also called Kaṭhināniṃsa. The reward received by the Kathina donor from that merit is also called by that name. It should be known that the five benefits received by the bhikkhus who have spread the Kathina, according to the Buddha's injunction, are not a result of that merit. The fivefold benefits received by the bhikkhu are declared thus in the Kaṭhinakkhandhaka:

Atthatakaṭhinānaṃ vo bhikkhave! Pañcakappissanti anāmantavāro, asamādānavāro, gaṇabhojanaṃ, yāvadatthacīvaraṃ, yo ca tattha cīvaruppādo so nesaṃ bhavissati.

Hereby it is declared that five matters become allowable for bhikkhus who have spread the Kathina due to that reason. These are as follows:-

1. Anāmantacāro (Going without prior invitation).

“Yo pana bhikkhu nimantito sa bhatto samāno santaṃ bhikkhuṃ anāpucchā purebhattaṃ vā pacchābhattaṃ vā kulesu cārittaṃ āpajjeyya aññatra samayā pācittiyaṃ tatrāyaṃ samayo cīvaradāna samayo, cīvarakārasamayo, ayaṃ tattha samayo.”

This is the sixth training rule in the Acelakavagga of the Pātimokkha. According to this training rule, the offense incurred by a bhikkhu residing in a monastery who goes to houses without asking another bhikkhu is not applicable to bhikkhus who have spread the Kathina. After the Kathina has been spread, freedom is obtained to frequent families without asking.

2. Asamādānacāro (Going without the three robes).

“Niṭṭhitacīvarasmiṃ pana bhikkhunā ubbhatasmiṃ kaṭhine. Ekarattaṃ’pi ce bhikkhu ticīvarena vippavaseyya aññatra bhikkhu sammutiyā, nissaggiyaṃ pācittiyaṃ.”

This is the second training rule in the Nissaggiya Pācittiyas of the Pātimokkha. According to this training rule, the offense incurred by a bhikkhu who spends a night separated from his determined set of three robes is not applicable to bhikkhus who have spread the Kathina.

3. Gaṇabhojanaṃ (Group meal).

“Gaṇabhojane aññatra samayā pācittiyaṃ. Tatthāyaṃ samayo. Gilānasamayo cīvaradānasamayo cīvarakārasamayo addhānagamanasamayo nāvābhirūhanasamayo mahāsamayo samaṇabhattasamayo ayaṃ tattha samayo.”

This is the second training rule in the Bhojanavagga of the Pātimokkha. According to this training rule, the offense incurred by partaking in a group meal is not applicable to bhikkhus who have spread the Kathina.

4. Yāvadattha Cīvaraṃ (As many robes as one wishes).

“Niṭṭhitacīvarasmiṃ pana bhikkhunā ubbhatasmiṃ kaṭhine dasāhaparamaṃ atirekacīvaraṃ dhāretabbaṃ: taṃ atikkāmayato nissaggiyaṃ pācittiyaṃ.”

This is the first training rule in the Nissaggiya Pācittiyas. According to this training rule, the offense incurred by keeping extra robes for more than ten days is not applicable to bhikkhus who have spread the Kathina.

5. Yo ca tattha Cīvaruppādo so nesaṃ bhavissati (Whatever robe-material arises there, that will be theirs).

If, within the boundary where the Kathina has been spread, robes belonging to the Community arise as rag-robes, or are offered by devotees as belonging to the Community, or from things produced on that land, or in any other way, during the period of Kathina benefits, all of those belong to the bhikkhus who have spread the Kathina. Apart from robes received communally, other requisites do not become theirs by the power of the Kathina spreading. Those belong to everyone. The reception of these five benefits is the special advantage for bhikkhus from the spreading of the Kathina.
9-14.html.prevLabel: The Formal Announcement for Revoking the Kathina
9-14.html.nextLabel: The Period of Spreading the Kathina and the Period of Benefits

9-15.html.title: The Period of Kathina Spreading and the Period of Benefits
9-15.html.body:
“Kaṭhinassa atthāramaso jānitabboti. Vassānassa pacchimo māso jānitabboti.” This being a Pali passage from the Parivara, the last month of the rainy season should be known as the period for spreading the Kathina. That is: the twenty-nine days from the new moon of Vap to the full moon of Il. The efficacy of the Monastic Procedure of spreading the Kathina lasts from the day the Kathina is spread until the full moon Poya day of Medin, which is the last day of the winter season. Until then, the five benefits declared for bhikkhus who have spread the Kathina are present.

If there are extra robes that have not been determined or relinquished, their determination or relinquishment should be performed on that day. For all bhikkhus, whether they have spread the Kathina or not, the twenty-nine days of the last month of the rainy season constitute the robe period (cīvara-kāla). For bhikkhus who have spread the Kathina, the entire five months from the new moon of Vap until the full moon day of Medin constitute the robe period. During the robe period, keeping extra robes does not incur an offense.
9-15.html.nextLabel: Bhikkhus Who Receive and Do Not Receive the Kathina Spreading

9-16.html.title: Monks who Receive and Do Not Receive the Kathina Spreading
9-16.html.body:
“Kaṭhinatthāraṃ ke labhanti? Ke na labhanti? Gaṇanavasena tāva pacchimakoṭiyā pañcajanā labhanti. Uddhaṃ satasahassampi pañcannaṃ heṭṭhā na labhanti. Vutthavassavasena purimikāya vassaṃ upagantvā paṭhamapavāraṇāya pavāritā labhanti. Jinnavassā vā pacchimikāya upagatā vā na labhanti.”

According to what is stated in the Vinaya Commentary, for the performance of the Kathina spreading Monastic Procedure, there must be at least five bhikkhus. The Kathina is spread by one bhikkhu. This can only be done with a robe given by the Community of monks to a bhikkhu. Therefore, apart from the bhikkhu who receives it, four other bhikkhus must be present to give the robe. That is why there must be at least five bhikkhus for a Kathina to be spread. The Kathina can only be spread by a bhikkhu who observed the Rains Retreat previously and performed the Pavarana at the first Pavarana. Any bhikkhu may assist in assigning the Kathina cloth. Only bhikkhus who observed the Rains Retreat previously and performed the Pavarana receive the benefits of the Kathina. Others do not receive them. Bhikkhus who observed the Rains Retreat and performed the Pavarana in another monastery also do not receive the benefits of a Kathina spread in one monastery. If there is even one bhikkhu who observed the Rains Retreat previously and performed the Pavarana in a certain place, that bhikkhu can spread the Kathina there with the assistance of other bhikkhus.
9-16.html.prevLabel: The Period for Spreading the Kathina and the Period of its Benefits

9-2.html.title: Accessories Offered with the Kathina Robe
9-2.html.body:
When a Kathina robe is offered, donors also offer many other requisites, such as robes, as accessory requisites. These are called ‘kaṭhinānisaṃsa parikkhāra’. The manner in which these should be dealt with is stated in the Vinaya commentary as follows.

Evam atthate pana kaṭhine, sace kaṭhina cīvarena saddhiṃ āhataṃ ānisaṃsaṃ dāyakā ‘yena amhākaṃ kaṭhinaṃ gahitaṃ tasseva demā’ti denti, bhikkhusaṅgho anissaro. Atha avicāretvāva datvā gacchanti, bhikkhusaṅgho issaro. Tasmā sace kaṭhinatthārakassa sesacīvarāni’pi dubbalāni honti. Saṅghena apaloketvā tesam’pi atthāya vatthāni dātabbāni, kammavācā pana ekāva vaṭṭati. Avasese kaṭhinānisaṃse balavavatthāni vassāvāsikaṭṭhitikāya dātabbāni. Ṭhitikāya abhāve therāsanato paṭṭhāya dātabbāni. Garubhāṇḍaṃ na bhājetabbaṃ.

Its meaning is as follows:-

‘When the Kathina has thus been spread, if the donors, bringing accessory requisites along with that Kathina robe, say, “We give these to the Elder who received our Kathina,” then they do not belong to the Community of monks. If the donors depart without saying anything about them, then those requisites belong to the Community. Therefore, if the other robes of the bhikkhu who received the Kathina are also worn out, then, having informed the Community, cloth should be given to the Kathina-spreader for those robes as well. The formal proclamation (kammavācā) is to be recited only for the Kathina robe. From the remaining Kathina-anisaṃsa, strong robes (sturdy, good robes) should be given according to the vaṭṭasāvāsikaṭṭhitikā. If there is no ṭṭhitikā, they should be given in order, starting from the senior-most Elder. Heavy articles (garubhāṇḍa) should not be divided.

In places where many bhikkhus reside, robes received by the Community are distributed in order of seniority as they are received. This is done as follows: If five robes are received today, they are given to five bhikkhus in order, starting from the senior-most Elder. The robes received thereafter are given in order, starting from the sixth Elder. “Ṭṭhitikā” refers to the point where the distribution of robes stopped. If there is a point where the distribution of robes, given in order, has stopped, then the Kathina-anisaṃsa robes are distributed starting from that ṭṭhitikā.

9-4.html.title: Spreading the Kathina with a Pre-made Robe
9-4.html.body:
Because it was said: “Na aññat‍ra pañcakena vā atireka pañcakena vā tadaheva sañchinnena samaṇḍalikatena at‍thataṃ hoti kaṭhinaṃ”, the Kathina is not spread if it is done with a robe that is not cut into five or more parts on that very day the cloth was received, or if it is not made with the great circles and half-circles, or if it is uncut, or cut into only two or three parts.

Many people hold that 'spreading the Kathina with a robe made beforehand is a mistake' due to a misunderstanding of the aforementioned passage. The primary point indicated by that passage is that a robe which is uncut or has fewer than five parts is unsuitable for the spreading of the Kathina. The word “Tadaheva” (on that very day) is used in that passage in relation to storage (sannidhi) and the forfeiture (nissaggiya) connection. Regarding work done on the Kathina cloth beforehand, there is no storage issue. Storage occurs only if the Community, having received the cloth, does not complete everything and spread the Kathina on that same day.

After receiving the Kathina cloth and the Community has given it to a suitable bhikkhu, the sevenfold preliminary duties must be performed. Among the preliminary duties to be performed, if any particular duty has already been done to that cloth, there is no need to do it again. Preliminary duties are to be performed only if they have not been done. If a robe that has been sewn and dyed is received, the only preliminary duty remaining to be done is the marking with the smudge-point (kappabindu). Having done that, and having determined it and spread the Kathina with it, the spreading of the Kathina is accomplished.

In the Mahāvaggapāḷi, the method of going forth is stated as: “Evañca pana bhikkhave pabbājetabbo, upasampādetabbo, paṭhamaṃ kesamassuṃ ohāretvā kāsāyāni vatthāni acchādetvā ekaṃsaṃ uttarāsaṅgaṃ kārāpetvā bhikkhūnaṃ pāde vandāpetvā” and so on. If someone arrives to go forth having already shaved his hair and beard, it is not necessary to follow the aforementioned teaching literally by replanting hair on his head to cut it again, or to wait until the hair and beard grow back. Since the removal of hair and beard has already occurred, he should be made to don the robes and be given the going forth.

Similarly, if a Kathina cloth is received where the preliminary duties have already occurred, there is no need to perform those preliminary duties again. If there is an unfinished preliminary duty, it should be completed and the Kathina spread. Just as the going forth of one who has already shaved his hair and beard is valid, the spreading of the Kathina done with a robe where the preliminary duties were finished beforehand is also valid. There is no obstacle caused by it being done beforehand. Therefore, in the Vinaya Commentary:

“Evaṃ dinne pana kaṭhine sace taṃ kaṭhina dussaṃ niṭṭhitaparikammameva hoti. Iccetaṃ kusalaṃ. No ce niṭṭhitaparikammaṃ ahaṃ theroti vā bahussutoti vā ekenā pi akātuṃ na labhati. Sabbeheva sannipatitvā dhovanasibbanarajanāni niṭṭhāpetabbāni”

The meaning is: "When the Kathina is thus given, if that Kathina cloth is already finished in its preparation, that is well. If it is not finished in its preparation, even a single bhikkhu is not allowed to refrain, thinking 'I am an Elder' or 'I am learned.' All must assemble and complete the washing, sewing, and dyeing."

That passage is also mentioned in the same way in the Pāḷi muttaka vinaya vinicchaya of the Venerable Sariputta, the bearer of the Tipitaka. That passage is explained in the Vinayālaṃkāra ṭīkāva as follows:

“Sace taṃ kaṭhinadussaṃ niṭṭhitaparikammameva hotīti iminā kaṭhinadussaṃ nāma na kevalaṃ pakati sāṭakameva: atha kho pariniṭṭhitasattavidhapubbaniccaṃ cīvarampi hotīti daseti (tasmā niṭṭhita cīvarampi hotīti dasseti.) tasmā niṭṭhitacīvarasmiṃ dinne sattavidhapubbakiccakaraṇena attho natthi. Kevalaṃ paccuddharaṇādīni yeva kātabbāni. Sace pana kiñci apariniṭṭhitā hoti. Antamaso kappa bindumattampi. Taṃ niṭṭhāpetvā paccuddharaṇādīni kātabbāni.”

The meaning is as follows: "By the passage “Sace taṃ kaṭhinadussaṃ niṭṭhita-parikammameva hoti”, it is shown that what is called Kathina cloth is not merely raw cloth; it can also be a robe with the sevenfold preliminary duties completed. Therefore, when a finished robe is given (for the Kathina), there is no need for the sevenfold preliminary duties. Only the relinquishing and so forth should be done. If any preliminary duty is not finished, even if at least the mere marking with the smudge-point remains, that should be completed and then the relinquishing should be done."

That the Kathina should be spread with a robe that the donor has previously cut, sewn, and dyed is shown in the Vajirabuddhi ṭīkāva as follows:

Pariniṭṭhitapubbakaraṇameva ce dāyako saṅghassa deti. Sampaṭicchitvā kammavācāya dātabbaṃ. Tena ca tasmiṃ yeva sīmāmaṇḍale adhiṭṭhahitvā attharitvā saṅgho anumodāpetabbo. Katapubbakaraṇassa puna kattabbābhāvato.

The meaning is: If the donor gives to the Community a cloth that has its preliminary work already completed, the Community should accept it and give it to the Kathina-spreader by a formal act. The Kathina-spreader (if the boundary-circle is also part of the boundary where the rains were spent) should determine it and spread the Kathina in that very boundary-circle and have the Community express appreciation. This should be done because there is nothing further to be done to a cloth that has its preliminary duties already completed.

Furthermore, in the Vajirabuddhi ṭīkāva, regarding the passage “Tadaheva sañchinnena samaṇḍalīkatena” from the Mahāvagga pāḷiya, it is stated: “Tadaheva sañchinnenāti saṅghena kaṭhinatthārakassa kammavācaṃ vatvā dinneneva tadaheva sañchinnena samaṇḍalīkatena bhavitabbaṃ -pe- na dāyakena dīyamānaṃ”. This indicates that the requirement applies to the Kathina cloth given by the Community to the Kathina-spreader after reciting the formal act, and not to the cloth while it is in the hands of the donor. No matter how long the donor keeps it and performs the sewing etc., it is the donor's storage and not the storage of the Community of monks; therefore, a robe sewn beforehand is not something that should not be taken for the spreading of the Kathina. Regarding the Kathina, it is stated in the “Mātikaṭṭha kathāvehi” as follows:

Sace purimavassaṃ vutthānaṃ gahaṭṭhapabbajitesu yo koci dhammena samena cīvaraṃ deti, iminā kaṭhinaṃ attharathāti, taṃ khandhake vuttāya ñattidutiyakammavācāya kaṭhinatthārārahassa bhikkhuno dātabbaṃ. Tena tadaheva pañca vā atirekāni vā khaṇḍāni chinditvā saṅghāṭi vā uttarāsaṅgo vā antaravāsako vā kātabbo. Sesabhikkhūhi tassa sahāyehi bhavitabbaṃ. Sace katacīvarameva uppajjati sundarameva. Acchinnā sibbitaṃ pana na vaṭṭati.

The meaning is: If anyone among householders or those gone forth gives a robe righteously and fairly to the monks who have completed the first rains-residence, saying 'Spread the Kathina with this,' it should be given to a bhikkhu suitable for the spreading of the Kathina by the Formal Statement followed by one proclamation as stated in the Khandhaka. By that bhikkhu, on that very day, it should be cut into five or more parts and made into an outer robe, an upper robe, or an inner robe. The remaining monks should be assistants to that bhikkhu. If a robe already made is produced, it is excellent. However, a robe that is uncut and unsewn is not suitable.

Thus, the fact that a robe previously cut, sewn, and dyed by donors is suitable for the Kathina is shown in various places in the Commentaries and Sub-commentaries. This is not due to an inability to understand or a misunderstanding of the Buddha's word “Na aññat‍ra pañcakena vā atireka pañcakena vā tadaheva sañchinnena samaṇḍalīkatena atthataṃ hoti kaṭhinaṃ”, but rather from understanding its meaning exactly according to the Buddha's intention.

These Commentaries and Sub-commentaries were written not in a time like the present where monks do not show much respect for the Discipline or use Vinaya texts, but in a time when monks followed the Vinaya training rules to the letter with great care and respect, and performed the learning and teaching of the Discipline with devotion. There is no doubt that during that time, not just the raw cloth but also robes previously finished and offered by donors were taken for the spreading of the Kathina. Therefore, it should be known that spreading the Kathina with robes made beforehand is not a new thing that started today or yesterday.

Some say, "There is nothing in the Buddha's teaching about taking robes sewn beforehand for the Kathina; it is a commentarial opinion." Regarding the Discipline, there is no such thing as a 'commentarial opinion' versus a 'Buddha's opinion.' The commentators do not have the power to add to, subtract from, or change the Discipline. Those Venerable Teachers do not say anything contrary to the Buddha's opinion. What is done by the Commentary is the clarification of the Buddha's opinion regarding various matters. Therefore, it should be known that both the taking of a robe sewn beforehand and the taking of cloth for the Kathina are indeed the Buddha's opinion.

9-5.html.title: Views from a Modern Book
9-5.html.body:
In a modern book by a certain Mahathera, who has made a great effort to interpret commentarial passages in a way that would invalidate a Kathina robe cut, sewn, and dyed by donors, it is stated that there are two ways of giving: the Community itself making a robe from the cloth received from the donor and giving it to the monk who is the spreader, or giving the cloth exactly as it was received. He states:

“Evaṃ dinne pana kaṭhine sace taṃ niṭṭhitaparikammaṃ hoti iccetaṃ kusalaṃ. No ce niṭṭhitaparikammaṃ hoti sabbaheva sannipatitvā dhovanasibbanarajanāni niṭṭhāpetabbāni”

He claims this commentarial passage indicates that if the Kathina cloth assigned by the Community to the spreader monk does not have the preliminary work completed, everyone must assemble to finish the washing, sewing, and dyeing. That commentarial passage indeed shows that after the Community assigns the Kathina cloth to a monk, all monks should participate in the washing and other tasks. However, in that modern book, it is said: “In both cases—assigning and then making the robe, and making the robe and then assigning—the Community must be united. If any remain apart, there is an offense of wrong-doing.”

We do not know where that Thera discovered that an offense of wrong-doing occurs by not participating in the making of the robe *before* the assignment, as this is not mentioned in the commentary. If the intention of the commentator in the passage beginning with Evaṃ dinne pana kaṭhine was to show that everyone must unite to finish the Kathina robe, there would be no need for such a long sentence. In that case, it should simply say: “Evaṃ dinne pana kaṭhine sabbeheva sannipatitvā dhovana sibbana rajanāni niṭṭhāpetabbā.” If stated thus, it would clearly express that one must participate in the making of the Kathina robe at any stage, without many words and without confusion.

If the purpose were solely to show that one must participate in the making of the robe, inserting the phrase “Sace taṃ niṭṭhitaparikammaṃ hoti iccetaṃ kusalaṃ no ca niṭṭhitaparikammaṃ hoti” into the middle of the sentence would be useless. When that is included, it does not say that the Community must unite if the Kathina robe is being made before it is given to a monk. Would the commentators, who possessed sharp intelligence and were extremely proficient in composition, create such a confusion? When the commentator said “Sace taṃ niṭṭhitaparikammaṃ hoti,” he was referring not to a robe made by the Community, but to a robe made and offered by a donor. When the meaning is taken in this way, there is no confusion at all.

Although the modern book mentions a custom of the Community making the Kathina robe and later assigning it to the spreader monk, it is not mentioned anywhere in the Pali, the Commentaries, or the Sub-commentaries that there is a custom of performing the preliminary work before the Community gives the Kathina cloth to a monk. That is merely an opinion of that Thera. The manner in which the Community should proceed when a Kathina cloth arises is stated in the Parivara Pali as follows:

“Sace saṅghassa kaṭhinadussaṃ uppannaṃ hoti. Saṅghena kathaṃ paṭipajjitabbaṃ? At‍thārakena kathaṃ paṭipajjitabbaṃ? Anumodakena kathaṃ paṭipajjitabbaṃ? Saṅghena ñattidutiyakammena kaṭhinatthārakassa bhikkhuno dātabbaṃ. Tena kaṭhinatthārakena bhikkhunā tadaheva dhovitvā vimajjitvā vicāretvā jinditvā sibbetvā rajitvā kappaṃ katvā kaṭhinaṃ attharitabbaṃ.”

The meaning is: ‘If a Kathina cloth arises for the Community, how should the Community proceed? How should the spreader proceed? How should the one who appreciates proceed? The Community should give it to the spreader monk by a formal act consisting of a motion and a second. That spreader monk, on that very day, having washed it, pressed it, examined it, cut it, sewn it, dyed it, and marked it, should spread the Kathina.’

There, only the assignment of the cloth by the Community to the spreader monk is mentioned, not the assignment of a finished robe. It is also evident from the cited Parivara passage that the preliminary work belongs to the spreader, not the Community. In describing the qualities of a monk suitable for the spreading of the Kathina, the fact that “he knows the preliminary work” (Pubbakaraṇaṃ jānāti) is preached as the first quality because the preliminary work is a task belonging to the spreader monk. The task belonging to the Community is to assist the spreader monk in the preliminary work.

The Community making the Kathina robe before assigning it to a monk also contradicts the Parivara passage: “Kaṭhinassa pubbakaraṇaṃ ādi kiriyā majjhe atthāro pariyosānaṃ.” (The preliminary work of the Kathina is the beginning, the act is the middle, and the spreading is the end). If the preliminary work is done before assigning the Kathina, then the assignment of the robe would fall into the middle of the Kathina process. In the Parivara, the two acts of relinquishing and determination are shown as the middle of the Kathina process. Therefore, the method of the Community making a robe and giving it to the spreader—

Must be described as a method of assigning the Kathina fabricated by that Mahathera in order to confuse the meaning of the commentarial passage beginning with “Evaṃ dinne pana kaṭhine.” Even though such a custom is not mentioned in the Monastic Procedures, it cannot be said that it results in a failure of the Kathina.

The commentarial passage “Karaṇavipannaṃ nāma tadaheva chinditvā akataṃ” is also cited in that book to show that a robe made by donors is unsuitable for the Kathina. This is an excellent commentarial passage to cite for the purpose of deceiving those who do not know the Monastic Procedures. Anyone who does not know the facts stated in the Kathina-kkhandhaka regarding the Kathina, and who does not know about the three failures of the Kathina, can be deceived by citing that passage. The meaning of the commentarial passage “Karaṇavipannaṃ nāma tadaheva chinditvā akataṃ” can only be correctly understood by looking at all three failures of the Kathina together.

Three failures of the spreading of the Kathina are mentioned in the Parivara Pali: “failure of object, failure of time, and failure in making.” Among these, “failure of object” means the Kathina cloth being an unallowable cloth. If the Kathina cloth is an unallowable cloth unsuitable for a robe, even if the preliminary work and other tasks are performed and the Kathina is spread with it, it does not achieve success.

As stated in the commentary, “Kālavipannaṃ nāma ajja dāyakehi dinnaṃ sve saṅgho kaṭhinatthārakassa deti”—if the Community does not give the Kathina cloth received today to the spreader monk today, but gives it tomorrow—the Kathina robe becomes a failure of time. A spreading of the Kathina performed with a robe that has failed in time also does not achieve success.

As stated in the commentary, “Karaṇavipannaṃ nāma tadaheva chinditvā akataṃ”—a robe where the preliminary work was not done on the same day the Community gave the cloth to the spreader monk, but was completed on a later day, is called a failure in making. A spreading of the Kathina performed with such a robe also does not achieve success.

All the rules and regulations regarding the Kathina do not concern cloth or robes found in shops or with donors, but rather cloth or robes offered to the Community. There is no rule in the Monastic Procedures stating that a donor must offer only cloth and not a robe for the Kathina, or that if a robe is given, it should not be accepted, or that the Kathina should not be spread with it. If a donor offers a finished robe for the Kathina, the Community should accept it and give it to the spreader monk.

Since the preliminary work such as cutting and sewing has already been done, there is no need for the spreader monk to wash out the dye and cut and sew it again, thinking, ‘I must complete the preliminary work.’ Since those tasks have already been performed on that robe, there is no failure in making if they are not done again. It is endowed with the success of making. It would be a great absurdity to destroy a robe that has been cut, sewn, and dyed, claiming it is to achieve the success of making. The Buddha does not prescribe such useless work. Therefore, it should be understood that spreading the Kathina with a robe previously made by a donor does not result in a Kathina failed in making; rather, a robe becomes failed in making only by performing the preliminary work on a day later than the day the cloth was received.
9-5.html.prevLabel: Spreading the Kathina with a Pre-made Robe

9-6.html.title: Inquiry into the Kathina
9-6.html.body:
Just as in the Monastic Procedures of Uposatha and Pavāraṇā, there is a Vinaya inquiry to be made when conferring the Kathina. This is stated in the Vinayālaṅkāra thus:

“Kaṭhinadussaṃ labhitvā saṅghe sīmāya sannipatite ekena bhikkhunā bhante! saṅghassa idaṃ kaṭhinadussaṃ uppannaṃ saṅgho idaṃ kaṭhinadussaṃ itthaṃ nāmassa bhikkhuno dadey‍ya kaṭhinaṃ attharitunti vuttaṃ, aññena yo chiṇṇacīvaro tassāti vattabbaṃ. Puna purimena bahu chiṇṇacīvarāti vā natthi idha chiṇṇacīvaroti vā vutte, aparena tenahi vuḍḍhassāti vattabbaṃ. Puna purimena ko ettha vuḍḍhoti vutte, itarena itthaṃ nāmo bhikkhūti vattabbo. Puna purimena so bhikkhu tadaheva cīvaraṃ katvā attharituṃ sakkotīti vutte, aparena so sakkoti vā saṅgho mahātherassa saṅgahaṃ karissatītivā vattabbo. Puna purimena so mahāthero aṭṭhahi aṅgehi samannāgatoti vutte itarena āma samannāgatoti vattabbo. Tato sādhu suṭṭhu tassa dātabbanti vutte b‍yattena bhikkhunā paṭibalena saṅgho ñāpetabbo.”

The meaning is as follows:-

When the Kathina cloth has been received and the Community of monks has assembled within the sīmā, one bhikkhu should say: ‘Venerable Sirs, this Kathina cloth has arisen for the Community. May the Community give this Kathina cloth to such-and-such a bhikkhu for the purpose of spreading the Kathina.’ When this is said, another bhikkhu should say: ‘To the bhikkhu who has a worn-out robe.’ Again, when the first bhikkhu says: ‘There are many who have worn-out robes,’ or ‘There is no bhikkhu here who has a worn-out robe,’ the other bhikkhu should say: ‘Then to the senior bhikkhu.’ When the first bhikkhu says: ‘Who is the senior bhikkhu in this assembly?’ the other bhikkhu should say: ‘The bhikkhu named such-and-such.’ Again, when the first bhikkhu says: ‘Is that senior bhikkhu able to make a robe and spread the Kathina on this very day?’ the other bhikkhu should say: ‘He is able to do so,’ or ‘The Community will show favor to the Mahāthera.’ Again, when the first bhikkhu says: ‘Is that Mahāthera endowed with the eight qualities?’ the other bhikkhu should say: ‘Yes, he is endowed with the eight qualities.’ Thereupon, when it is said: ‘Good, it should be given to that bhikkhu,’ a competent and capable bhikkhu should inform the Community with the motion (ñatti) and confer the Kathina cloth upon that bhikkhu.
9-6.html.nextLabel: The Person Suitable for Conferring the Kathina Cloth

9-7.html.title: The Person Suitable for Bestowing the Kathina Cloth
9-7.html.body:
Not all are suitable for bestowing the Kathina cloth. “Aṭṭhahaṃgehi samannāgato puggalo bhabbo kaṭhinaṃ attharituṃ. Katamehi aṭṭhahaṃgehi? Pubbakaraṇaṃ jānāti. Paccuddhāraṃ jānāti. Adhiṭṭhānaṃ jānāti. Atthāraṃ jānāti. Māṭikaṃ jānāti. Paḷibodhaṃ jānāti. Uddhāraṃ jānāti. Ānisaṃsaṃ jānāti. Imehi aṭṭhahaṃgehi samannāgato puggalo bhabbo kaṭhinaṃ attharituṃ”

Thus, in the Parivārapāḷi, it is declared that only a bhikkhu endowed with the eight factors—knowing the preliminary act, knowing the withdrawal, knowing the determination, knowing the spreading, knowing the matrix, knowing the impediments, knowing the removal, and knowing the advantages—is suitable for spreading the Kathina. The eight points mentioned for a person to be suitable for receiving a Kathina must be learned by the bhikkhus.

9-8.html.title: Preliminary Action
9-8.html.body: What is meant by knowing the preliminary action is not knowing the sentences that appear in the Monastic Procedures book about the preliminary action, but being capable of cutting and sewing these three robes, namely the saṅghāṭi, uttarāsaṅga, and antaravāsaka, after drawing threads.
9-8.html.prevLabel: The Person Suitable for Entrusting the Kathina Cloth

9-9.html.title: Revocation and Determination
9-9.html.body:
These two matters should also be especially known by the bhikkhus who spread the Kathina. The paccuddhāra intended in this discourse on the Kathina is stated in the Parivāra as: ‘Paccuddhāro tīhi dhammehi saṅgahito. Saṅghāṭiyā uttarāsaṅgena antaravāsakena’, meaning the revocation pertaining to the three robes: the outer robe, the upper robe, and the inner robe.

At present, few bhikkhus use robes determined as the three robes (ticīvarādhiṭṭhāna). Many bhikkhus, for convenience, use robes determined as accessory cloths (parikkhāracola). A bhikkhu who uses robes in this way does not have an outer robe, an upper robe, or an inner robe to perform a revocation before determining the Kathina robe. Revoking robes determined as accessory cloths is not sufficient for this purpose. In such a situation, what should a bhikkhu do to be able to spread the Kathina? This is explained in the Vinayālaṅkāra Ṭīkā as follows:

Ticīvarādhiṭṭhānassa dupparihāriyattā sabbadā dhāretuṃ asakkonto hutvā parikkhāravolavasena dhārento’pi taṃ paccuddhāritvā āsanṇe kāle ticīvarādhiṭṭhānena adhiṭṭhahanto’ pi kaṭhinaṃ attharituṃ labhati yeva.

The meaning of this is: ‘Since it is difficult to use robes determined as the three robes at all times, even a bhikkhu who uses robes determined as accessory cloths, having revoked those robes determined as accessory cloths, and having determined them according to the method of the three robes at a time close to the spreading of the Kathina, is indeed suitable to spread the Kathina.’

Therefore, if a bhikkhu who uses robes determined as accessory cloths (parikkhāracola adhiṭṭhāna) has to receive a Kathina robe, that bhikkhu should take a double-layered robe, a single-layered robe, and an undergarment from the robes he uses as accessory cloths, and say: “Imāni cīvarāni parikkhāracolāni paccuddhārāmi” (I revoke these robes determined as accessory cloths). Having revoked them, he should then determine them one by one according to the method of the three robes (ticīvarādhiṭṭhāna vidhi). Then, he should revoke one of those three robes and, in its place, determine the Kathina robe and spread the Kathina. Only by doing so are the acts of revocation and determination (paccuddhāra-adhiṭṭhāna kamma) complete.

9.html.title: The Monastic Procedure for Spreading the Kathina
